THE CAL’SE OF BUSINESS
DEPRESSION.
“The Fairbanks Manufacturing
and Trading Company has made a
full assignment of all their property
for the benefit of their creditors, to
G. A. Jeffery. It is rumored that
there are other big firms unable to
meet their obligations, and that se
rious developments may be ex
pected in the next few days. The
unexpected business depression lias
done much to bring about this state
of affairs.”— Chena Times, June 30.
The Fairbanks News makes no
concealment of the unsatisfactory
state of business, and says tons of
produce now lie idle because of
high freight rates to the creeks.
As a remedy, the paper very prop
erly urges haste in the construction
of roads.
Hut this unexpected business
depression,” which is not confined
to the Tanana country or to any
other section of Alaska, is not
wholly attributable to high freight
rates. A year ago, it is said, many
st iples could l*e lauight at Nome at
Seattle prices, because the demand
did not come up to expectations and
dealers had to stimulate consump
tion by selling at a loss. 1‘eople are
simply living on less and confining
themselves to the common uo mu
nesotliie. Prospec tors and miners
are luoking out lor rainy da\.
Two years have brought a great
change in this respect Business
concerns themselves instituted the
new order of things, which they
said would be belter (or all ton
cctned. They adopted a cash »ys
tem and allowed miners less for
tlieir gold. “This is business,”
they said.
Miners and prospectors bought
bacon and beans and flour, and
snared rabbits for a change of diet;
and “This is business” they said.
And perhaps it is well. At any
rate, the individual miner will not
experience greater difficulty than
others in adjusting himself to the
new conditions.
THE EXPOSITION.
Newspapers received lately have
had practically nothing to say
about the Lewis and Clark exposi
tion. A dispatch printed in Alaska
papers briefly stated that over
60,000 people attended on opening
day, and another raid that bad
characters of all kinds were flock
ing into Portland; but that was all.
Outside papers as late as June 4
have no word of the fair.
Wbat is the matter If the ex
position is not worth mentioning in
the newspapers it is not worth any
thing.
Dawson has decided to send no
exhibit and the Fairbanks exhibit
will contain no gold, and little of
anything else, if the tone of the
News of that camp is a fair indica
tion. Again, what is the matter?
Did all the enthusiasm go out with
Mr. Chealander?
The truth of the business is that
something is lacking in some vital
part, or northern exhibitors would
have a guarantee for the safety
of gold without having to pay an
outrageous insurance, amounting
to 5 per cent.
A strong reminder that all peo
ple do not think alike is presented
by a late number of the Cbeua
Times, with a long article attempt
ing to prove that forest fires are
beneficial to the country. The
pap>er says much smoke is the
principal part about them, and if
that smoke brings al«>ut a shower
enabling steamboats to reach their
destination, we sav that such a
blessing would be more than an
ample compensation tor the damage
•lone by the fire." Heretofore it
has remained an unchallenged prop
osition that the forests amt the
nu«we* play an important part in
keeping up an abundant ami ttiii
fotm rtow of water in the streams
by holding in check the melted
•now and summer rain ami causing
them to gradually drain into the
channels instead of pouring in in
fktfml and then leaving the

for want of supply, as might be the
case if the land was reduced to a
more or less barren state. It is
doubtful if the Times’ conclusions
regarding this important matter
will be generally accepted as true.
In 1896 the whole world’s gold
output, estimated by the United
States mint, was $202,251,000. In
1903 it reached $325,527,000. Es
timates for last year place the yield
at between $350,000,000 and $360,
000,000. This increase has oc
curred notwithstanding the output
from the Transvaal mines has not
even yet regained the volume
reached just before the outbreak of
war in 1899. It is largely the pro
duction of the United States that
has made good the difference. In
1896 the gold production of the.
United States slightly exceeded
$53,000,000. In 1903 the figure
had risen to $73,591,000, and last
year the country turned out no less
than $84,551,000.

A mineral claim does not take
prior right over a homestead, if the
homestead entry was filed first and
in good faith.
Xo title can be given to gravel j
bars in navigable streams.
Those who file for mineral upon
a homestead will be dealt with as

These were important points set
tled in an important written decis
ion handed down by Judge Wicker
sham June 9, according to the Fair
banks News, in the case of C. H.
Heine vs C.eorge Rotb.
W hen once a homestead entry
is allowed,” said the court, "no
other claim of any kind could be
permitted to attach to the tract un
til, after notice and hearing, the
laudstead entry is canceled by the
land office.”

\1. R. DOWNING.
May io, 1905, defendants located
the same ground. On the same
day plaintiffs began to sink. The
day following defendants began 10
work. Dn May 16, before either
party had struck bedrock, plaintiffs
brought suit, basing their rights
upon their location of June 19,
1904.
The court said, in part: “Plaint
iffs do not show why they did not
make a discovery during the eleven
months preceding the location of
the defendants. A discovery is
necessary to the validity of a placer
claim. If the act of discovery fol
lows the acts of staking and record
ing, the validity and life of the
claim begins only with the discov
ery. In the case at bar there is yet
no valid mining location by virtue
of the acts j>erformed by cither
plaintiffs or defendants. Plaintiffs
appeal to the court to hold defend
ants' hands while they sink their
discovery shatt. Tbit appeal comes
with little force ftotn a locator who
has had eleven months in which to
comply with the law The appli
cation for an injunction is denied."
The congressional party of tj,
supposed to lie en route to the in
terior, turned hack at Bennett a
lew days ago. Their future intett
lions are unknown.

Billings points with pride to its banking insti
tutions and to the distinction of having the largest
line of bank deposits of any town of its size in the
United States. The combined deposits of the six
banks of Billings being, in round numbers, $2,000,000.
One feature which is also worthy of attention in
connection with the banking interests of this city
is the fact that during the panic of 1893 when the
whole country was suffering from a loss of confi
dence, resulting in financial stringency and business
depression, when the whole Northwest verged peril
ously near to disaster, when banks in neighboring
towns were closing their doors, and when there were
few banks left in eastern Montana to tell the tale,
the Billings banks withstood the storm and did not
record a single bank failure.
A most important factor in the growth and de
velopment of Billings, Yellowstone county and east
ern Montana has been the remarkable, rapid growth
of its banking facilities since the depression of 1893.
No other business and no other industry is in closer
touch with the development, growth and welfare of
a community than its banks. To them are entrusted
the fruits of labor, the accumulations of industry
and thrift, and their deposits largely reflect the pros
perity of the people and the resources of the country.
Banking in Billings, in the main, has been a
natural growth, relying for its maintenance upon
capital produced in this favored section of the state,
gathering together the surplus money of the country
and each depositor affording comparatively little but
collectively producing a large sum. The banks in
turn respond to the demands of their customers, the
security of the loans being based largely upon the
stable foundation stones of agriculture and live stock
interests.
The six banks of the city are: The First Na
tional bank, the Yellowstone National bank, the Bil
lings State bank, Yegen Bros. Savings bank, People's
Savings bank and Austin North bank.
The First National bank is the oldest and in
capital, surplus and volume of business is the largest.
It was organized December 1, 1883, with a capital
of $50,000, being successor to the private bank of
Stebbins, Post Mund, this being the first bank estab
lished in Billings. It was organized in 1882, and on
June 1 of that year its first statement showed a cap
ital stock of $20,000 and deposits of $5,779.14.
The First National bank now has a capital stock
of $150,000, surplus $30,000, and deposits $1,250,000.
The Yellowstone National bank was chartered
July 7, 1891, with a capital stock of $50,000, being the
outgrowth of the private bank of Baily Billings,
which had been established in 1887. The capital of
the Yellowstone National is now $50,000, with $40,000
surplus and $450,000 deposits.
The Yegen Bros. Savings bank, a private insti
tution, owned and organized in 1901 by the firm of
Yegen Bros., and has $125,000 individual responsi
bility and deposits of $150,000.
The Billings State bank was organized under the
laws of the state of Montana as a state bank in 1902
with $50,000 capital, all paid up, and its deposits are
now $165,000.
The Austin North bank and the People's Savings
bank are recent additions to the banking interests
of the city. Both are private institutions with re
sponsible capital, established to enter the field of
banking heretofore neglected by the other banks,
that of the accumulation of savings deposits, and for
the investment of the same in real estate securities.
The field is large and with the development of the
country the volume of savings deposits should keep
pace with commercial deposits.
A comparison of the combined deposits of the
banks for each year from 1893 to 1904 inclusive, as
shown by their published statements, is as follows:

Not included in the above figures, the deposits of
the three private banks, which do not publish state
ments, will swell the total deposits to an amount in
excess of $2,000,000.
In 1903 the Billings banks established a system
of bank clearings similar to the clearing house asso
ciations of the large cities, in order to expedite the
growing business and settlement of checks against
each other, which is simple and yet effective. Bach
bank, instead of presenting separately to the other
banks for payment such of their checks as it holds,
and in turn paying cash to all the other banks for
such of its own checks as they hold, sends them all
at a certain hour to the clearing house, and the sum
total of the checks each bank presents, compared with
the sum total of checks presented against it, gives
the balances to be settled and the transactions are
finished in a few minutes.

JAPAN'S GREAT -VICTORY.
From Leslie's Weekly: It was a
brilliant victory which Japan won in
the Straits of Korea, but it will not end
the war. When Themistoc'les and his
Greeks defeated the Persians at Sala
mis nearly 500 years before the begin
ning of the Christian Era, he, indeed,
hurled Asia back out of Europe, and
overthrew the most powerful naval
armament which the world saw until
twenty centuries later, but he did not
immediately end the Persian war. The
victories at Platea and Mycale more
than a year later, had to come before
the Persian invasion ended for Greece.
Don John of Austria in command of the
combined Spanish. Venetian and Papal
fleets, defeated the Turks at Lepanto
i n 1 3 7 1 a n u s a s i w a s e a
tlie time, savted Christendom from sub
mersion in the Moslem wave which
threatened to sweep over Europe buta
Europe was not entirely emancipated
from Turkish control. The crescent
flag, in fact, remains .'still in Europe,
though the area of its sway has been
reduced close to the vanishing point
in the past half-century.

•‘HOLLER ’NUFE”
THE BOVS' FIGHT IN
“HOME FOLKS” McV,cSJoHEATRE
‘•Homo Folks,” the new melodrama
by T. Ihizey. will be produced at
MeVicker's Theater Sunday night,
Maivh 10th, for a short run. the en
gagement extending over some four
weeks. The new play deals with life
in this great State of Illinois and its
locale is a small town on the Missis
sippi River—Red Oak. by name. The
time is 1807, just after the civil war.
and the characters are the homely and
home loving men and women who
made the wilderness “bloom like a
garden.” There are five big scenes
in the play, some of them employing
a great number of supernumeraries.
One of the quaintest is that of the
picnic on the banks of the grand old
river. The crowd arrives on board a
paddle-wheeled steamboat, and at
once starts in to enjoy life. They race,
climb greased poles, eat lunch—that
most important event of a picnic—
ami have a general good time. An
other picture of life in the early days
is that which shows the country dam e
and the apple parin’ n't Selby’s farm.
Here the young folks, dreset! in their
best, help the Selbys and have a jolly
time while they are doing it. Still an
other of the novel scenes is one in
which a number of the village boys
have stolen away from home at night
for a “swim in the old swimtnln’ hole."
Their enemies catch them out. steal
their clothes and -the boys have to go
home in barrels.
There are four love stories in ‘"Home
Folks” and there is an intense and
melodramatic plot which runs through
the play. Mr. Joseph Brooks whose
managerial direction the piny Is un
der is the manager of the big "Ben
Hur” production. He has brought to
gether a fine company and some pic
turesque and interesting scenery for
“Home Folks.”
OBITUARY
MRS. EMILY C. LACY.
Mrs. Emily Catherine Lacy, wife of
Maurice Lacy, a prominent business
man of Chicago, died last Tuesday
evening at her residence in Wheaton,
111., after a lingering illness of twelve
months’ duration. Mrs. Lacy was born
in Ramlon, Cork County, Ireland, in
1S39. Her mother was of noted Scotch
ancestry. Her father, John Fitzgerald
Smyth, taught school in Cork and was
distinguished for his literary and
scholarly attainments. Shortly after
the death of her mother, Mrs. Lacy
h ft Ireland to take up her residence in
Halifax, England, where she remained
for a short time, and then went to pe-r
side with relatives in Manchester.
After several years' residence In the
latter place, she became a convert to
Catholicity and was united in mar
riage to Mr. Lacy by the Rev. Canon
Sheehan at St. Chad’s Roman Catholic
'hurch. In 18<»2, owing to business
depression in Lancashire caused by
i lie American rebellion, Mr. and Mrs.
Lacy left for America and settled iu
Baltimore, Md. Mr. Lacy gave up a
profitable business which he had es
tablished in Baltimore to seek his for
tune in the West. He located in Chi
cago in 3870 in the hope that the
memory of the loss which he and his
wife suffered might, in a measure, be
obliterated. Mrs. Lacy was for a num
ber of years prominently identified
with St. Mary’s and St. John’s Cath
olic churches, Chicago, during the pas
j to rate of the Rev. Fathers Noonan and
i Waldron, who were greatly attracted
by her sweet, womanly dignity, Chris
tian piety and charity.
The funeral of Mrs. Lacy was held
from her late residence in Wheaton to
St. Michael’s Church, where solemn
requiem mass was celebrated by the
Rev. Win. de Laporte. Her remains
were interred in St. Michael’s Ceme
tery amid every manifestation of re- i
speet and sympathy.
CAPTAIN P. S. COAKLEY.
Captain Patrick S. Coakle.v. late of
the Clan-na-Gaol Guards, died at the
residence of his father-in-law, Mr.
Thomas Fitzgerald, 2924 South Park
avenue, on Tuesday, March 7. His dis
ease was acute pneumonia, to which
he succumbed after a few days’ ill
ness. Captain Coakley was born in
Ireland, but came to the United States
early in life and had resided in Chi
cago for several years. His wife died
three years ago and since then his
health had been gradually declining.
The funeral occurred from the house
to St. James’ Church, which was filled
with mourners, on Thursday morning,
March 9. High mass of requiem was
offered up and the funeral sermon was
preached by the Rev. Hugh McGuire,
who paid a very high tribute to the
Christian virtues which had distin
guished Captain Coakley during his
comparatively brief lifetime. The in-
terment was at Mount Olivet, and the
following were pallbearers: Thomas
Garvey, P. A. McGough, Thomas Clin
ton, John .T. Flanagan. P. Callaghan
and M. Callaghan. Among the many
friends present were James A. Hogan,
P. J. O’Brien, ,T. P. Walsh. C. 'Mo
Ready, M. F. Fanning, J. I>. Fitzger
ald, Lieutenant James O'D. Storen.
Captain J. T. Cahill, Robert Dore, Ser
geant Thomas Fitzgerald, C. Ryan, N.

RESOLUTIONS OF CONDOLENCE.
At a meeting of the Geraldine Club,
U. I. S.. held on Saturday evening,
March 11, 1905, at Landmark Hall, the
following resolutions were unanimous
ly adopted:
Whereas, It has pleased Almighty
God to remove from our midst our true
and faithful brother and member. Cap
tain Patrick S. Coakley, who departed
this life on Tuesday, March 7. 1005;
that we the members of the Geraldine
Club, deeply deplore the loss of the
truest, most patriotic and stanchest
Irishman it has ever been our pleasure
to associate with; therefore be it
Resolved, That we, the members of
j the Geraldine Club, in meeting assem
bled. tender our sincere condolence and
heartfelt sympathy to the members of
his family in this their sad hour of be
reavement; and be it further
Resolved, That a copy of these reso
lu flops be forwarded to his family at
the earliest opportunity, and also be
spread on the minutes of the meeting
and a copy be sent to The Chicago Cit
izen. the official organ of the U. I. 8.,
for publication.
ReijUiescat in pace.
JAMES P. WALSII,

FUNERAL OF Mils. CROWLEY.
The funeral of tile lute Mrs. John J.
Crowley took place from the residence
to the Nativity Church, where a high
requiem mass was chanted for the re
pose of her soul, on the morning of
Fell. 22. One hundred altar boys pre
ceded the officiating priests into (lie
sanctuary. Rev. Father Curtail, cele
brant; hr. McGee, deacon; Fr. Langun,
subdeacon, and Fr. Code, master of
ceremonies. After the mass the obit
uary sermon was preached most feel
ingly by Father Aylward, of Kanka
kee, an old acquaintance of the family,
whose words were a loving tribute to
tile many virtues of the deceased.
Present in the sanctuary were I’rs.
O'Sullivan, Doran, Jennings and Fr. I*.
Scanlan. After the last words of ab
solution were offered the largest funer
al cortege witnessed in the vicinity for
many years proceeded by carriages to
Mount Olivet Cemetery, where the re
mains were laid to rest in the family
plot. The pallbearers were six nephews
of the deceased, viz.: Michael Crow
ley, Dennis Crowley, John Crowley,
Timothy Casey, Daniel I.yneh and Cor
nelius L. Walsh.

tional UrniK bill is sun large, oui 11 fi,jng Mw -Louisville Courier-Jour-
was last year $So.OOO,niO les than live naJ
years liefore. One of the largest res-1
taurant firms reports that while it
eervinir more customers year by year, it
is selling less beer, wine and spirit
The cause of this change is an interest
ing matter of investigation.
According to board of trade officials.
it is closely connected with general busi
ness conditions. When the country
prosperous there is much drinking; when
it is unprosperous, drinking decreases.
Thus, in 1894, when the country was
suffering business depression, the con'
gumption of strong drink was small, and
with returning prosperity it steadily in
creased for five years, until 19, when
the South African war broke out and
the tide of prosperity turned. Since
then, prosperity has been waning, and
drinking has correspondingly decreased,
It is quite possible that this explana
tion contains some, truth, though
would tax credulity to believe it com
pletely accounts for the very marked
changes of the last five years.
Another explanation is put forward
by some well informed and judicious ob
servers, which we should be glad to ac
cept as applicable to America as well
as to England that is, that the growth
of other forms of entertainment has
won people away from mere drinking
places. Out-of-doors sports of various
kinds, musical and theatrical perform
ances at low prii-es ami great amuse
mcnt establishments like those which
have teen built at Coney Island, and
Lave so greatly changed the character
of that resort, have Ix-en multiplied in
recent year. Myriads of people, it is
argued, go to them and spend their time
and money there, instead of merely
standing at bar or sitting around
tables and drinking. It seems entirely
probable that such influences are potent
and are doing much for tbe promotion
of temperance.
The belief that Baron Komura's for-
getfulness, anent his credentials, which
he neglected to bring' to the first meet
ing of the pence emissaries at Ports
mouth, Wednesday, was intentional
will doubtless receive credence in many
parts of the country. While M. Witte
and his colleague, Baron Rosen, appar
ently look upon this breach of diplo
matie etiquette apathetically, or seem
willing to concede that the ostensible
oversight of the Japanese plenipotenti
ary was due to a misunderstanding, it
is, indeed, diflicult to conceive their real
interpretation, of the incident. The
Japanese, unquestionably, are pursuing
the course which has characterized their
A'erv move since the commencement of
hostilities and although many of their
act may be accepted as exigent to the
success of their aggressive operations,
this lafct, if premeditated, can hardly
be called strategic. In fact, if as hint
ed, the credentials of Baron Komur'a
were purposely left at his hotel, the
procedure was grossly improper. To re
sort to such a crude method of evading
the speedy commencement of the peace
conference, apparently to spar for time
required tot some reason we cannot
comprehend, is hardly in keeping with
the subtlety of diplomacy to which the
mikado's representatives have given to
understand they aspire.. That such a
circumstance has accompanied the open
ing of the conference is deplorable. The
embassadors of both countries are at
Portsmouth, the guests of a nation re
nowned for traightforwardness in its
dealings and especially, insofar as this
,is the first instance in which it has
lieen deemed fitting to select the United
Slates for the meeting place of pleni
potentiaries on so important a mis
sion, it is to 1m? regretted if the Japanese
envoys have been so indiscreet as to
place this stigma upon what promised to
be most amicable negotiations.

ORDERS IHAVRE
SEWERS SHUT
Chinook has agaim grown threaten
ing about Havre's sewer system, and
the county board of health-which is
composed of the three county com
missioners, with Dr. C. F. Hopkins of
Chinook as secretary last night
served notico on Mayor Newman, as
official head of the city, that it was
ORDERED that the city's sewage
"must be treated by chemical means,
septic tanks or filtered," before being
allowed to pour into the Milk River.
The order is a belated one, being
dated March 25, and the reason for
delay in the legal notification is not
known. It appears from this dis
tance that Chinook has simply sud
denly grown grouchy. Dr. Hopkins
who served the notice on Mayor New
man is mayor of Chinook.
The charge is made by the county
board of health that Havre sewage
is "befouling the watersof Milk River
and rendering them unfit for family
and domestic uses," and it is
stated that the order is issued "for
the purpose of preventing infectious
diseases."
Mayor Newman replied to the order
that he would do nothing about the
matter unitl the council hadjconsidered
it at a meeting, whieh will be called
for next Monday might. He frankly
told I)r. Hopkins that he thought the
action a bluff, simply meant for the
purpose of injuring Havre unneces
sarily. He pointed out that the state
law msde it incumbent upon corn
plainants in such cases to prove con
tamination of waters to the satisfac
tion of the district court and declared
that the case would have to go there
for adjudication.
At the council meeting the mayor
will urge the employment of an ex
pert to test the water at Chinook
and at Toledo, Yantic and all inter
vening points.
All but five per cent of the cities of
the United States run their sewerage
inrto adjacent streams and it has lo;ig
been established that water purities
itself within a short distance, p:tr
ticularly in such selil ai that along
Milk river. It is not believed that
the water at Chinook is in the least
contaminated.
The order served on Havre also ap
plies to Fort Assinniboine and the
United States authoriuies, also, will
bave to oppose it..
In this connection it may be again
pointed out that on the county board
of health Chinook has two members,
Fort Benton has one, Harlem has one
and Havre has none.

LOCAL' MENTION
Henry Reichel spent a portion of
the week in Great Falls on a business
trip.
Mr. and Mrs. John O.'Neil of St.
Paul are visiting their brother, Mar
tin O'Neil on Box Elder.
Workingmen's Union, No. 516, gave
a "pink tea" at their hall last even
ing to which a number of their
friends were invited.
F. G. Hopkins leaves to-night for
St. Paul, and will continue his
sojourn to Milwaukee in the interest
of his wholesale business. lie will be
absent a couple of weeks.
H. F. Traub, a Washington sheep
man, was this week arrested at Har
lem and paid a fine of $25 for having
brought a herd of rams into Chouteau
county without having complied with
quarantine conditions.
Beaumont oil is being used in the
Big Sandy cattle dipping operations
and is giving such satisfaction that
large numbers of horses and cattle
not originally intended for that point
are being driven there to undergo the
necessary ordeal.
John McDonough, sr., has resigned
his position as yard foreman of the
Great Northern road in Havre, and
left Wednesday for West Superior,
Wis., where he will hereafter reside.
Mr. McDonough is succeeded by Sec
tion Foreman Green of Galata.
Lewistown officials this week settled
upon their plan for allowing gambling.
The proprietor of each of the five
gaming houses in the city was hauled
into police court and fined $200. These
fines are to be levied each quarter, so
that each house will pay $800 a year.
The suit brought against the Great
Northern by members of the Shoikin
Stock association to recover damages
for delay in a cattle shipment last
October has been settled out of court.
The settlement, it is understood, in
volves the payment of $1,500 to the
cattle shippers.
Water rights suits have been insti
tilted by HI. H. Hedges and twenty
five others as plaintiffs vs. D. W.
Bateman and thirty-six other defen
dants, involving the waters of Beaver
creek. A great many of these rights
have been adjudicated, but the Malta
Enterprise says, "As the country set
tles up, the new comer wants a little
of the water that he sees flowing past
him, and this suit. is brought with
the hope of determining the standing
of every appropriator."
United States Commissioner Pyper
Monday declined to hold Charles
Roska on a charge of horsestealing.
The complaint was brought by
Baptist Champain. It was shown,
to begin with, that the horse in
question belonged to Louis Cham
paign and consequently the complain
ant was barred from action. Beside
it appeared that the horse was a stray
among many from oqer the Canadian
line and that Roska simply used it as
a temporary convenience with no
intention of appropriating it.

DIPPING ORDER IS UPBELD
Sheepmen of Havre and vicinity
have received notice of a most im
portant decision regarding dipping
rendered by Attorney General Galen,
in response to a request of State
Veterinarian M. E. Knowles. It is
held that the rules and regulations of
the national agricultural department
are supreme and that the state has
no right, under any circumstances, to
pass laws or to impose regulations in
conflict with those decided on by con
gress. Dipping all over Chouteau
cou~lty must proceed according to the
the orders of the national inspectors
and there will probably be no further
opposition to these orders on the
part of the local owners.
The same ruling, of course, applies
to cattle and horses.
The opinion holds, also, that even
though sheep coming from other
territory have been dipped under
federal inspection prior to or after
their arrival in Montana, thestate in
spector has authority to put the
sheep in quarantine until it shall
have been determined that the
dipping has been effective.
Cuetoms In Mongolia.
Tea, with an admixture of salt and
mutton grease, is the common beverage
in Mongolia. It is not recommenda
ble. Snuff taking is universal, and
the offer of the snuff bottle is the gen
eral method of greeting. Mongols ap
pear to seclude their women in some
measure, at least, from strangers, and
a traveler's arrival is usually the sig
nal for a hasty departure of the ladies
of the family for the tents of their
next neighbors.

EDITORIAL COMMENT
It looks as if peace between
Marks of Progress. Russia and Japan is almost
in sight, and the strongest
clement in promoting it is the public opinion of the
civilized world. Humanity has grown tired of the
ruthless waste of life and the prodigal expenditure of
wealth to maintain the war. The time has been, and
not so long ago, when all the world would have stood
aside without uttering a word or taking a step to
stop such a conflict. Each nation would have re
garded it as none of the other’s business. Now it is
quite otherwise. For at least France, England, and
the United States are actively bestirring themselves
to stop hostilities and bring about peace on an equit
able basis. War is a relic of barbarism. Yet in this
attitude of the world toward putting an end to this
one, we can discern a distinct mark of an advance
in civilization and morals. First, humanity is shocked
at the gigantic horror of it. And this is significant
when one considers how remote geographically and
racially these two countries and peoples are from
us. The truth is, the facilities of communication at
present are so marvelously easy that there is no such
thing as remoteness, and Japan and Russia are our
neighbors. We look in on their distress and suffer
ing almost as if they were at our doors. Hencz all
shudder at the very sorrows of the Russian peasant
and the sufferings of the poor in Japan already
brought on by the war, and shrink from the thought
of their continuing any longer. This is the humane
sentiment behind the demand for peace. But equally
significant is the combined movement on the part of
the several governments to interest themselves as
governments in the effort to separate the belligerent.-.
It is prophetic of a possible condition when a com
bination of governments may, in fact, forbid any two
nations from going to war. So we catch a vision of
a bright side, as to the future anyway, to the gloom
of these eastern horrors. The sensitive humane sen
timent that shudders at them and the international
activity to have done with them are marks of prog
ress in civilization and morals.
No End to the CTiicogoThe Chicago labor troubles
Strike. still drag out their confus
ing and disturbing length. It
seems that no terms can be found satisfactory to both
parties. The general results arc that feeling is be
coming more intense, business depression and stag
nation in certain lines are setting in, and destitution
is added to the burdens of those out of work. Who
ever is to blame, it does scent to be little short of
a public crime that petty grievances should be per
mitted to disturb the orderly on-going of the normal
currents of trade, and bring discomfort and depriva
tion to thousands who have no immediate connection
with the strike and its causes. For a small group of
employees of one house to be able, through what is
known as "sympathetic” methods, to involve in con
flict almost the entire industrial conditions of a great
city is a deplorable situation. Surely it ought to be
possible in some way to adjust upon an equitable
basis such difficulties before they go to such extreme
lengths as we are now witnessing at Chicago. Acts
of violence have been of almost daily occurrence, and
one may hear at any time of fearful rioting. Of
course the whole question is an exceedingly complex
one, and a remedy cannot be proposed off-hand. Yet
it does seem that we shall be forced to compulsory
arbitration in such matters. Larger public interests
are too precious to be put in continual danger either
by the grasping greed of the employer or the over
weening arrogance of organized labor. It should be
possible, therefore, legally to provide disinterested,
expert committees or boards for the righteous settle
ment of such matters. As conditions are now, in
considering its own rights each party, the employer
and the employed, shows a blind disregard of the
rights of the public. And there should certainly be
devised some method by which the public can be pro
tected.
Yale University is in recent
College Amity. receipt of $to,ooo front a
graduate of Harvard. The
object of the gift is the promotion of good feeling
between the two universities. The corporation has
finally decided to use the income to establish occas
ional lectures to the Yale students by professors from
Harvard. The first lecturer is to be President Eliot.
The decision is to be commended. It at once fixes
upon the high plane of intellectual touch as the
truest and surest point of friendly contact between
these two great institutions. We of the South can
not yet hope to further such closer touch between in
stitutions by means of endowed lectureships, but
meanwhile more frequent intercourse between facul
ties, a growing recognition of a common duty and
large opportunity have drawn our colleges into fuller
sympathy and cooperation. In South Carolina this
harmony between institutions is especially noticeable,
and is not a little due to the organization of several
associations between the colleges and to the influence
of the summer schools.
The alertness and initiative
In re The Equitable, of the American mind has
been finely illustrated in the
speedy and widespread movement among policyhold
ers in the Equitable. The press has been filled with
notices of meetings, organizations, injunctions and
petitions. Individual holders have resorted to the
courts and report has told of a direct appeal to the
intervention of President Roosevelt. The newspa
pers of the past few days have announced a new and
entirely unexpected turn of affairs. The irreconcil
able have been reconciled. Alexander and Hyde have
joined forces to thwart the recently formed plans of
the board of directors. These plans included the ap
pointment of a chairman with plenary powers over
all officers, boards, and departments of the company
and a call upon Vice-President Hyde to sell at an
early date his controlling share in the stock of the
corporation. The execution of these plans would vir
tually withdraw from President Alexander and Vice
President Hyde much of their power in the company.

Morbid Craving for the Report comes from Iowa
Sensational. that a body of young peoptc,
boys and girls, have named
their social club after Miss Nan Patterson. This in
cident is suggestive of two marked characteristics of
modern American life. One is the prevalent craving
for the sensational, a certain feverish interest in scan
dal, crime, the fictitious or legal uncovering of de
generacy or depravity in the life of the day. Strange
as this may seem in a people most of whom live in
the open air and in touch with the pure and invig
orating influences of country life, yet it is marked
enough to attract the attention of foreigners and to
win for us a by no means enviable reputation. A
friend who spent some time in London was struck
and humiliated to note that in the display of book
stalls the term “American fiction” was marked upon
a class of novels whose titles bespoke the trashy, the
sensational, the indecent. Whether this designation
of such fiction be true or not, its use is evidence of
a widespread belief with regard to our tastes. Nor
would a foreign reader of many of our large dailies
he led to an opposite conclusion. The readers of
these papers are daily served ad nauseam with ac
counts to the minutest details of all classes of sen
sations and crimes that mark the depths of human
misfortune and depravity. Le Sage is rivaled in the
ease with which walls vanish before peering eyes and
reveal to the curious the secrets of the home. The
very courts of justice in their efforts to save the in
nocents and protect society .are made purveyors to
prying curiosity and vicious tastes. Equally as dis
couraging and sickening is the public exploitation of
the chief actor or actors in this drama of crime.
Curious and applauding crowds greet their appear
ance on stage or platform and their notoriety is thus
converted into material gain. Though in the case of
Miss Patterson the triple trial seems to have proved
too much even for morbid vulgarity. Her company
has failed to attract and she herself has for the pres
ent at least retired to the privacy of home life. Let
us trust that there she may remain to the realization
of a better womanhood.
The other characteristic is suggested by the action
of these silly young people in a remote community.
It is the intense and widespread personal interest
awakened among the young people in such sensational
cases. While this case was on trial, the writer was
more than once struck by the interest in it, and ac
quaintance shown into its details by young men.
Even on railway trains it was often an absorbing
topic with young as well as old. This is not unnatu
ral. The young are imaginative, sensitive, sympa
thetic, and peculiarly open to the influence of the
sensational. For this reason few more pernicious in
fluences than these purveyors of scandal and sensa
tionalism can enter our homes. Unfortunately, too, j
they are widely scattered and rendered especially at- I
tratcive by profuse and catchy illustrations. South I
Carolina may well feel thankful for the purity and j
lofty spirit that mark her own press—larger dailies j
as well as the smaller weeklies.
Our Contributors.
“DISTRUST OF THE LAW.”
Mr. Editor; In the issue of June the first, the ac
complished editor of the Advocate made a deliver
ance on this subject.
He declared in the outset he did not “raise the
question as to whether or not the courts do actually
deal out justice as it should be dealt out.”
The issue not having been raised, this reflection
cannot be regarded as “taking issue” with the Ad
vocate on the subject referred to.
But, indeed, without the editor's disclaimer, the
writer of this article does not put down anything
here by way of controversy; his object is to find the
truth; and the editorial has furnished the opportunity
to hunt for it.
"The law” includes all the law; and that means
the rule of conduct set to govern the citizens, and
to govern all those set in authority to administer it.
If the law be distrusted, who distrusts it?
The editor cites three concrete instances of such
a distrust; and he cites them to warrant the general
statement he first made, thaf the "people are losing
confidence in the very citadel of the law which they
themselves set up for their protection.”
The cases may be referred to as the Darlington
case, the Lancaster case, and the Orangeburg case,
for they arose in those counties.
In the Darlington case the negro charged with
killing a white man was tried and convicted.
The editor declares that before the trial the neigh
bors rose up in hot resentment, bent on lynching.
If that be so, why did the white neighbors want
to lynch a negro for killing a white man?
One thing is certain, it is easy enough, and too
easy sometimes, to convict a negro in the courts for
a wrong to a white man.
Will that statement be challenged anywhere?
If the mob then, was not animated by a fear that
the negro would go free at the Hands of a jury of
twelve white men, by what motive was it moved?
If the motive was hatred of the negro, and an in
tolerance of wrong and insult at his hands, then the
fault is not with the law, or distrust of it.
But in the same case the editor says, “Some sort
of influence was brought to bear upon the governor,
who commuted the sentence. The result is that the
confidence of the community in the whole system of
legal machinery is shaken.
"They say that they trusted to the law, and the
law gave its verdict. But an outside power interferes
and sets it aside.
"‘Hereafter,’ they say, ‘we will take matters into
our own hands, for the machinery of the law cannot
be depended, upon to protect us.’
But that a citizen may be tried and suffer convic
tion at the hands of a jury is not the whole of the
law.
In no government of checks and balances is the
life of the citizen dependent on one arm of the law.
It is not, or it ought not to be.
The same law which set up the courts vested in
the governor of the State full power to commute the
sentence of the court which tried the culprit.
The governor is not an “outside power.”
Because a courageous governor exercises the power
granted to him, shall the community say “because we
cannot have executed a part of the law, we shall not
have the whole; we shall have no lazv hereafter?”
Has distrust of the courts brought the community
to this pass?
It was distrust of the governor, and a contempt for
the constitution which the same community helped to
make.
In the Lancaster case, a mob took a prisoner of
the law front custody of the law and shot him to
death. This prisoner was white, and he had killed
a white man.
Did the mob lynch the prisoner because they dis-
trusted the law?
The editor declares that the prisoner had before
time committed murder, and had gone unwhipped of
justice, and the mob feared he might again be turned
loose.
1 ask the question again. Did the mob kill Morri
son because they distrusted the laivt
And that leads to the inquiry, who was this mob?
What their motive was is a question of fact, and how
can it be determined when their identity is un
known
Who made up the mob? Who does make up mobs?
Men anxious enthrone the law above all ideals, or
men who despise the law?
If these men despised the law, t,hen they dpi not

THE POSTAL. DEFICIT.
(Spokesman-Review.)
One of the gravest problems before
congress at its next session will be the
$20,000,000 deficit in the postofflce
business of the government This de
partment is chargable with flve-
seveuths of tho total national snort'
age of $28,000,000 for the flscial year
ending June 30, 1005. The situation
evidently needs attention.
Henry A. Castle, formerly auditor
for the postofflce department, writing
in Inst week's Harper's Weekly, saya
the principal sources of lose are: Ex
cessive pay to railroads, abuses of the
second class' rates, the money order
system aud the rural free delivery sys
tern. This means that the carrying of
letters, first class matter, is the only
part of tho business that pays.
After showing that the railways
receive many times as much for mail
carrying than for any other class of
freight, Mr. Castle points out that
the rates paid the roads for mail mat
ter have not boen reduced since 1878,
although, aa admitted by J. J. Hill
before the senate committee in May
last, the average rate for other freight
fell from 3 1-2 cents per ton per mile
in 1802 to .857 of a cent in 1903
a Specialty
KASER CO.
WASHINGTON
Store
practically a redaction of two thirds.
A former United States senator is
quoted aa saying that the roads re
ceive $40,000,000 from the govern
ment and $4,000,000 from the express
oompanies for carrying the same
amount of freight, and that the New
York Central, for carrying mail over
its main linea between New York and
Chicago, received 8 1-2 per cent of the
value of those lines, including their
rolling stock and city terminals. The
roads got $46,000,000 last year for
mail carriage, and there ia little doubt
that the allotment, which are made
on weighinga once in four years for a
period of only thirty days, are several
milliona too large aa a result of fraud
ulent padding of the mails at those
periods. In many European countries
the roads carry the mails free in re
turn for the same government protec
tion that is freely given here.
The franking privilege is abused to
the extent of $5,000,000 or $10,000,
000, and this leak should be reduced
by stringent measures.
The rural free delivery started in
1897 with an experimental appropria
tion of $10,000. Last year it cost
$21,000,000,and for the current twelve
months the appropriation is $25,828,
000. The service pays only 22 per cent
of its cost In the main the money
is well spent
The money order system loses $2,-
500,000 a year, the loss being on the
39,000,000 orders for leas than $5.
Reform here ia needed, and it may be
provided on the post check plan of
currency "payable to order."
The rates for second class matter.
which were fixed at 1 centra pound for
legitimate newspapers and periodicals
as a measure with educational value,
have been abused by serial book pub
lications, patent medicine firms and
other improper schemes, many of
which hare been out off. But this
branch of the service still loses money
by the million, i'lne department, as
was announced the other day, may ask
congress to raise these rates. This
ia only just It costs tho govern
ment an average of 7 cents a pound
to handle mail of all classes, and
there is no reason why the publisher
should not pay more than one cent a
pound for the distribution of his pub
lications.
NORMAI, BfmOOL SITUATION.
(Weston Leader,
The time hag come when immediate
and vigorous efforts must be made by
the citizens of Weston and others in
terested in the continuance of the
State Normal School of this town to
meet the conditions made by the re
gents in their meeting in Pendleton
last Thursday, viz. That by the 28th
of July $10,000 must be placed at
their disposal. Failing in this the nor
mal school is to be considered, offici
ally closed. It ia the opinion of the
regonta that for a less amount the
present efficiency of the school could
not be maintained, and it would be
wiser to close it now than to find it
impossible to continue it after it had
been in operation for a part of the
year.
This may not necessarily follow,
however, for the faculty, appreciat
ing the irreparable loss to the school
if closing its doors be found to be the,
only possible course, stand ready to
meet any reasoualbe condition that
will enable them to carry on the
school. The people of the town and
all others interested are assured of
their co-opeiation in any plan which
will secure the coutiunanoe of the
normal.
The disappointmnet of the earnest
aud worthy student body tuatnculat
ed here, the ruined prospect of build-
ing up a large work in the "near
future, loss to the town of the pres
ence of students and faculty,.- as fao
tors in its social life,, deserted build
ings, financial losses in trade and
consequent business depression are not
matters that oau be considered with
out feelings of great apprehension, It
is evident that these conditions are
upon us unless some plan can be de
vised by means of which they can be
averted.
The question involved is not one of
local interest merely but involves the
interests of education in the state and
particularly of Eastern Oregon. All
should unite in their efforts to secure
to Cas tern Oregon the benefits of its
one state institution, and should, if
failure to maintain normal schools in
the state be the final outcome, insist
that this institution should be last to
close its doors instead of first

New Ideas in Railroading.
Much praise has 'been' given the Rus
sians for their utility to conduct in any
manner at all a great campaign at tha
end of a single track railroad live thou
sand miles long. i"or should such praise
be grudged, for in the Russian collapse
it is easy to overlook both the special
handicaps under which the Russians
have labored, and those things in which
they have made worthy achievements.
But the Japanese operation of the rail
roads seized in Xlanehuria and the mili
tary railroad and tramway built in
in Korea, up to the Valu at the open
ing of hostilities, is more remarkable
than that which Russia lias accom
plished in Siberia. The Japanese took
a poorly built railroad, captured from
the enemy after having been destroyed
as far as possible after the retreat,
changed it from wide to narrow gunge,
brought over locomotives and cars from
Japan, and put the line into stape to
carry the supplies and reinforcements
for "the army of 200,000 besieging Port
Arthur, and" the other army of 300,-
000 to 400,000 men three hundred miles
to the north. They did all this in a
few months in all the severity of mid
winter. Port Arthur's fall was due
largely to the terrific bombardment of
the eleven-inch guns brought up from
Dalny on the railroad.: Twenty-five of
these guns, weighing ten tons each with
out their carriages, were placed in the
besiegers forts under hre, and wit Inn
three weeks from the surrender they
were hammering the enemy before
Mukden, having lieen bought with
Xo'is army of 100,000 men a distance
of 330 miles on the railroad. Japanese
have been in the hnhit of coming to this
country to learn railroading. As the
Railroad Gazette remarks, American
railroad managers nnd engineers might
send some of their young men to the
Far East to learn some things about
railroads they never thought of.

Chicago, 111., April 20. The Tribune,
yesterday, says: The strong box con
taining the papers ofthe Aetna Trading
Company, stolen from the residence of
(lustav Preund, one of tho members of
the firm, last Wednesday night, has
been discovered in the house of Willis
Heron by a detective.
The trading company has figured in
the investigation of the beef industry
by the federal grand jury. Secret ser
vice men have been detailed in the case,
but when they asked that the papers bo
delivered to the government they were
informed that they had been given to
Clarence L. Colemnn, son-in-law of Mr.
Freund. The latter is out of the city.
An impounding order probably will be
asked today by the government, so the
box can be taken before the grand jury.
Heron, Who is a negro, said that he
was awakened by a sound in li!s chicken
coop early on Wednesday nionuiig. do
ing to the door he saw three men whis
pering together, tind one of them car
ried a bundle. Finally they went away,
and "Heron followed them to .a railroad
track .near by. Under a viaduct they
took a coupling pin and broke the box
open. They .found only papers, and
after looking them over hurriedly ther
scattered them over the tracks und
went away. Heron picked up the box
and papers and kept them until the
offer of a rewaid.
ALL BECAUSE OF THE BEEF TRUST
Immense Losses Inflicted on the West
by the Vast Packing Combination.
Mr. Charles K. Russell, writing of
the '-Greatest Trust in tho World" in
tlie May number of Everybody's, shows
what the Beef Trust has cost the farm
er, the local banker, and the feeder. He
savsts
"From July, 1003, to July, 1901, the
average loss on every head of cattle
fattened and sold in tho Middle West
was $8 Of course there could be but
one result: tho farmer, the real sup
porter of a largo part of the Middle
West, became straitened in his money
affairs to the general ill-fortune of his
neighborhood, and in many regions the
farm mortgage,, that blighting emblem
of wrong conditions, returned to an evil
eminence from which it had once been
banished.
"The total losses of tho cattle-feeders
in Iowa, in 1904 are computed at $12,
500,000. In 1903 and 1904 forty banks
in Iowa failed, closed their doors, or
went out of business, and at least seven
bank officers committed suicide. Even
in time of national business depression
and panic there has been no such record
as this.
"Mr. Doane Robinson, secretary of
the Historical Society, has estimated
the losses, in 1904, to South Dakota cat
tlemen at $10,000,000.
"In Iowa, $12,500,000; in South Da
kota, $10,000,000; and wherever in the
Middle West cattle were sent to market,
an average loss of eight dollars a head."

ALMOST DIED ON TRIP.
Secretary of State Hay Given Up on
First Part of Sea Voyage.
New York, April 26. According to
the officers of White Star liner Cretie,
which arrived here from Genoa and Na
ples yesterday, Secretary of State John
Hay, who sailed on the steamer on the
Eastern trip, almost died on the way
over.
One of the officers of the Cretic said:
"In spite of the stories that Secre
tary Hay had a fair passage it is a
fact that he almost died on the way
out. There was one time when he was
practically given up. After we left here
Secretary Hay was taken to his state
room and put to bed.
"Mrs. Hay went with him and nursed
him. Then, on the second day out, she
collapsed and had to be put to bed.
Our physician, Dr. Alfred Green, was
in constant attendance, but for days
it was a question whether Secretary
Hay would live or not.
"Tho day before wo reached Genoa,
Mr. Hay managed to get up for the
first time. He went to the dining room,
supported by Lord and Lady Gordon
Lenox, but was unable to remain there
for part of the meal. The sea voyage
worked wonders with him, but it was
a grave question for days whether he
would survive it or not."

PROTECTION'S ADVANTAGES
SLxperienc Hat Tanght th Country
tht Benefit of the Bepuh
lima Tariff.
It is well that the people of this
country should bear in mind that the
democratic party is opposed to a pro
tective tarHff as-a principle of govern
ment, and will do all in its power to
break it down. The proposed demo
cratic policy, says the St. Louis Globe
Democrat, is a tariff for revenue only,
and their free trade purpose is to
sweep away the whole protective sys
tem. Throwing open the gates to for
eign products necessarily means a
competition with the world in wages,
.as in other respects. A moment's re
flection is enough to show that Ameri
can wages, which are much the high
est, cannot be maintained if the mar
gin of protection is removed. Ocean
ships have now a vast carrying capaci
ty and cross the seas in half the time
formerly required. In a single day 15,
000 immigrants are sometimes landed
In "New York, and the same, ships
many thousand tons of freight. With
facilities of this kind, that may be add
ed to indefinitely, it is clear that pro
tection alone is the bulwark against an
unlimited flood of foreign goods, and
tne first to suffer would be American
wage-earners. Here Is food for sober-minded
thought when democratic bat
teries are playing on what that party
conceives to be any weak spot in pro
tective lines.' They welcome, and aie
at great pains to exaggerate, every
vague charge that can be brought
against protection and that may be
used to divide its supporters. It is a
reckless game, but it is a reckless party
that resorts to It.
In the matter of competing rates, the
development of American industries
under protection, and the high skill
and spirit that are the result of Amer
ican wages, have caused a great
growth in the exports of manufactured
articles from the United States, which
la the proof that, in general, our fac
tories turn out what is best and cheap
est. Our manufacturers are selling
abroad 5500,000,000 of their products a
year, which they could not do unless
they had reached a lead in quality and
prices. It will be time enough to say
they can be underbid when the figures
establishing the assertion are given.
Our shipping has not grown, like that
of Germany for example, for the sim
ple reason that the German policy is
to protect ships with subsidies and
preferential rates on inland railway
freights. No such aid is bestowed on
American ships, and so our merchant
marine declines,, while that of the Eu
ropean countries where ships are fos
tered by the government grows rapidly.
Foreign ships' crews are not as well
paid as tnose on American vessels. But
the American standard will never be
sacrificed unless a party different from
that now In power takes control. It is
admitted that this country, where ship
subsidies have been defeated by fili
bustering democrats In congress, can
not vie with the subsidized shipping
of Europe, but our manuafcturers own
to no disability to compete, abroad as
well as at home, along protected lines.
What have been the lessons of ex
perience in the United States in regard
to republican and democratic tariffs
and republican and democratic admin
istrations? Citizens must have short
memories who have forgotten the peri
od between. 1894 and 1897, with its
mongrel democratic tariff reform, its
millions of unemployed men and Its
long period of business depression.
Perhaps there are some who will not
learn even In the school of experience
but It cannot be believed that persons
of that caliber form a majority of the
people of this country. When tariff re
vision becomes necessary the only or
ganization that can perform the duty
safely is the party of protection, the
friends of the policy that has been test
ed and found good. Ask a democrat
If he Is for protection In any form and
he will answer in the negative, or must
now that on that subject he disagrees
with his party.- The democratic fight
fa not on inequalities that may, in
eourae of time, develop In a protective
tariff; the democratic party wars on
protection itself.
Wrong About Panama.
The democrats will be intensely dis
appointed to hear thatthe issue of sup
plies for Panama has suddenly failed
them, thanks to the intervention of
Speaker Cannon. Our friends the, en
emy took far too much for granted, a
habit that has wrecked their hopes so
often that it Is strange they continue
to fall a victim to It so readily. The
matter of supplies for i-anama, as
might have been foreseen, will be re
ferred to congress when It assembles
and the republican majority In that
body Is amply large enough for the
proper settlement of public questions
as they arise for legislative action.
Speaker Cannon's wise Judgment and
advice have frequently been of timely
service to the country. The democratic
assumption In the whole Incident was
hasty and exaggerated, and now that
the subject is closed the free-trade par
ty win bare to reconcile Itself to facts
as best It can St Louis Globe-Demo,
trst,
THE FLURRY OVER PANAMA
Mistake of Over-Anxious Frte-Trad-,
srs Regarding the Presl
dent's Policy.
When It was first announced in
Washington that supplies for the Pan
ama canal would be purchased in the
cheapest market, whether foreign or
domestic, there was no intimation
whatever that the administration in
tended to run amuck like a bargain
Monday Shopper and Btralghtway buy
all the cheap things It could abroad.
Instead, says the Chicago Record-Herald,
it was distinctly stated, that sup
plies would be brought abroad only In
case of necessity and "In the absence
of action by congress."
If any ardent partisans at ones
Jumped to the conclusion that Presi
dent Roosevelt was stabbing protec
tion to the heart that was. their affair.
And If now, when they find the for
eign purchases in the immediate fu
ture are apt to be very few, they
change their tune to denounce the
president for "backsliding under pres
sure," that Is again their affair. The
administration hardly needed to Issue
the statement that "much amusement
had been created over the attempts to
distort the facts." The distortion and
the motive of It were plain enough as
It was.
As for the manufacturers who were
so excited at the first announcement, it
is clear that they have already come
to take a saner view of the situation.
The steel rail pool is out with an ex
planation that is a sort of apology. If
Panama sales are to be regarded as do
mestic business, it announces. It can
not afford to make low rates to the
government, because then all the
American railroad companies would be
entitled to-equally low rates. But If
the, canal zone is "not to be considered
a part of the United States" then It Is
confident It can undersell any rail
makers In the world, and so get the
contracts. One of the steel companies
feels certain that Russia will soon take
40,000 tons of steel rails from it for the
Siberian road, so that the confidence
of the pool that It can get the much
smaller Panama contracts if It wants
them certainly seems Justified.
Regarded from the business stand
point, which Is the proper standpoint
to take, the president's policy is thor
oughly consistent and praiseworthy.
INDUSTRY GREATLY HELPED
American Silk Manufacturers Are
Now Supplying the Home
Market.
The home market is now well occu
pied by American manufacturers. Un
der the Dingley tariff our importation
of luxuries and other articles during
the past 15 years have been declining,
as compared with the Increased con
sumption of the country, says the Lew
Istown (Me.) Journal. For example
manufactures ready for consumption
Imported into this country in 1904
amounted to $158,000,000. an increase
of but $8,000,000 from 1890, while the
consumption of domestic manufacture,
increased $200,000,000 in the same peri
od. The class of goods required bj
American manufactures Is the only
group which has shown any marked In
crease during the past 15 years. There
is one exception and that is diamonds.
We seem to be unable to get along
without gems. The past ten years we
have Imported over $129,000,000 worth
of diamonds.
Protection has established the Amer
ican silk industry so that silk is large
ly supplied by home industry. For
example, while we are using much
more silk than in 1890. the value of the
Importation is $10,000,000 less than
then. We are buying less silk in Ger
many than we did 15 years ago, while
we are -buying more of Japan. Our
American silk manufacturers are en
gaged in supplying the home market,
Notwithstanding the Increased con
sumption of light liquors, like wines
and champagnes, we have not in
creased imports, but have been pro
ducing wines in the United'" States,
amounting to 38.5ou,000 gallons In 1904.
Wherever the action of protection is
inspected, It looks as if the present
tariff Is worthy Its unprecedented per
petuation on our statute books. If we
must use hurtful luxuries, perhaps we
shall be better off In the substitution
of light drinks for hot stuff; we cer
tainly are oetter off for not spending
wastefully In Europe.
The Tariff for a Football.
There are a good many peoole who
are hot members of the Fifty-ninth
congress who take the same view as
does Senator -Dick on the undesirability
of making a football of the tariff issue.
That there are schedules which have
been outgrown Is generally admitted,
but it should not be necessary In order
to remedy these to stir up the entire
question. This country has prospered
under the present' tariff arrangement,
and there is no Immediate danger of
the United States going to the dogs if
no change Is made In the schedules for
a year yet Nashua (N. H.) Tele
graph.

COT AN EXCUSE TO TALK.
Wanted to Say Something to the Old
Woman and the Way Was
Opened.
T T?as in a Long Island village last
romnier," said a New York lawyer, ac
cording to the Cleveland Leader, "when a
big automobile came tearing along, and
just in front of the post office it struck
and killed a big hog belonging to a vil
lager who was leaning over his gate at
the time. T
"'How fast was the machine going? I
asked of him, as he came out and looked
after the flying auto.
'Forty miles an hour, he replied.
'Then you can make the owner pay
for your hog.' .11
YeB, I suppose so, but I don t think
I shall fool with the case.' v
'Don't you think the hog u worth
going to law about, eh?'
'He was a fine hog, and I ought to
be paid for him, but me and the old
woman have been grouty at each other
for a week, and I've been aching for a
chance to open out on her. This is just
what I wanted, and 111 let the auto go
and go in and tell her some things she
won't forget for the next 30 years!
TELEPHONE AS REFORMER.
An Example of the Surprising Effect
It May Have on Our
Morals.
Congressman Bingham, at a dinner of
the Philadelphia Clover club, spoke of
modern life in the most optimistic way,
relates the Cincinnati Enquirer.
"The world is growing better," he said.
"Daily it becomes cleaner, kinder, and
more upright. Nearly everything makes
for reform. Even the telephone has a tre
mendous reforming influence.
"Let me give you an example of the
urprising effect that the telephone exer
cises over our morals.
"A certain man, at breakfast one morn
ing, said to his wife:
B 'My dear, I'll not be home to dinner
to-night, and you had better not sit up for
me, ns I shall be detained very late at the
office posting my books.'
'Very well,' the lady answered. 'Before
I go to bed I'll say good night to you over
the telephone.'
"The man had forgotten, you see, that a
telephone now connected his office and his
house."

VOL. 1. NO. 9.
Edward F. Dunne,
Mayor of Chicago.
His Early Life and
Education.
CAREER ON THE BENCH
ADVOCATE OF MUNICIPAL
OWNERSHIP.
Triumphant Election.
The Task Before Him.
(From The Public.)
Mayor Dunne’s full name is Edward
Fitzsimons Dunne.
His parents, Patrick W. Dunne and
Delia M. Dunne, came to New York
from Ireland in 1849, and were living
at Waterville, Conn., when their son
was born, October 12, 1853.
Within a year they moved to Peoria,
111.,, and here the son spent his youth
and earlier manhood.
The father became a prominent citi
zen in Peoria. He served as alderman
several years, and was for one term
a member of the Illinois legislature.
Also a prosperous business man, he
was able, when his son had graduated
from the public schools at Peoria, to
send him to the world-famous Trinity
college at Dublin to complete his edu
cation.
Doing his work at Trinity with such
marked success for three years that
he became first honor man of his class,
young Dunne expected to graduate
with distinction at the close of an
other college year. But this ambition
was balked by financial reverses suf
fered by his father at the beginning
of the long and desolating business
depression of the ’7o’s.
Obliged for that reason to return to
Peoria, the *young man served one
year in his father’s mill, meanwhile
pursuing, however, a course of reading
with reference to the legal profession.
In 1876 he regularly began the study
of the law at Chicago, and in 1877 he
was admitted to the Illinois bar.
In partnership with such distin
guished lawyers as Judge Scates,
formerly of the Supreme Court of
Illinois, and Congressman Hayes, Mr.
Dunne built up a large practice, to
which he devoted his energies for
fifteen years. He withdrew from
practice in 1892, upon being elected to
fill a vacancy on the Circuit Court
bench. Here he soon began to make
a record for judicial ability and fidel
ity, which, strengthening as it grew,
secured his re-election in 1897 and
again in 1903. His nomination for
the latter election was indorsed by the
bar association and the various good
government organizations and news
papers, and was confirmed by a popu
lar vote which fell but slightly short
only of the highest—that which was
cast at the same time for the venera
PROVIDENCE, R. 1., AUGUST 5, 1905.
EDWARD F. DUNNE, Mayor of Chicago.
ble and revered Judge Murray F.
Tuley.
Judge Dunne had meanwhile mar
ried with Elizabeth J. Kelly, of Chi
cago, at Chicago in 1881, They have
had thirteen children, of whom ten are
still living. These range in years from
17 to 2.
In the course of his thirteen years’
service on the bench, Judge Dunne de
cided many important cases, some of
them involving clashes over partisan
and class interests; but he never fell
under suspicion of bias, and only a
small percentage of his decisions
were reversed. His judicial reputation,
no less with the judiciary and at the
bar than among the people, measured
up to a high standard. Yet he always
refused conformity to judicial conven
tionalities that tend to alienate the
sympathies of judges from the com
mon life and the common interests,
and are therefore supposed to shield
them from demoralizing influences.
His insistence upon his freedom as a
citizen notwithstanding his judicial
office, did not disturb his judicial bal
ance. Throughout his career on the
bench, he was a worthy example of
the citizen-judge.
When the movement for municipal
ownership of public utilities passed
from the academic to the practical
stage in Chicago, Judge Dunne did not
hesitate to enter into it actively and
conspicuously. His interest was awak
ened by his investigations as a mem
ber of a committee of aldermen and
citizens appointed by Mayor Harrison
in 1902 to suggest plants for dealing
with the street car franchises.
Judge Dunne’s earliest contribution
to the municipal ownership movement,
after his work on the committee men
tioned above, was a speech on the sub
ject of public ownership of public util
ities in general, which he delivered be
fore the Henry George Association of
Chicago in the winter of 1903. But
his most valuable and timely service
in the matter was rendered in August,
1904. This was when he and Judge
Tuley courageously attacked and ex
posed what was known as “the tenta
tive ordinance.”
That ordinance, designed for the
gettlement of the traction question.
was ostensibly a municipal ownership
measure. It seemed to quiet litigation
by commuting all franchise claims in
one new franchise grant for a term of
thirteen years; it seemed to assure re
habilitation of the physical property,
and to enable the city to enforce good
service with universal transfers dur
ing the commuted term; and it seemed
to guarantee to the city the right to
purchase for the purpose of municipal
ownership at the end of the commuted

PRICE 3 CENTS.
What We Stand For.
The regular issue of The State for
August 5 will contain an accurate re
port of to-night’s meeting in Infantry
Hall, and Mayor Dunne’s speech in
full. The State is on sale at all news
stands every Friday.
The State is in favor of public own
ership of public utilities here and now.
Not in Chicago or Australia or Eng
land merely, but in Providence and
Pawtucket and all Rhode Island. Not
at some future time when our officials
shall have become angels and our
governments perfect—Aldrich, Perry,
Roelker and the rest having mean
while multiplied their millions—but
now.
Not public ownership in general,
but public ownership in particular.
The Providence electric light plant for
street lighting purposes in 1906, and
for commercial purposes and private
houses in 1912. There is nothing in
the law to prevent this now, but you'd
better watch the legislature and see
that no perpetual franchise trick is
tried as it was in the case of the
street railways in 1898.
But the street railway franchise ex
pires in 1912, notwithstanding the
trick. A perpetual franchise is absurd
on the face of it, and no court is go
ing to uphold it.
And The State favors public owner
ship of the street railways of Rhode
Island in 1912. If not ready to operate
them in that year we must at least
own them. Why give away to million
aires, for nothing, the chance to take
more millions.
The State favors making the gas
monopoly a publrc‘ one also,—the
Providence gas monopoly and the
Pawtucket gas monopoly. The exclu
sive franchise of the former expires
in 1912.
We hope we are sufficiently explicit.
We are not talking to make words.
We mean business. Are you with us?
Every advocate of public ownership
should read The State. It is the only
paper in Rhode Island which gives
the news and current opinion upon this
immensely important and growingly
popular subject. The article ‘“How
British Cities Manage Public Utilities”
is one of a series of twelve, written
by that keen student and able writer,
Frederick Upham Adams.
Every opponent of public ownership
should read The State. It is the only
way to find out what the other side is
saying. It is not safe to see this
question through the Providence Jour
nal’'s eyes. It is too much interested
in maintaining the gas, electric light
ing and street railway monopolies
here in Rhode Island.

Job for Construction of Dip*
ping Tanks Let to C. D.
Higgins
Four Will be Built—Dipping Fee
is Placed a Fifteen Cents
per Head
Board met in an adjourned session
on Thursday, April 20, all members
present.
After allowing a number of bills
and petitions for rebate of taxes the
board adjourned to meet at 2 p. m.
Board met at 2 o'clock.
Moved that S6000 be transferred
from the general to the poor fund.
The compensation of county cattle
inspector was fixed at $4 per day for
time actually spent.
Board adjourned to meet at 9 a. m.
Friday.
Friday forenoon another grist of
bills was disposed of and a petition of
Chas. Olsen for a change of the bound
aries of [independent school district
23. Warner township, was rejected be
cause of insufficient petition.
In the afternoon, the time having
arrived for the opening of dipping
tank construction bids, it.was found
that only two bids had been filed, as
follows:
Nelson Bros, for the concrete work
8231, and for the woodwork $313, total
for each, $544.
C. D. Higgins, total $400.
This bid includes the construction
of a tank with concrete sides and
bottom, the side walls to be 15 inches
thick at base and 10 inches thick at
the top. A portion of the tank in
which the animals are submerged to
be 8 feet deep, 19 feet long and 28
inches wide. Beyond this is to be a
slope 10 feet, and then 10 feet farther
a slight slope where the animal drips,
making the entire length of cement
39 feet.
At the entrance to the tank is a
wood cage standing above the service
of the water, into which the animal
is driven. Immediately on the
animal entering, a lever releases the
cage which sinks with it to the bot
tom of the tank, a gate opening in
front so the animal swims out and
the cage arises for another victim.
When worked to its capacity four
animals can be in at one time, from
cage to dripping slope. The tank is in
an excavation, all below ground level.
The bid also includes the construc
tion of three yards. 45 feet square
with necessary gates.
It is understood the board stands
ready to construct five of these tanks,
but the sites as yet have only been
selected for two, at Frederick and
Hecla. One will be built somewhere
in Claremont township, one south of
Houghton, and one at Warner or
west of that place.
The board by motion fixed the
price per hea3 at 15 cents. The cost
of filling one of these tanks with the
solution is close to $30, so that it is
quite necessary that the tank be run
to the limit when once filled. Four
hundred head can be dipped in one
day.
ALL BECAUSE OF BEEF TRUST
Immense Losses Inflicted by tbe
Vast Packing Combination
Mr. Charles E. Russell, writing of
the "Greatest Trust in the World"
in the May number of Everybody's
shows what the Beef Trust has cost
the farmer, the local banker, and the
feeder. He says:
"From July, 19o3, to July.. 1904, the
average loss on every head of cattle
fattened and sold in the Middle West
was $8. Of course thore could be but
one result: the farmer, the real sup
porter of a large part of the Middle
West, became straitened in his money
affairs to the general ill-fortune of his
neighborhood, and in many regions
the farm mortgage, that blighting
emblem of wrong conditions, return
ed to an evil eminence from which it
had once been banished.
"The total losses of tlie cattle-feed
ers in Iowa in 1904 are computed at
$12,500,000. In 1903 and 1904 forty
banks in Iowa failed, closed their
doors, or went out of business, and at
least seven bank officers committed
suicide. Eyen in time of national
business depression and panic there
has been no such record as this.
"Mr. Doane Robinson, secretary of
the Historical Society, has estimated
the losses, in 1904, to South Dakota
cattlemen at $10,000,000.
"In Iowa, $12,500,000 in South
Dakota, $10,000,000 and wherever in
the Middle West cattle wer6 sent to
market, an average loss of eight dol
lars a head."
Charles E. Russell goes on and
shows exactly how the Beef Trust
VJ
manipulates the market. "On
Monday, February 6. 1905 he says,
"the Chicago receipts of hogs were
light, 33.000 as against 56,000 on the
previous Saturday.
This afforded the exact situation
that best suits the Trust managers.
They can put prices as they please,
and "they put up the price of hogs
twenty-five cents a hundredweight.
As the offerings were light their pur
chases at the high figure were few.
As soon as the advanced price had
been widely telegraphed (and the cun
ning supervision of market quotation
has been reduced by the Trust to an
absolute science), a great rush of
hogs was started from all directions.
On Wednesday they arrived. Im
mediately the Trust put the price
down to fiteen cents lower than Mon
day's opening figures, and at this rate
bought immense droves, with which
they proceeded to till their cellars."
SENSATION AT FERNEY
Postmaster Mielke $1100 Short
in Accounts
A post office inspector made a trip
to Ferney last week, and after a care
ful investigation found Postmaster
Mielke short in his accounts SHOO.
His bondsmen were notified of the
state of affairs and were called upjn
to meet in Ferney this afternoon.
The bondsmen are August Pigars,
John Radke, Paul Gengerke and
Gerd Sparks.
The announcement of the shortage
comes as a surprise, as Mr. Mielke
has always been regarded as a busi
ness man of integrity. In addition
to the postoffice he has for years con
ducted a general store in Ferney, and
his friends assert that
1
he shortage
is caused by muddled bookkeeping,
rather than by any intended dishon
esty.
The bondsmen are financially able
to make good the shortage, but this
will not release Mr. Mielke in the
eyes of the law. There is a severe
penalty for such transactions, and
Uncle Sam generally insists upon
sending the transgressor over the
road in addition to exacting the
amount of shortage from the bonds
men. There are many cases on re
cord in which a very small shortage
has sent the man up for a long term
of years. In the present case the age
of the offender may induce the de
partment to look upon the offence
with leniency.
Gus Grieben isspokenof asapossible
successor to the Ferney office.—Gro
ton Herald.
Meyer-Miller
A quiet wedding took place Monday
evening at the residence of Mr. and
Mrs. Edward Moore when Fred Meyer
and Miss Maud Miller were united in
the holy bonds of matrimony. An
elegant wedding dinner was served
immediately after the ceremony, and
later in the evening the happy couple
was serenaded by the Groton band.
The groom is a former resident of
Groton, having had charge of the
jewelry department in C. B. Collins
store for a number of years. Last
winter he went to Anamoose, N. D.
to engage in business for himself and
is at present successfully conducting
a leading jewelry store in that place.
The bride is a teacher in the Gro
ton public schools and during her
stay here has made many warm
friends. She was asked to accept the
position for another year, but refused
for reasons which are now evident.
She will however, complete her con
tract for the present year, before
joining her husband at Anamoose.
Many friends unite in extending
congratulations and best wishes to
the newly wedded pair.—Groton Her
ald.

Married in Sioux Falls
This evening at 6 o'clock at the
home of the bride's father, O. M.
Parkinson, will occur the marriage
of Miss Elizabeth Parkinson, to Dr.
Walter Vincent Goodfellow, of Aber
deen. After a short visit to Minne
apolis the couple will go to Aberdeen
where they will make their home. Dr.
Goodfellow was formerly a resident
of Sioux Falls —Sioux Falls Press,
April 26.
