AJBERDEEN DEMOCRAT. FRIDAY-
Social Science Club Held the
Final Meeting of the Year
Monday Night
A Good Paper by Prof. Johnson
Was Read and New Officers
Elected
The last meeting for the season of
members of the Social .Science club
took place Monday evening and a
very
fair attendance came out, The
paper on Religion in the Public
Schools was presented by Prof. W. L.
Johnson of the Northern Normal. It
was a most clever handling of a very
delicate subject. Education without
the incubation of basic morals to
which no one could possibly object
would not be education. Father
Ilaire, although stating the strict
position of the Catholic church, still
believed some common ground could
be found upon which the instructor
in the public school could teach
morals without offense to any one.
C. M. Stevens took the legal -stand,
stating that the only warrant the
state has for taking the money of the
tax payer to spend in the education of
the children of the community is
that they may be made better citi
zens. To make better citizens the
moral side of the child's teaching
could not be neglected.
The discussion was necessarily
shortened by the fact that the an
nual election of officers and other
business had to be disposed of. Ow
ing to the certainty of President
Koehler's removal from the city a
new president had to be selected.
The unanimous choice of the club
for this place was Chas. M. Stevens.
W. H. Morgan was elected vice presi
dent and Prof. W. E. Johnson was re
elected secretary. The members of
the executive board will be announc
ed later by President Stevens.
A resolution of thanks to the retir
ing president and also to Professor
Johnson for faithful service to the
club was passed by unanimous rising
vote. Many expressions of esteem
and good will for Mr. Koehler were
expressed and much regret is felt at
his departure from the city, which
occasions an irreparable loss to the
club.

JLT this season of the year, when school bells can be heard on
every hill, it is rather sad, and deeply to be regretted, that si
lence reigns at the Jas. K. Shook School and instead of the three
or four hundred happy voices of chilhood heard there at this sea
son of the year for the past fifteen years, we hear the reveille of
the soldier and see the campus dotted with tents. Somebody is
responsible for this state of affairs. How long is it going to re
main thus? We all know something of the value of a good school
and what it means to be without one. Shook School has been a po
tent factor in making Tracy City'the model mining town of Ten
nessee and the people should rally to its support. Its doors should
bo thrown open if only two or three teachers be employed. The
great business depression caused by the closing down of the mines
should not have the effect of closing the doers of our magnificent
school buildiugand prevent our youth from acquiring a common
school education.
()UR Gruetli correspondent touches a vital point in speaking of
the neglingence of parents in sending their children to school.
We know from observation that every district has parents similar
to the one spoken of parents who have but little education them
selves and see no necessity for their children being educated. Such
parents will be the cause of the legislature enacting a compulsory
school law some day. Our school terms are entirely too short, and
children should have the benefit of every day.'

Up-To-Datc Farming;.
To Harrison Davis, who re
sides in the upper part of Marion
County belongs the honor of
using the first steam plow in Se
quatchie Valley. Mr. Davis has
a gang of six disc plows attached
to his thresher engine, and the
combination knocks out from
ten to fifteen acres of ground
easily in a day. The plows are
the size which three mules usual
ly work. Hense it will be seen
that .Mr. Davis makes his engine
take the place of eighteen mules.
Instantly Killed by a Live Wire.
John Jackson, of Knoxville,
was instantly killed in that city
Monday by touching a live wire.
He was walking on the street
while on his way to work and in
some mannef reached to remove
a wire that was suspended across
the pavement and this caused
the current to pass through his
body and ho died instantly
On his person was found a card
bearing his name and a paid-up
policy for a small amount on the
National Life Insurance Com
pany. The remains are being
held .there for identification.

INCENDL\RY FIRE.
John Mouzon, an industrious and
prosperous colored farmer, who lives
in the Jordan neighborhood on Mrs.
W. A. Mell waite's place, lost his
iiouse, barn and stables, all of his
household goods, corn, fodder, wagon
and fanning implements by fire about
3 a. m. Saturday. When the fire
was discovered it was too late to save
anything, Monz?n and bis family hav?
ing just time to escape from the
terning house.
He saved his mule from the stable
by hard work, but nothing else. In
sis store room he bad 700 pounds ol!
home raised bacon and a supply o':
other provisions and these were also
burned. It is suspected that the fire
was the work of incendiaries

MISLETGE INJURIOUS TO TREES.
Frequently Entirely Kills Vigorous Trees
Upon Which it Grows.
Some time ago I was asked if the
mistletoe were injurions to trees, my
?piuion was that it was; but having
nothing upon which to based the opin?
ion but my peisonal observation, I
?wrote for and secured the information
which is given herewith
S. H. Edmunds.
"Replying to your letter of February
2<>d, I beg to say that: so far as we
kuow now the effect of mistletoe grow?
ing anon various trees is decidedly
harmful. Many instances are to he
observed in the Southern States, as
so in the far West, in which mistletoe
h as completely killed trees. I regret
that we have no publication which
I can send you dealing with this sub?
ject."
Very truly yours.
Geo. B. Sudworth,
Chief Bureau of Forsetry.
COTTON BURNED.
Dr. S. C. Baker has Serious Fire at His
Farm.
From Daily Item March 2.
The barn on Dr. S. G. Baker's farm
a few miles from the city was burned
a bout 7 o'clock this morning. In a
shed adjoining the barn were stored
thirty-three bales of cotton and the
entire supply of fertilizers which he
expected to ase on bis farm this year,
fi otb the cotton and fertilizers were
destroyed.
The fire is supposed to have been
c 3us2d by a spark from one of the
laborers' pipe. A short time before
t :e fire was discovered one of the farm
hands went behind the barn to light
his pipe, and so far as known this was
tue only time that there wa* any fire
m th? vicinity of the barn during the
morning.

CHAMBER OF COMMERCE NOTES.
Someof the Things That Health Of?
ficer Reardon is Doing.
The secretary of the Chamber of
Commerce is receiving a number of
letters inquiring about Sumter from
parties who say that they have heard
ito much about Sumter that they
think of locating here. One firm wants
to open a general hardware business,
another seeks information about lo?
fting a factory for the manufacture
of car wheels, box cars and all kinds
of iron work.
Sumter has been well advertised al?
ready by the recent fall festival and
the Chamber of Commerce is keeping
up the good work. Secretary Reardon
is goingto make an effort to advertise
Sumterand Sumter county through
Commissioner of Immigration Watson
and is preparing for that work. He
thinks that Sumter county is a good
place for that Boer colony that Mr.
Watson is bringing to South Carolina
and will try to interest some of the
home-seekers in Sumter county.
The proposed Live Stock, Agricul?
tural and Poultry show is being work?
ed up through the Chamber of Com?
merce and a mass meeting will be held
soon.

TO INCREASE WATER SUPPLY.
Tbe> Sumter Water Company is at
Work Drilling the First of Six
Beep Wells.
'From the Daily Item March 9.
The Snorter Water Company com?
menced work a few days cn the first
taf the six new wells which will be
aonk at the pumping station for fcbe
-p?rpese bf increasing the water sup
i?y of the city. Special apparatus for
boring the wells has been erected und
???rt well drillers are jin charge of
tile work. The progress being
made is entirely satisfactory, the
average thus far having been about
25 feet a day.
depth of 167 feet had been reach?
ed yesterday afternoon when work
was stopped for the day. The d rill
passed through four feet of hard rock
yesterday and it is expected that more
nock will be encountered before a
satisfactory Sow of water is obtained.
This well is somewhat in the nature
of an experiment and no one can pre?
dict how deep it will be necessary to
sink it to obtain a satisfactory s up; ply
of pure water. It is not even known
*hat satisfactory water can be obtained
from a deep well. Nevertheless i;he
well will be drilled to a depth of sev?
eral hundred feet in the effort to fud
good water in abundance.
The wells from which the water
supply is drawn are not being int3r
fexred with at all and if the deep
well experiment proves a failure, more
wells will have to put down to he
same water bearing stratum that the
old wells tap. The old wells go down
?only about 35 to 40 feet and the
quality of water leaves nothing to
be desired. The new wells are needed
to increase the supply to meet the
?rapidly increasing consumption of
"water.

ADVERTISING SU3ITEK.
_ .-ti.
The Chamber of Commerce Enlists the
Co-operation of Commissioner
Watson.
Secretary Reardon of the Chamber
of Commerce, has submitted to
Commissioner E. J. Watson, of
the Department of Agriculture,
Commerce and Immigration at Colum?
bia, a copy of the pamphlet that he is
getting out in regard to Sumter. The
secretary proposes to advertise Sum?
ter and Sumter county through the
department of Agriculture and Immi?
gration as a desirable county for
home-seekers and as a splendid lo?
cation for manufacturing and' other
commercial enterprises.
He requested Mr. Watson to make
such suggestions in regard to the kind
of information to be placed in this
kind of advertising matter, and Mr.
Watson has replied as follows:
Columbia, S. C., March 7, 1905.
Mr. E. L Reardon, Secretary Sumter
Chamber of Commerce, Sumter,
S. C.
Dear Sir:,I was*really delighted yes?
terday when I received your letter of
March 4, and the copy of the pam?
phlet you are getting out in regard to
Sumter. Your pamphlet is well gotten
up, and I am sure will result in un?
told good to your town and commu?
nity. I have taken the liberty of mak?
ing some suggestions on the margins
of the proof sent me, which calls at?
tention to the class of information that
the home-seeker desires above all else.
It does very little to state in general
terms that we have the finest country
and can raise any kind of crop, but
the home-seeker wishes to know in de?
tail how many bushels to the acre on
the average can be raised of the dif?
ferent crops and such other exact in?
formation. With the addition of these
few facts, I think you will have an
ideal pamphlet and as soon as you can
print them, you might send to this
department any number of copies that
wish and we will be glad to scatter
them all over this and other countries.
I would take the liberty of suggesting
further that you try to get these pam?
phlets into my hands as early as possi?
ble, as'this is the season of the year
when we are pushing our advertise?
ments to the fullest. I am sending you
under saparte cover, a considerable*
number of samples of the literature
issued by other towns in the state by
this department from which you might
capture a few ideas. I am a great be?
liever in the future of Sumter and it
has been a matter of regret to me that
I have been unable to visit your city
since I took charge of this department.
Very truly yours,
E. J. Watson, Commissioner.
Report of Grand Jury.
First report of. the year of the Lee
county grand jury:
j To His Honor, J. C. Klugh, presid
I ing Judge, February term of Court of
General sessions:
The grand jury beg leave to make
the following brief report at this term
of court, viz:
In view of the fact that this is the
first term of court for the year, also
that the newly elected officers of the
county have so recently entered upon
their duties, we have not deemed it
necessary to make more than a brief j
examination of their offices and to I
that extent we find each of them rea- j
I sonably well kept. Also their bonds j
satisfactory for the present. Still we
would renew the request made by the
tgrand jury last year, i. e., for each of
'the bonded officers? to secure surety
'bonds. We are glad to be able to re-
-port, that the Judge of Probate, Su?
pervisor, Auditor and Superintendent
of Education have secured surety
Jbonds. We have by committee inspect?
ed the county jail and find conditions
such as-in bur opinion is a menace to
the health of inmates of jail and to
those living near same. However, we
are assured that the evil will be
speedily abated.
The r?port of the supervisor we
have, in hand for inspection by a spe?
cial committee appointed for the pur
jpose and to report at a subsequent
meeting; it being the opinion of the
grand jury that it will require more
.time to make said investigation than it
will be practical to devote to it at this
.term of court.
We', liave passed upon all bills sub?
mitted to us by the solicitor and here?
by thank your honor and the solicitor
for aid rendered us m the discharge
$>f ojnr duties. Respectfully submitted.

ste THING FOR SUMTER.
?Haa to Make This City Warehouse Center
for Cotton Growers' Association.
Prom The Daily Item, June 1.
Mr. E. D. Smith, president of
the South Carolina Cotton
Growers' Association, who was
in the city for a short while this
^norning, stated to a representa
>tive of The Daily Item that he
and the other officers of the as
\80ciation are now at work on
plans to establish 'at a few rail?
road centres in this State large
standard cotton warehouses and
compresses to handle all the cot?
ton that the Association can
control or influence, and that in
a short time he desires to spend
a day in this city and discuss
the matter with the business
men and bank officials. He ex?
pressed the opinion that Sumter
is the best place in this section
of the State for the location of
the warehouses and as there is
a compress already here he
hopes that the compress com?
pany and other business men
mil co-operate with the Cotton
Growers in this undertaking.
He is confident that if the ware?
houses are provided there will
be no trouble in doubling the
cotton receipts of this market
the first year and that eventual?
ly Sumter can be made one of
the greatest interior cotton
markets in the South.
Mr. Smith has already taken
Bp the matter with the railroads
and he has assurances that the
Association will be given favor?
able rates and this being se?
cured, the creation of a central
market here will work to the ad
TFantage of the farmers since the
"hovers would be in a position to
give them the benefit of the sav?
ing in freight. Another thing
that would work to the benefit
ef the farmers would be the
greater number of buyers that
would be attracted here by. the
increased receipts.
Mr. Smith stated that one of
the railroads had already ap?
proached him with a proposi?
tion to bring about the erection
of all the warehouses that would
be needed and a compress in an?
other town in eastern South
Carolina on condition that the
Association would exert its in
iraence to divert the cotton of
this section of the State to that
town, which, by the way, is not
now much of a cotton market,
nor as well provided with rail?
road facilities as Sumter. Mr.
Smith is altogteher in favor of
Sumter, for what appear to!
Mm to be good and sufficient j
reasons. Sumter already has
the largest receipts of cotton of
any interior market in the State,
is the geographical and trade
center of the most productive
cotton growing district,* has the
best railroad facilities for
bringing in and shipping ont
cotton, of any town in South
Carolina, and has a compress in
operation so that all that is
needed is the warehouse facil?
ities.
The business men of Sumter
shauld meet Mr. Smith more j
than half way and the compress j
company should jump at the]
proposition for if the plan suc?
ceeds the compress would have
the business brought to it that
lt now lacks.
In many ways Mr. Smith's
plan is the biggest thing that
has been proposed for Sumter
in years and the business men
of Sumter should do everything
within their power to carry ii j
through.

SUMTER COTTON MARKET.
It's the Best in the State and and Will be
Better.
A cotton buyer from another cotton
market while iu Sumter a lew days
ago remarked, that be bad tried to
buy cotton on the Sumter market; but
that "Sumter prices were fabulous"
and he could not pay tbe high prices
paid for colton in Sumter, so he with?
drew from tbe Sumter market. If
that be the case, and it must be so or
he would not have withdrawn from
this market, then what would be the
result if the Sooth Carolina Cotton
Growers' Association should decide to
locate their ware houses in Sumter.
With tbe cotton receipts of Sumter
more than doubled tberehy, and a
great many more cotton buyers station?
ed here, the competition would be
greater, and prices much hipher than
they are in Sumter ar present or ever
have been. Sumter is the place for
the farmers to store their cotton, then
to sell it. and then to buy their
goods. We bave convenient telephone
connections, splendid R. P. D., Mail
routes, convenient passenger sched?
ules, good roads. a?id the largest num?
ber of fast freight trains and passeng?
er trains of anv place in the State.
Therefore the farmer can keep in
touch with tbe cotton market and be
in close roach with the warehouses
also. Sumter is tbe place beyond
J??uot for the farmer to store hts cot?
ton.
Again Located in Sumter.

time has been practicing iu Asheville,

city, bas returned to Somter and es?

Sumter has the talent and it is

umpire will soon be heard in
this city.
The scarcity of frying chick?
ens is said to be alarming. The
grocers, who handle these birds j
in the spring for the benefit of
their customers, say that they
cannot be bought at any price.
The demand is great and there
are chickens in the country, but
the supply is short and hard?
ly enough for home consump?
tion. But few are brought to
the city and those are bought in
a hurry.
If Sumter can only secure the
railroad schedule which the
Chamber of Commerce is mak?
ing an earnest effort to do it will
be of benefit to those of our
merchants who are members of
that body-also to those who
are not members.
The Chamber of Commerce is
accomplishing much good for:
Sumter. Any assistance you
might lend will be appreciated
by the members of this body.

G t TY COUNCIL MEETING.
Tax Exemption Granted Sumter Machi?
nery Co.- Routine Matters.
The City Council held a regular
meeting at 8 o'clock Wednesday night.
Present, Mayor Dick, Aldermen Bar?
att, Haynsworth, Hood, Hurst, Row?
land, Stubbs and Wilder. Absent,
Alderman Finn.
Messrs. W. B. Burns and Geo. D.
Shore, appeared on behalf of the Sum?
ter Machinery Co., to request exemp?
tion for five years from city taxes and
also for the privilege of laying a
track from the C. S. N, Mad
?cross Pierson street into their prem?
ises. On assurance that the chief
business of the company will be the
manufacture of machinery and other
things for sale outside the city the
following resolution was adopted
Resolved, that on representations
made by the Sumter Machinery Co.,
Council considers their business such
as may be exempted from taxation as
a manufacturing enterprise. And
that exemptnien be granted to them
Irom payment of city taxes and
licenses for a period of five years.
Their request in regard to laying a
railroad track across Pierson street
into their premises was also granted.
Ah ordinance entitled, "An ordi?
nance to permit the Atlantic Coast
Trine Bailway to construct and operate
a railroad track across Pierson, street
into the premises of the Sumter
Machinery Co."was read, adopted and
ordered published under a resolution
^dispensing with the second reading.
Mr. D. D. Moise requested the
privil?ge Of connectng a waste water
pipe from a steam laundry at the cor?
ner of Washington street and Oakland
Avenue with the terra cotta drain
and open ditch on Oakland avenue.
Council concluded that sewerage from
laundry would be offensive and per- j
haps dangerous to health and there?
fore refused the request
Mr. Rowland for the finance com?
mittee reported that all claims re?
ferred to then had been disposed of.
That the Clerk and Treasurer's books
SOT January had been checked up and
found correct. The committee recom?
mended that the rent for the Opera
House store be Increased in proportion
to other stores on street.
Mr. Barnett for the police commit?
tee filed report of ligh ts not burning.
Mr. Hood called attention to the
dangerous condition of the stairway
at rear of the Opera House, and leaks
in the roof. The committee of pub?
lic works was requested to have the
accessary repairs made at once. The
question of fire escapes for the Opera
Souse was discussed.
A letter was read from Supt. of
Streets, Ii. E. White asking that
Council pay him sixtseen dolllars fov
extra services during^ the Fall Festi?
val in November last. The claim was
ordered paid and a resoluton ?as
adopted as follows
Resolved, tnat hereafter tl'?y em?
ployes be charged for all lost time,
except that the various committees of
Council may, at their discretion,
grant leave of absence and vacation to
cfners as heretofore, who do not claim
payment for overtime.
A telegram was received from Ike
Monk, agent of carnival shows, asking
special terms on license for one week.
Conni 1 refomake any sed vto conces?
sion and directed a strict enforce?
ment of the Icense ordinance.
Mr. Barnett presented a letter of
Officer W. W. McKagen stating that
Iiis bicycle had been worn out in the
city service and asking that it be
repaired for. him, as it is necessary
that be- have one to accomplish the
work expected of him. The matter
was referred to the police committee
with power to act.
The Mayor reported that he had j
takes the liberty of offering a reward
of $15 for information leading to the
arrest of John Henderson, colored,
who murdered his wife on Washing?
ton street Dec 17, and that the offer
bad resulted in the capture of the j
murderer and payment of the reward
to the informer. Council endorsed the
course pursued by the Mayor.
Bills on file were referred to the
?nance committee and Council ad

FIRE AT ROCKY BLUFF.
Sestdence of Mr. J. H. Scarborough Burn?
ed Thursday Night
The res dence of Mr. Jas. H. Scar?
borough, at the Rocky Bluff Lumber
mili, about two miles north of town,
was burned Thursday night The fire
was -seen by the policemen on duty
ia this city and the blowing of the
mill whistle to arouse the hands could
be plainly heard.
Mr. Scarborough saved a portion of
bis furniture and household effects,
but as he had no insurance his loss;
was quite heavy.

le the Recorder's Court
Three negro women, Alice
Nelson, Minnie Truesdale and
Mattie Truesdale, all became
involved in a quarrel this morn?
ing which ended up in a gener
al mix-up, hair pulling anet
fighting. The shrieks and curses
of the enraged women could be
heard blocks away from the
scene of the difficulty and S?)
disturbed the neighbors that it
.became necessary to 'phone for
tbe police. When arrested and
fighting. The shrieks and curses
they all declared their inno?
cence and the two sisters laid
the blame on the Nelson woman
and vice versa. Owing to the
fact that Alice Nelson attempt?
ed to use a knife her offense
was graver than that of the
other women, and she was fined
$5. Mattie Truedale was sen?
tenced to pay a fine of $1.00 and
Minnie Truesdale, who only as?
sisted her sister in getting,put
of the clutches of Alice Nelsen,
was dismissed.
of the Week
THE DOINGS OF SOCIETY FOLK
WHAT THEY FIND TO MAKE

ed with the gentleman's prize, a silver

vin street on Thursday afternoon.

Brock delightfully entertained the

noon. The room was tastefully de?
corated with flags and the national
i colors and the dainty'score cards each

w?t?Kcberries. The guests then "cut"

coming nearest was given a basket

salad course was served during the

Cooper,. Lydia Lee, Sharlie Graham,
Kate Moses, Armida Moses, Margue?
rite Cromer, Mrs. Loring Lee, Mrs. i

IN THE MAGISTRATE'S COURT.
J. M. Phillips vs J. M. Woodley-D. A.
Minor vs. D. 6. Zeigler.
The entire day Friday was consumed
in the case of Phillips vs. Woodley, a
civil action for the recovery of balance
due ??on wages claimed by plaintiff
under contract entered into between
the two parties litigent. The defend?
ant interposed a counter claim, which
was sustained by the court, and the
verdict of the jury was for the defend1
ant.
The preliminary hearing in the
criminal prosecution of D. A Minor
vs. D. G. Zeigler for perjury was
heard before Magistrate narby this
morning at 10 o'clock.
The defendant's counsersubmittedan
affidavit to the effect that he did not
believe that be could obtain an impar
tail trail before Magistrate Harby,
and made a motion for a change of
venue. H. D Moise, Esq., attorney
fer the prosecutor opposed the motion,
'on the ground that the affidavit was
not in conformity with the statute in
such cases made and provided. The
Magistrate overruled the motion,
whereupon the defendant waived his
right to the preliminary hearing, and
the case will be tried at the next

ATTEMPTED BURGLARY.
Residence of Rev. A. J. McNeil' Broken
Into Thursday Night.
Thursday night about ll o'clock one
or more men made an attempt to break
into the residence of Rev. A. J. Mc?
Neill on East Liberty street for the
purpose of robbery.
They forced open a window, but the
noise awakened Father McNeill and
when he went to the window the men
ret eated. He closed the window and
retired, but shortly aterwards the
door was forced open, the lock heine
broken by an explosion. Father Mc?
Neill says that there were two sepa?
rate explosions, both being quite
loud and distinct and it is thought
that dynamite was used. When the
first explosion occurred Father Mc?
Neill rang tbeeiectric bell that con?
nects his residence with the convent
j next door, and the Sisters thinking
that he was ill sent at once to his as?
sistance. Their coming frigntened
the men oif. The police were notified
and an c?crt was made io capture the
housebreakers but they had made
their escape.

DEATH.
Mr. A. M. Reese, of Boykin, died
Wednesday morning, after a long ill?
ness. He bad a severe attack tof
typhoid fever in the early spring and
never entirely recovered from it. Mr.
Reese bad been railroad agent at Boy
kin's for many years and was popular
in that community.
Mr. Marion Sanders, who went to
Tucson, Arizona, on March 7 th,, died
in that city at noon Sunday. Mr. H.
M. Sanders received a telegram Sun?
day night informing him of his broth?
er's death. The funeral was h^ld in
Tucson last Monday and the body
was interred there. The deceased
was the youngest son of the late Col.
Karion Sanders and was 25 years old.
Mrs. Henry B. Richardson died at
her family home at Panola, Clarendon
county, Sunday morning after a long
illness, aged 62 years. She was the
wife of Maj. Henry B. Richardson and
the daughter of the late Gov. John L.
Manning. Her mother was a sister of
the late Governor Wade Hampton.
Her husband, four sons and two
daughters survive her.
Capt. A. E. Hutchinson, formerly
of Rock Hill died Monday at the
sun?mer home of Dr. George W. Dick,
his son-in-law, at Providence Springs.
The body will be taken to Rock Hill
this morning where the funeral
services will be held in the afternoon.
Capt. Hutcchinson was OI.Q of the
small group of progressive, energetic
and far-sighted business men who
built Rock Hill They found it an un?
important vi liage and made of it one
of the most prosperous and best
known cities of the State, a center of
manufacturing industry, of education
and of wealth. He was one of thepio
neer cotton mill builders and manag?
ers and assisted in carrying South
Carolina 'o the front as a cotton man?
ufacturing stale. By his energy and
business ability he acquired more
than a competency, but a few years
ago during the business depression
and conspuent financial stringency
which crippled the cotton mills more
than almost ruy branch of industry
he had the accumulation of a life time
swept away as he surrendered prac?
tically >;U of his property to pay the
debts of the .mills of which he was
president, and th<">se who were best
acquainted with the circumstances
say that no act of his long life was
more to his honor and credit than the
course he pursued in this financial
disaster. Though he lost his fortune
the mills that he was instrumental in
building still stand as an industrial
monument to his memory and the
work that he did to make Rock Hill
what it is today also remains to tes?
tify to his ability and public spirit.

city, and was 24 years old.

CAUGHT IX THE BELTING.
Lawson Anderson Loses His Leg as
Result of Accident at Sumter
Lumber Co.'s Mill
From the Daily Item June 14.
Lawson Anderson, colored, was seri?
ously injured todav at the mill of the
Sumter Lumber Co. He was causbt
in the belt of one of the big planing
machines and one lee was so badly
crushed that it is thought it will be
ncessary to amputate the limb to save
his life. He was taken at once to the
Baker Infirmary and everything possi?
ble is being done for him. Anderson
was an experienced mill hand and bad
been in the employ pf the Sumter
Lumber Co for a loug time; conse?
quently it is difficult to understand
how he happened to get caught in the
belt.

AS TO MAIL SERVICE.
For some time past Congressman
Lever has been using his influence to
secure a mail service between Sumter
and Lucknow, S. C. Regarding this
matter he has received the following
from the postoffice department at
Washington.
House of Representatives.
Washington, June 13, 1905.
Hon. A. F. Lever, M. C.,
Lexington, S. C.
Sir:
Referring again to correspondence
received from you by the General Su?
perintendent of Railway Mail Service
in reference to the necessiity for a
clerk to run between Sumter and
Lucknow, S. C., he too, if possible,
continue on to Florence, S. C., I
would state that it does not seem pos?
sible to arrange so as to have this
clerk run through to Florence, but
an allowance of one clerk will be
made between Sumter and Lucknow,
and an appointment of this kind-will
be made as early in the coming fiscal
year as practicable.
Very Respectfully,
W, S. Shallenberger,
Second Asst. Postmaster General.

covered, was on the roof ox \he house

OBJECTIONS TO
Sometimes people object to glasses, claiming
that once put on they cannot see without them.
This argument has no logical basis. If after
putting on glasses the wearer finds it difficult
to go without them, this simply proves that be?
fore he wore glasses he was forcing his eyes
beyond the limit of safety, a practice which, if
continued, is sure to affect the health and sight.
Just think for a moment, ii people derived no
benefit from them, or if they were more bother
to the wearer than the good they accomplish,
do you think they would always use them
There will come a time when the eyes will call
for help they would not do so unless they
really needed it. They ought then to be pro?
vided with glasses. The person who lets pride,
even excusable pride, stand in the way of this
help is doing his eyes and health injustice and
is merely paving the way tor future suffering
and discomforts,

RAPID CITY. SOUTH DAKOTA. FRIDAY, MAY 26, 1905
Wanted—A Fireman's Hall.
Something' over a year has passed since the Mayor ap
peared before th» differant fire companies of the city and askec
them for support in the following resolution:
That each fire compaav, aa«ely, the Tom Sweeney Hose
Company, the Gate City Hose Company and the Hook and
Ladder Company, pool their interests and deposit all monies on
hand for the erection of a hall for their use, the money for
same to be raised by the insurance money received each year
and amounts raised from yealry dances and rental of the hall.
In fact, the upper part of the hall was to be an excellent,
fine, large hall for dancing purposes, for public rental and pri
vate dancing parties as well as the three annual balls given
by the three companies mentioned, Library Hall being out of
commission as the owners do not care to rent it for such pur
as not that an excellent idea? A lar^e, pretty building
that would be a credit to any community and give the fire com
panies something they could call their own^a place that
would loom up as a fixture of honest endeavor io take the place
of the yearly debauch at Hot Springs.
Some of you may think the companies were to blame, but
you are wrong. To a man they come forward and hurrahed
the idea and enthusiasm was again rejuvenated in the best fire
companies in the Hills.
The Mayor was pleased and delighted. ''Here is some
thing worth whie," he said. Then he proceeded to get a cite
for the building and many were looked at. Finally one was
chosen and would you believe it?
HE WAS REFUSED IT
Rigkt kere in Rapid City there was a citizen so narrow
minded and close-fisted that he absolutely refused the city the
property at any price
"Here," argued th« Mayor,"is property you have paid taxes
on for over twenty years and yet have never realized a cent of
benefit from it, and yel you refuse to sell it ftt a profit."
Do you realize why it is that Rapid City, with its wonderful
natural advantages has been so long without smelters, rail
roads, etc?
We do.
We know we have a class of old moss-backs here who are
parambulating tombstones so far at energy or progress is
concerned. They sew not, but they want to reap all there is.
They will have to move on to some back-wood village, where
they belong. They must make room for live, intelligent people,
people who are willing to put a shoulder to the wheel of
progress.
Mr. Crouch is completing his railroad as he said he would.
He has leased the smelter, which will be in operation before
many months The Milwaukee is coming The Northwest
ern is aroused at last, and will push across from Pierre just
as sure as anything Even the Great Northern is making
eyes at us
What d#es all this mean
It means BUSINESS
It means Rapid City is going to be a city THE city of
the Black Hills, and it means Mr. Deadhead and Mr. Stumbl
ingblock will haVe to go
Now then
The Fireman's Hall matter is being agitated again. The
city needs it and the ever-ready fireboys want it. There is no
question of the value to the city of such a structure.
AND YOU, MR. READER MUST DO YOUR PART
BOOST THE IDEA
TELL EVERY MAN YOU MEET ABOUT IT
We CAN get if ".t we will pull together. And we can get
it THIS summer, not next.
Mayor Emrick will bustle the money. Leave tba££oMm
and an intelligent city council, and don't forget to
Boost
Reform in Cattle Business Needed,
The recent storm with the heavy loss of cattle brings to
mind very forcibly indeed the cruelty and lawlessness of the
methods employed by the large cattle owners. A correspon
dent of the Ft. Pierre Fairplay voices the sentiments of all
bumantariat^s as follows:
Owners of live stosk now on this range could increase
piofits, gain the good will of present settlers and do a much
needed act of-humanity by looking after and controlling stock
at large. One herder for each 950 head of stock would un
doubetdly give good returns for the salary paid year-around
men. Several could ranch together and when a "critter"
needed special care, take it into ranch. Water at all times on
this range, and sheds during rttorms, is alt there is needed to
keep and grow stock, as the grass can't be beat. As it now
is, the business surely can't pay good dividends, and it should
not while run 00 snch a "cruelty-to-animals" and lawless
plan. I do not believe these? men Know they are running a
lawless business, but if they are not, then supreme court rul
ings are wrong and a state can make laws that reverse or
"•cross themselves. Surely, witb no expense whatever for good,
year.aroand pasture, a man or company could at least pay for
au un
N
control of stock, give employment to deserving men, abide
by the state laws and not be guilty of cruelty to dumb brutes.
Suaeiitig trom water and exposure 10 storms must be intense
arid, from this treatment, loss in growth, loss in fltsh and loss
in number Is the resu't. Many cows and mares lose their
\oung for lack of sheds and care. Stock now suflers more
tl.in formerly because of settlers, who likewise are made to
suffer by this uncontrolled stock. Cattle are now run and
dogged from place to place, as no settler wants them n«ar
him, and nu one who understands the situation can blame
him: for when here today and ten miles away tomorrow, and
settler's stock ^one wuh them, perhaps never to return, it
dtservine hardship on the toiling home-builder,
have had a wild storm, and now you can't get out of
sight of dead stack. I counted eighteen head in one pile. In
P1}' judgment. 33 per cent of range stock in this vicinity per
ished for the w*nt of a little protection, and today it looks
like stoekmen were sure going out of business. It is a sin
and a shame to go out this way, and with the ill will of abu»ed
settlers, who are forced to smell the stench and from it, per
haps, suffer sickneis. An effort is now being made to induce
the United Ststes laad departmsnt to compel ill this uncon
trolled stock removed from government land, which would
make every home builder shout with joy more intense joy
than the coyote now constantly sends forth at thj sight of so
much meat everywhere.
Government Control of Railroads.
Our railroad people would be less sensative to the proposi
tion to establish government control if they would reflect that
railroads occupy the same position towards the [people as the
old highways before the coming of the iron horse. No one
thought it out of the way that the collection of tolls should te
regulated by law. Railroads, when they collect freight or
passenger fare, are simply gathering toll, like their predeces
sors.
It has been thoroughly demonstrated that this business,
so vital to the public well-fare, cannot be safely entrusted to
those who, as owners of the line are interested chiefly in mak
ing money for themselves. The power is too great to be left
to any man or set of men, and its abuse works a great public
calamity. It will be best for the railroads as well as the peo
ele that there shall be responsible control by law. All realize
the immense, the indispensible benefits of the railroads to the
country, and none would do them injustice or wanton injury.
But the}' can not be allowed to become the absolute rulers of
the people, greater than state or nation. Railroad rate regu
lation is indispensable to healthy progress and is bound to
come.

Coeducation and Its Evils
By President G. STANLEY HALL of Cltrl University
EECENT STUDY IT SEEMS ESTABLISHED
TEtAT HIGHEK EDUCATION IX THIS COUN
TRY REDUCES THE RATE BOTH OF MAR
RIAGE AND OFFSPRING,
fourths of our male and
female graduates marry, and
late and have few children.
In the family budding, boys and girls draw apart. To run with
those of the other sex is condemned by sentiment. How the higher
school interferes with these laws of nature recent studies show, in
which A LARGE PERCENTAGE OF GIRLS ACTUALLY
WISH THEY WERE BOYS. THEIR IDEALS GROW MAS
CULINE, AND WE SEEM SLOWLY TO BE DEVELOPING
BEX WITHOUT A FEMALE CHARACTER.
Boys often grow unchivalric and girls careless in conduct, man
ner and attire. THUS ROMANCE THAT HAS ALWAYS
GELDED THIS RELATION DULLS DOWN INTO COM
MONPLACE. Where the presence of good girls stimulates the
thought of wedlock before its time in young men, plans for bread
winning are involved. If he marries a classmate a year or two after
graduation, happy as those unions often are, he is often led to teach
ing or other occupations that involve a compromise with his ideals
tod perhaps change of plan or profession.
Congress and Interstate
Commerce
a
O authority can be found in the writings of the commenta
tors or in the decisions of the highest courts which justi
fies the belief that the- power given to congress by the
constitution with respect to the regulation of interstate
commerce was intended to authorize congress TO LEGISLATE
WITH RESPECT TO EVERY POSSIBLE SUBJECT OF
COMMERCE beween the different states.
I know it is said that Chief Justice Marshall in the leading case
of Gibbons against Ogden decided in substance that congress had
INDEFINITE powers over interstate commerce. This, however,
an examination of the decision will show, is an erroneous view. There
is no doubt that congress has power to regulate INTERSTATE com
merce—that is to say, traffic and intercourse between the different
states—but the supreme court has over and over again held that this
power does not deprive the states of their right to legislate concerning
the ownership of property OF EVERY CHARACTER and to
ereate corporations and give them the necessary powers to carry on
their business.

“COTTON WILL 60 BEGGING
IF WE DUPLICATE CROP”
President Jordan Says Mills Cannot Handle 13,000,000
Bale Crop This Year. Panic Will Follow An
other Immense Cotton Crop in 1905.
“‘Cotton will go begging on the
streets at 4 cents a pound if we dupli
gate the 1904 crop in 1905.
**Such a condition as that, at this
present time, would mean a business
panic in the south.
“‘Farmers and business men must
learn that, and they must reduce the
cotton crop for 1905. More than a
nine or ten million bale crop will do
irreperable damage to the south.”
In detailing the trip to the west and
telling of the conditions in the va
rious cotton growing states, Presi
dent Harvie Jordan of the Southern
Cotton Association made the state
ment to the Atlanta Journal that if
the 13,000,000 bale crop were duplicat
ed, the south would suffer terribly from
business depression, if not panic.
“I have been all over the south and
1 am familiar jwith the conditions of
which I speak,”’ he continued. ‘‘The
situation is simply this:
“Kvery cotton mill is running full
time, making up cotton goods. The
cotton c¢rop this vear has been 13,000,-
000 bales. Two million of these bales
have been retired from the market and
are being held for higher prices. So
we have to figure on 11,000,000 bales.
‘‘Now, with these 11,000,000 bales,
there is more cotton in the world than
there are spindles to make it up and

looms to weave it. Realize that, and
then tell me the southern farmers will
raise such another crop.
““Think of that, we raise 13,000,000
bales, and yet 11,000,000 bales drug
the markets to such an extent that the
mills cannot possibly handle them,
not having the mechanical facilities
for weaving that many bales of cot
ton.
“The price of cotton must depend on
the supply. and if we allow the supply
to again exceed the demand as it has
done this vear the results will be dis
astrous to the south. The farmers
will not be the only people injured.
Every line of trade suffers when cot
ton, up to this time the chief commod
ity of the south, is being sold below
the cost of production.
““There is but one thing the south
ern farmer can do to prevent this de
pression, and that is to cut his cotton
crop, diversify his products and force
the mill men and speculators to give
him living prices for his raw mate
rial.
““The fertilizer people say their
sales are almost as good as they were
last year. That may be true, possi
bly is, but from all the information
that has come to hand and from per
sonal observation I believe the farm
ers are using more fertilizers for tiil
ing corn lands and less for cotton
lands. We have advised them to fer
tilize corn lands and raise all the
corn they can, and let all this cotton
alone.
i “Of course the south must raise
cotton, but nine million bales will be
an ample sufficiency for 1905, and
more than ten million will be ruin
ous. But I have faith enough in the
southern farmer to believe he will
realize that the more cotton he raises
the more harm he does himself, and
he will cut his cotton erop.’’

I ENLIGHTMENT EVERYWHERE.
:Appearanvo Not a Good Forecast
t of Mental Capacity.
Pleasant Porter, governor of the
Creek Indians, pointed one day to a
brave in paint and eagle feathers, re
lates the Kansas City Journal.
‘‘He doesn’t look enlightened, does
he?’’ said Governor Porter. ‘‘Yet he
can read Virgil and Horace in the
Latin and Homer and Theocritus in
the Greek, and once he wrote a son
net that a leading New York maga
zine printed and paid $4 for.”’
‘‘Enlightment,’’ said Governor Por
ter, smiling, ‘‘is everywhere. One
day 1 visited an apple farm. The
farmer and his sons were filling bar
rels with apples.. They put the big
apples in the bottom and the little
ones on top. I thought that this was
a place enlightment had not struck,
and said:
““‘My good friends, don’t you know
it is unwise and unusual to put the
big apples in the bottom and the lit
tle ones on top?
‘Yes,’ said the farmer, ‘but these
city chaps are getting so smart nowa
days that they open all the apple bar
rels from the bottom, so as to see
whether us farmers is tryin’ to cheat
‘'smornogt
FIRE I QUITMAN COUNTY.
Rev. 1. Wilkerson Loses His Res
iderite and Its Contents.
Saturday evening at about 3 o’clock
the residence of Rev. I. .C. Wilkerson
and Mrs. C. Madison, three miles
from Georgetown, together with very
nearly all of the contents, was de
stroyed by fire. The fire was discov
ered in the roof, under good headway.
Mr. Wilkerson was in Cuthbert at the
time and the boys off in the field, and
before help could be had the fire was
'undex' such headway that very little of
the household goods could be saved.
The house was a large two-story build
ing, and the fire was so great that
even many things that were thrown
out were burned. This falls very
heavily on these people, as they had
no insurance whatever and are thrown
out of doors with a large family. The
loss is probably between $BOO and
#l.ooo.—Eufaula Times.

longest train through here this morn
ing that has ever entered our city.
It was a freight train and consisted of
two large engines, that is, it was a
double header. They pulled the coal
car, fifty-three freight cars and a ca
boose, and we guess that is pulling
some. The eleven o’clock passenger
train had to run gupon a side track
and wait for the long train to pass on
the main line, for there was no side
track long enough to hold it.—Eufaula
Times.

Has Nothing to Say in Answer to

Hon J. Pope Brown, chairman of
the railroad commission and prospeo
tive candidate for governor, has given
out a statement explanatory of his
recent interview published in Savan
'nah and telegraphed over the state in
'Which he declared in favor of the in

The interview in question was pub
lished in Savannah on March 23, when
the railroad commission was on a
visit to that city. It was generally
commerted upon by the state press,‘
and The Dawson News printed an
interview from Chairman M. J. Yeo
mans of the state democratic executive
committee showing that the effect of
introducing the South Carolina ;pri
mary plan into Georgia would be to
rob of their political power all of the
smaller counties of the state, and put
the party power entirely in the hands
of the larger counties.
In his statement given out to the
Atlanta Constitution while in that
city Mr. Brown does not go so far asl
to say that he does not favor the
South Caroclina plan for Georgia, but
says simply that he is not familiar
with the details of that plan and that
he had in view that in the event of a
deadlock in the convention it would
be hetter to arrange to have the two
leading candidates run the race over
than to have some one nominated
who had not been voted for in the
primary.
When asked about Chairman Yeo
man’s interview, Mr. Brown wrote the
following, which is given just as
furnished by him, as follows:
“I am not familiar with the details
of the South Carolina plan, and have
given the matter but little thought.

I will state just what I have said
about the matter, and the circum
starces under which it was said.
“‘Last week while in Savannah it
was'suggested that from present indi
cations there would be several entries
in the gubernatorial race. It was also
suggested that possibly there would
be a deadlock in the convention and
that some one mighibe nominated who
had not been voted for in the primary,
whereupon I made the remark that
rather than have such a state of affairs
JUDGE GRIGGS TO FARMERS.
What the Congressman Said in Speech
Over in Randolph.
The Cuthbert Leader has the follow
ing to say of the speech Congressman
Griggs ,delivered to the farmers of
Randolph county the other day:
In the beginning Judge Griggs ex
plained why he had declined to speak
at a recent meeting of farmers in this
county. He feared that political sig
nificance might be erroneously attach
ed to the meeting. He had come in re-
Tsp()nse to a second invitation.
The speaker said that the prosperity
of the whole country is dependent to a
large extent upon the success of the
southern farmer. It is right for them
to organize. They should have more
‘confidence in each other. They should
not be discouraged if little should be
accomplished this year. No trust ever
made money the first year of its ex
istence. Reforms come slowly.
He urged the farmers to raise more
food stuffs and catile.
In conclusion he advised the farmers
to pay more attention to educating
their children and instructing them to
continue the organization just begun.
The address produced a favorable
impression, the speaker being fre
quently applauded.
“WORKING” LAURENS NEGROES.
Stranger Claims to Be a Doctor and
Foretells Sickness.
For some time the people of Laur
iens county have been disturbed over
the strange actions of a man who
Icla.ims to be a doctor, and who lives
entirely with the negroes and rarely
goes out in the day time. When he
does show himself he seems to desire
to prevent his face from being seen.
It is said that he has succeeded in get
ting a good deal of money out of
negroes by telling them that he is in a
position to secure for them govern
ment land, for which the fee is $2.50.
He also makes the negroes pay him
for prescriptions when they are not
sick and have not consulted him. He
looks at them, tells them that he is
able to foretell coming illness and
that they can avoid being sick by
taking his medicine.

ton, isolated and alone, is a de
lightful rural community among
New Hampshire's hills, sheltered
by protecting oaks and elms, deco
rated with fruitful orchards and
fertile gardens, surrounded by

homelike and cozy, witb all its

old fashioned farmhouse.
Not the large, roomy old doml
clle of Colonial days, nor the
rough unfinished and uncouth
shack of a frontier farmer; rather,
a small, real, homelike New
Hampshire farmhouse. Sixroomg
measure the size of the house,

near by.
The exact date when the old
house was bnilt is not known, but
it is a production of the colonial
period of architecture; for before
the revolution a band of counter
feiters occupied it, and here man
ufactured, until captured by the
authorities, their spurious coin.
A broad drive leads to the front
door. The exterior is black with
age, except for a few decorated
patches of recently applied clap
boards to keep out the wind. No
blinds guard the windows, and
the sunshine sparkles through the
small window panes without hin
drance. A front door and a side one are
the means of egress and entrance.
A large strip of lawn fronts the
house, and besides the efforts oi
nature, man has unknowingly aid
ed in the beautifying, by an adorn
ment in the shape of a huge pile ol
pine and birch logs, the winter's
supply of fuel. Giant shade trees
shelter the roadways leading to
the house; the blue peaks of near-
bv hills look down upon the fifty
acres of fertile farm land, where
good crops of oats, potatoes aand
Indian corn are grown; the sharp
ledges which jut from the hill
sides and the rocks which rise pro
miscuously here and there among
the corn fields convincingly prove
that the fertility of these lands
was the result of much labor and
trouble on the part of the pioneer
New England farmer. 1

700 feet a minute.

ANOTHER WHOLESALE HOUSE.
Beck Bros. Co., Will Establish
Large Wholesale Dry Goods and
Notion House.
Sumter is to have a wholesale dry
goods and notion house in the near fu?
ture, the members pf the Beck Bros.
Company having decided to go out of
the retail business in the near future
and establish a wholesale house. They
have contemplated this move for
some time btu have been unable to
secure a suitable site for their ware?
house and offices until the last few
days. They have now succeeded in
purchasing the lot on which the Epis
j copal church and rectory now stand
and as soon as they can obtain posses?
sion, which will be about August 1,
they will begin the erection of the
warehouse. They have planned to
erect a building 80x150 feet, two
stories and basement, so constructed
that if the need for more room arises
additional stories may be added.
It is expected that the buildings, will
be completed and everything in read j
iness for business early next year. It j
will be imposible to erect the build?
ings and make the other necessary ar?
rangements to begin business in time
for fall trade, but even tohugh the
building cannot be., completed by the
time they are ready to put men on the
road to solicit" orders for the spring
trade they will secure temporary
quarters and, begin business.
They expect to carry a large stock j
and will travel several men from the
start who will visit the trade in this
and adjoining states. If the business
develops as they hope they will travel j
a number of men.
The enterprise of Beck Bros. Com?
pany means a great deal for Sumter
in a business way and their success
will he the means of inducing others
to embark in the same line of business,
for the geographical situation and'the
railroad facilities of Sumter are such
as to make it certain to become a
.wholesale centre for this section of the
state. For tue time being Beck Bros.
Company will have the field to them?
selves in the wholesale dry goods and
notions line and they shoulc succeed
in building up a large and profitable
business. They will have the best
wishes of the community and they
can count on the support and hacking
of Sumter people.

W. E. Curtis, writing, to the Chica?
go Record-Herald from Birmingham
says: Southern iron is said to make
the smoothest castings of any produced
in the United States, and for many
purposes satisfactory foundry work
cannot be secured without it. No oth?
er state produces so large a quantity
of foundry iron as Alabama, and yet
very little is used here. The largest
domestic buyers of Birmingham iron,
I am told, are manufacturers of ma?
chinery and machine tools in Ohio
and the west, while the New England
manufacturers of cotton mill machin?
ery consume large quantities. Some
one who bas been looking up the sub?
ject asserts that nearly ali of the cot?
ton mill machinery nsed in the south
is manufactured in New England out
of Birmingham iron. It is especially
adapted to locomotive building, also,
and large quantities are being sent to
Philadelphia. Providence, Chicago,
and Richmond locomotive works for
that purpose. That is the reason why
the new International Power company,
which has consolidated nearly all of
the locomotive works of the United
States, has secured control of one of
the largest pig iron concerns in Bir?
mingham.

The city council met in regular ses?

and Stubbs.
-Absent-Aldermen Hurst, Pinn and

been recently reported to him in a
negro family living a short distance

rected and was informed that each

territory, and that he had consented

of this work for Sumter county.

NEGROES IEELI> SCOTCHMAN.
Scotch Immigrant Living in Lower
Richland Claims Brutal Treatment
at the Hands of Highwaymen.
F. W. McDonald, a Scotch immi?
grant, who has bought a farm in the
lower part of the county and settled
there, is not particularly pleased with
Richland on the whole. Late on
Monday night he was held up by two
negroes near his place of abode avd
beaten and robbed. He appeared] in
the city yesterday morning and went
to Magistrate McMaster to have a war?
rant sworn out against two men
whom he thinks are the parties, but
later he returned and withdrew the
warrants, preferring to have them
issued at Ea s tc ver.
McDonald says that he was on his
way home whsn the negroes met him
in the road tmd seized bim. One
held him while the other went
through his clothes. After the little
money that he carried was taken, he
says that the nergoes attacked him
with their fis tn and then ran away j
into the dark cess of some nearby
woods.
The young man has only been in the
United States about one year. He
likes the country and says he will re- j
main here. His reason for the attacks
is not wholly the motive of robbery,
but a jealousy of the negroes, for he
says that the feeling of envy against
him is something decided in character.
The negroes have the idea, be
thinks, that the immigrants, will
come here and'4run them away," as
he expressed i';.
When he left town yesterday ?he
took a shotgun with him.-The State,
March 22.

COTTON IX AFRICA.
It Turns Out That Cotton Cannot be
Successfully Raised in East Africa.
London, March 20.-The report of
the commissioners sent out by the
British government to investigate the
cotton growing- possibilities in East
Africa is published this evening in
the formof a White Paper. It says:
"Unless difficulties which at present
appear to be insuperable can be re?
moved, cotton cultivation in East
Africa will never be undertaken
on any considerable scale.* First
among the difficulties the com?
missioners place labor, on account of
the apathy of the natives and their
disinclination for work. The to^al area
devoted to cotton growing unde- Eu?
ropean supervision can scarcely ex?
ceed a fewthousand acres. The com?
missioner's opinion is that the only
solution of the difficulty is indentured
labor from India or China.

Remiiniscences hy One of His School
mates and Friends of a Stirring In?
cident iu Which He Was a
Leading: Actor. t
The death of Mr. John McQueen
brings back to the memory .of his sur?
viving schoolmates and companions
of long years ago some Interesting rec?
ollections of a more or fess stirring
nature. In his boyhood and ?arly
manhood John was of an adventurous
disposition. Manly sports and daring
exploits were more to his liking than
books and he was recognized as a
leader in those thingfc which appeal
most to boyish hearts. Nevertheless
he had a bright mind and by applica?
tion could easily have attained a good
standing as a scholar. He was of a
cheerful, jolly temperament' and a
popular fellow with his chums. In the
days when John was a schoolboy the
Radical' regime had full sway in the
state government. There was wide?
spread ill feeling and constant friction
between whites and blacks. In no way
did this spirit show itself more in?
tensely than in the open hostility be?
tween white and black school chi dren.
Rarely did parties of the opposing
colors meet that fight did not follow,
and in more than one stand up and
knock down affair John distinguished
himself as a fearless and effective
leader. Later on he became a mem?
ber of the Sumter Light Infantry
which did good service in those dark
days in the preservation of peace by
preparations to fight if need appeared.*
On all occasions John was present
and willing to do his whole duty. In
the memorial campaign of 1878 the
great Republican rally for Sumter
county was held on October the 12th.
It was a short,time after the Cainhoy
massacre and subsequent murder of
whHe men in Charleston. The white
people were aroused to the fighting
pitch. It needed but a spark to start
a conflagration, the end of which
could not have been foretold. The
great effort of General Hampton and
other leaders at that time was directed
towards preventing violence on the
part of the whites. They foresaw that
widespread disorder would result in
military rule, which would have bfcfen
disastrous to the prospect of wrestling
the state from the control "of the pi?
rate band who were playing havoc
with its institutions and prosperity.
On the day in question the streets
of Sumter were filled by thousands of
negroes. The whites had adopted the
plan of attending these metfngs and
demanding a division of time in order
that they might present their views
j and interest negroes, if possible, in the
j redemption of the state. Acocrding to
I this plan there was a force of 350 or
I 400 militia and calvalry on the ground
j to back this demand and incidentally
to protect the town from the dangers
of a negro riot. A detach?
ment of the Columbia Flying Artil?
lery with two of their guns, also
came over to lend their persuasive as?
sistance. The request of the Democrats
for a division of time was refused and
the meeting which was held below the
depot broke up in disorder. The great
multitude of -negroes surged up Main
street in a frenzied mass. An alarm
was sounded and two companies of
militia formed quickly at the court
house and stopped the negroes in
their mad march. They were enraged
and began to curse and throw rocks
at the militia, who were with difficul?
ty kept from firing on the mob by
their commanding officers and by the
entreaties of Gen. Johnson Hagood.
The negroes on finding that the mili?
tia were restrained from shooting,
became more and more abusive. Some
of them attempted to break through
the ranks. One bolder than tthe others
threw himself against the Sumter
Light Infantry just where John Mc?
Queen was in line. That was more
than John could stand, so he prompt?
ly broke ranks without waiting for or?
ders, chased that darkey through the
crowd and at last laid him out with
a well directed blow on his head from
a rifle. The effedt was magical.
No further assaults were made upon
the line. That one act perhaps did
more to check the impetuosity of the
mob than anything else. Very
soon the negroes stampeded. Sum?
ter was saved from what threat?
ened to be a bloody riot without
the loss of a life or the firing of a gun.
Many will recall that day and the feel?
ing and sentiments which were preva?
lent as indicated by the legends which
appeared upon the various banners
pf the white people. Most significant
was one upon which was blazoned in
big letters: ^'Remember Cainhoy."

AS OTHERS SEE US.
Ml
An Interesting Lecture By an Elo?
quent Scholar.
Professor H. S. Snyder, president of
Wofford college, delivered a lecture in
the First Methodist church last night,
entitled, "As Others See us." A large
audience attended and were delighted
with the pleasing manner in which
the provincialisms and peculiarities of
the American people were portrayed.
The lecture was largely on personal
observations in Europe. The speaker
brought out very clearly and with an
occasional dash o? humor the differ?
ence between the estimate which
Americans place upon themselves and
that which is accorded them by Eu?
ropeans. Foreign opinion is not cal?
culated to promote the self-satisfac
tjon we feel with ourselves and our
country. Their ignorance of our coun?
try people is remarkable almost past
comprehension. Most prominently was
madeto appear the disgust of foreign?
ers with American manners, or rath-
made to appear of foreigners the dis- I
gust with American manners, or rath?
er lack of manners, which the lec?
turer thought well deserved by reason
of the self assertiveness and piggish?
ness of many tourists in the old coun- j
try. The lecturer is none the less a J
true blue American and regards these
minor matters of what others think I
as of immeasurably less importance j
than the real worth of those things j
which have been accomplished by and
the real sentiments which have act?
uated the people of the new world..
The lecture was a rare treat and
we hope Professor Snyder may come
again. The proceeds go to the new
church building fund.
The Ladies' society, under whose
auspices the lecture was given, are to J
be congratulated en the succ?s of j
their venture.

TENNIS CLUB ORGANIZED.'
Two Courts to Be Laid off and Tour?
naments to be Arranged.
From the Daily Item June 21.
Yesterday afternoon at 7 o'clock in
the office of The Daily Item, the
Game Cock Tennis club was organ?
ized. The meeting was a very enthu?
siastic one, which was evinced b\
twelve of the club members being
present out of the limited member'
ship of sixteen.
Mr. L. C. Moise, president pro tem,
called the meeting to order and de?
clared the chair ready to entertain
nominations for officers. The follow?
ing were elected*
President-Hugh -C. Haynsworth.
Vice President-3. J. Cunning?
ham.
Secretary and Treasurer-L. M.
Green.
Manager-Geo. D. Levy.
Two courts will be put into play?
ing condition, one in the lot adjoin?
ing the residence of the Mrs. Rose
Ryttenberg and the other court will
be located in the pretty lawn of the
Misses Haynsworth.
Wednesday afternoon of each week
has been set aside for ladies' day, at
which time the members of the club
are privileged to bring their young
lady friends to the courts' for sets of
mixe<1 doubles and mixed singles.
The subject of mixed tournaments
was discussed, and it was the sense
of the club that the manager should
arrange a series of contests with
teams from neighboring towns.
Below is given the club roll:
H. C. Haynsworth, H. P. Moses, H.

?Levy, J. H. Levy, Jr., E. J. Cunning?
ham, L. Moise, W. B. Levy, W. W.
Bewley, J. D. Graham, L. M. Green,
G. E. Haynswprth, A. A. Manning,
Harry Spann and Ray Ryttenberg.
Fire Loss.
Between January 1st and June 1st
the Sumter fire department hes re?
sponded to 38 alarms, 5 of which
Iwere false alarms. The value of the
property endangered was $178,100.00.
It was protected by insurance policies;
?amounting to$91,S50.00. The actual
loss by fire during the period was
$24,117.75. The greatest part of this
loss^ resulted from the Minor Store
fire. But for the efficiency of the fire
department the loss would have been
much greater.

Coroner Flowers received a mes?
sage this morning from Mr. W. O.
j Cain, of Privateer, asking permission
I to bury the body of a negro woman,
J
j who was, found dead on his place yes?
terday. After ascertaining all the
facts in the case Mr. Flowers decided

granted the burial permit.

WHY NOT CAMP HERE?
Sumter Offers Splendid Inducements
to Soldiers.
The militia of South Carolina will
T^O into camp this summer, an appro?
priation having been made by the
United States government to defray
She expenses of the encampment and
'to pay the officers and privates while
?en duty.
As yet the place of encampment has
not been selected and it will be sev?
eral ?days before a* decision will be
xeached. Sumter offers as many in?
ducements as any other" town in the
"State and the encampment, it is ar?
gued by military men, should -be held
in this city. Encampments have been
iield in Anderson, Columbia, Green?
ville, Camden and Charleston and
Sumter should be given an opportuni?
ty to entertain the citizen soldiers of
South Carolina.
Sumter is well located for the next j
encampment. The climat? is delight?
ful, the nights being cool and there
?re no mosquitoes to worry the sol?
diers. Then besides there are several
??tes in close proximity to the city
that could be converted into a splen
?id camp ground. There is an abund?
ance of good fresh water and the
markets furnish good things to eat
that soldiers are so fond of.
Columbia is making a strong pull
?for the encampment, hut as yet no
-decision has been reached, and if the
people of Sumter want in^ military
encampment the right sort of effort
"should he made to capture it.. Sumte?.
should send in an invitation at once
and a delegation consisting of military
.men and business men should appear
before the proper military authorities
and set forth the advantages of Sum?
ter as the place for the military en?
campment
EARLY MORNING FIRE.
Ivesioenee of Mr. W. D. Colclough
Burned to the Ground.
Prom The Daily Item, June 7.
The residence of Mr. W. D. Col
dough, on Salem avenue, was burned
this morning about 3 o'clock and the
house and contents were a total loss.
The fire is supposed to have originated
in the kitchen, but when it was dis?
covered the whole rear portion of the
house was in flames. When the alarm
vas given the hose wagons made a
^.prompt response, but little could be
done save to prevent the fire from
spreading to the adjacent houses.
The loss is estimated at $2,000 with
5750 insurance on the house and $500
*?n the furniture.
The house was owned by the estate
-<wf H. Harby.
Prom The Daily Item, June 7.
The third fire within twenty-four
aours occurred today between 1 and
2 o'clock at Mr. F. L. Adams' home
on South Washington street. The fire
?was started by a spark falling on the
roof, but the blaze was discovered be
iore much damage was done and the
alarm brought the firemen promptly
to the scene. The house is owned by
the estate of H. Harby.
Mr. Julian Gayden, who for some
time past has been clerking at Harris'
?cigar store, has accepted a position
as night clerk at the Hotel Sumter.
The Ministerlial Union does not ap?
prove of Sunday automobile trips, and
the resolution which was adopted on
Monday, puts that body squarely on
record in opposition of Sunday p'leas
are jaunts.
The third fire came on the heels
the other two as usual.
Sewerage for Sumter is a necessity.
Those who are most earnestly advo?
cating it do not do so because they
think it one of the trimmings that a
-growing and progressive town should
3iave but because they realize that the
public health demands it.
The City Board of Education will
2aa.ve to provide another school build?
ing within the next three years and
the time to buy a lot is not when you
must have it at once.
The fish traps in the streams of
-Sumter county have about done their
-work and fresh fish are so scarce that
they are seldom offered for sale on the
streets.
There is some probability of the
Presbyterian College being removed
?xom Clinton and as there is no col?
lege in this section of the State this
?Hy would he an advantageous loca
tl?tm for the college.
The boy that is not the member of
two or three Sunday Schools at pres?
ent is not regarded as very bright by
his companions. This is the picnic
season. j
The dust on the streets is already a
nuisance and grows worse daily.
Unless Sumter provides cotton ware?
houses for the storage of cotton the
market will be handicapped in com?
peting with other markets that have
these facilities. The cotton business
is in a state of transition and in the
future the warehouse is destined to
play an important part in it. The day
of selling cotton at any price the buy?
ers see fit to offer is past.

WH?PPEDHIS WIFE
And is Now in Jail Charged With Murder
From The Daily Item. June 10.
On last Sunday Heyward
Smith, colored, who lives on the
plantation of Mr. Luther
Thompson, near Durwood, Shi?
loh township, administered to
his wife a severe whipping. The
neighbors did not report the
affair until Tuesday, when the
woman died. The authorities
were then notified and Magis?
trate H. H. Player, acting cor?
oner, had the body examined by
Dr. Tarrent, who found several
bruises on the head sufficient to
cause death.
jury was empanelled and
upon the report of Dr. Tarrent,
found that the woman came to
her death from the effects of the
whipping she received on last
Sunda;-.
Smith was immediately ar?
rested by Deputy J. W. Hines
and brought to Sumter and
placed in jail to await trial on
the charge of murder at the
next term of court.
There are all Kinds of Fools.
About 5 o'clock last Sunday after?
noon J. B. Prevatt, lineman for the
Manning Telephone Company, created
somewhat of a sensation by putting
the contents of a laudanum vial into
his stomach, and then went oft! and
fell into a deep sleep. Marion Smith,
colored, saw Prevatt drink the stuff,
and gave the alarm. After sometime,
the services of Drs. Geiger and Dick?
son were secured. They made a con?
nection with the victim's stomach by
means of a stomach pump, which
brought up a lot of food, but no laud?
anum. It was then discovered that Pre?
vatt had faked, what for, can only be
found in the man's peculiar manner
of making himself notorious. What
gave the matter the appearance of
reality was a letter he wrote which
was very despondent, attributing his
desire to call up "central" of the other
world, being tired of this life, and do?
mestic trouble. A few days ago he
spoke to a lawyer about divorces, and
to some one else he mentioned his 1
family troubles. It was fortunate for
Prevatt that those who went to his
rescue did not know his fake game
sooner, had they, we believe they
would have resorted to a remedy that
taking laudanum would have been a
sweet dream to it. He would have
been placed across a barrel and sooth?
ed into a wakefulness that would
have for a long time driven away any
thought of drowsiness. Mr. Prevatt
had better not try such a game any
more if he appreciates the comfort of
a sitting posture when fatigued.

ton warehouse proposition. All

ton dealers and farmers were
represented. Hon. Richard L
Manning was elected chairman.
A full discussion was held as
to the desirability and advisa?
bility of building standard
warehouses in Sumter. I
A feeling of general ana wide?
spread interest in the enterprise
was manifested. And the opin-
ion is universal that great ad?
vantage to Sumter will be the j
result of the establishment of
warehouses here.
There are certain questions
arising in connection with the
matter which the meeting con?
sidered wise to determine be?
fore attempting a permanent
organization. Committees were
appointed to take up these pre?
liminary questions', obtan ali
possible information and report
at a subsequent meeting to be
called by the chairman.
Should the reports of the com?
mittees be of a favorable nature
a permanent organization will
likely be made at an early date.

IO BUILD TO CH?RLESTOM.
Sumter Men Consult Wi4h Wan?
ning People on Project Gf Con?
structing a New Railway
Line.
Manning, June 9.-A meeting
of the business men of Manning
was held in the Court House yes?
terday afternoon to consider the
matter of joining in a movement
to build an independent railroad
from Charleston by way of Man?
ning and Sumter northward to
conect with the Seaboard Air
Line Railroad.
On motion of Major Louis Ap
pelt, Mr. J. H. Rigby was called j
to the chair, and Major Marion j
Moise, of Sumter, was called up-
on to address the meeting. Ma- i
j or Moise's interest in the pro-
jected railroad from Sumter
northward is well known, and it
was his presence in Manning
that suggested the meeting yes?
terday afternoon. Major Moise
responded to the ''all, saying
that Sumter realized the need of i
railroad competition and had
determined to build a line north?
ward to conect with the Sea- j
board, with the view of either
selling or leasing to that road, j
He said it was agreed between
the Southern, the Coast Line
and the Seaboard not to build I
new lines into each other's ter- j
ritory, but there was nothing in i
their agreement to prevent the
people in this section from build-
ing a road and then leasing it to j
the Seaboard. He said that Sum?
ter had already secured the
right of way for projected road, j
and since that has been done
and their determination to build
the road had been evinced, the>
have been approached by capi?
talists with assurance of co-op?
eration if they will extend their
road on an air line to tide-water
at Charleston. This he threw
out as a suggestion, withput de?
finite promise, that an opportun?
ity might soon be offered Man?
ning to secure another line of
road by investing a little aio?cy.
There can be no doubt that there
is a possibility of great material
development in this section, bul
even now the amount of busi?
ness done offers no little induce?
ment for competition in railroad
traffic.
Major Moise's suggestion was
favorably received, and on mo?
tion a committee was appointed
to look into the matter and be in
touch with the projectors shouid
the movement take tangible
shape. It would not entail a very
heavy expense per mile to build
a railroad from Sumter to Char?
leston, on a practically air line,
and Manning would not be slow
in offering substantial encour?
agement to such a road.

BETTER TRAIN SERVICE.
Chamber of Commerce Asks for New
Schedule on Summerton Railroad.
.The Secretary of the Chamber o?
Commerce has taken np with Presi?
dent Thos. Wilson of the Northwestern
j Railway, the complaint of patrons of
the road as to inconvenient schedules
between Summerton and Somter, de?
lays in running of traius, extra fares
and", other troubles which deter the
patrons of that road in coming to
Sumter to trade.
Secretary Reardon has requested
that the morning train from Summer?
ton be run through direct to Sumter
as a passenger train on a fast schedule
arriving at an early hour, so as to
connect with other morning trains at
Sumter, and that a train be run out
of j Sumter in the evening on a quick
schedule, returning to Summerton as
a passenger and mail train, so that
patrons of the road may spend most
of the day ia Sumter to do their trad?
ing and retorn in the afternoon. He
suggests that the Northwestern Bail
way run its train for freight purposes
during the hours of the day between
the morning and afternoon passenger
trains.
It appears from information at hand
that this can be done without any
extra expense for extra trains, as com?
pared with the present schedule mak?
ing connection at Wilson's Mill on the
Central railroad, instead of the train
coming direct from Summerton on its
own tracks of the Northwestern rail?
way.

HIGH LICENSE SALOONS.
A Citizen Favors High License and Aboli?
tion of Dispensary.
Mr. Editor: The time is fast ap?
proaching when the people will take
some action on the liquor question,
?in my humble opinion the dispen?
saries must go. The only and true
solution of the vexed liquor question
is "High Incense.'* For many rea?
sons I deem it to be the best. While
I it-is- true that the city and county re?
ceives some revenue from the sale of
the accursed stuff, under the locali
option, and high license system about
an equal amount of revenue would be
derived, therefrom. Let the City
Council Sx the license at (say fifteen
hundred dollars per year. To cpen at
6 a. in.? and doss promptly at 7 p. m.
The proprietor to give a good bond to
j guarantee a strict compliance with
?-the law -and keep an orderly house.
No gambling, or games of any kind to
be allowed on the premises. Under
this"system a few well -appointed
saloons could afford to' keep a geod
quality of liquor. Sell nc cheaper stuff
than ten cents a drink, and as much
higher as customers may be willing to
"pay for the fun. Under this order of
things the usual crowd of one X dis?
pensary poer unfortunates would to
great extend be saved from idleness
and ruin. When they find that they
can't, afford to indulge in the fire
water they ean afford to do without
s?y, and invest their spare change in
bread. G. W. R.
Opposed to Saloons.
Mr. Editor: We do not know the
author 'of/*Bigh License Saloons,"
but one thing we do know, is that G.
W. R. has; struck a dead note, one
that has been defunct for more than a
dozen years. The boys of today will
be adorned with snow white locks
from o?d age, before the people of
South Carolina will ever again submit
to open bar rooms. The dispensary is
bad enough,%ut God save us from the
old condition of things. S.
