Tribute of Respect,
At a meeting of the directors, of the
Bank of Sumter held on Wednesday,
Marchv 29th, <h? following preamble
and resolutions were adopted:
On the 16th. day of March, 1905,
fwith but little warning of the im?
pending blow, sorrow fell upon this
community- when it was known that
Major W. F. B. Haynsworth was dead.
This sudden blow brought a sense
of personal loss to our citizens, and
especially to those who had been
so closely associated with him as
members bf; the board of directors of
the Bank of Sumter.
He was the, president of the bank
from its organization in 1888, and his
connection with the bank was an as?
surance to the public that its affairs
were conservatively and honestly
managed, for his name was the
synonym of integrity and uprightness.
We desire to inscribe this testimo?
nial of our love and esteem of the no?
ble character of our departed friend.
Be it therefore resolved:
That In the neath of Major Hayns?
worth the bank has lost a painstaking,
careful 4 officer, the board of directors
a valued member and each member
of the board a sincere and faithful
friend,
Resolved, That a blank page in our
record book be inscribed to his mem?
ory ariB these resolutions be spread
upon the minutes, published in the
city newspapers and a copy sent to
the family of the deceased.
Richard I. Manning,
Marion Moise,
R? Lu Cooper,
Committee of Board of Directors.

IT IS ENGLAND'S TRIUMPH
I
The Price of Peace Was Fixed Before
the War Commenced, So Says Mr.
Warner, the Dramatic Author.
To the Editor The Daily Item:
The report in the air of* peace in six
weeks and the absurd notions that
some people have that Russia will be
allowed to dictate her o'wn terms is
inconsistent with the premonitory of
the war.
Russia will only gain peace by con?
senting to forever vacate Manchuria
and give up all possessions now held
by that power, furthermore it is also
planned that Russia shall now (and
for all time) be so situated by her
border line that she will be powerless
to ever again threaten the integrity
of China, Korea or Japan.
This was the plan agreed to by
England when she gave her moral
support by accepting Japan as a part?
ner offensive and defensive in all
Asiatic affairs.
The open door to the world for Chi?
na, Manchuria and Korea is the pol?
icy of England, America and Japan. A
Japanese victory is what this means.
Russia is already hopelessly defeat?
ed, and she has once again, without a
shot fired by her real opponent, been
made to eat humble pie to England's
diplomacy. It will be many years be?
fore Russia will be able to recover
from the blow administered by Japan
and she will never again be a rival of
England as dictator.
With the fall of the Russian expan?
sion plan the rise of the British is
positive. She already dictates to more
than one-half of the world, at whose
command the sword is drawn or shat?
tered.

The Cheapest Piece of Property Now
on the Market ls Court House
Square for $60,000.
Some people, staggered by the big
sound of $60,000 have become wed?
ded to the idea that a very big price
is being paid for the coust house
house square. If they will make the

A,QUESTION OF COSTS.
Poll Tax Delinquent Raises a Question
in Magistrate Harby's Court.
Magistrate Harby has been some?
what puzzled by a question raised by
a poll tax delinquent who is resisting
the payment of the tax with the costs
added under an execution issued by
County Treasurer Lee and placed in
his hands for collection. It seems
that the law^ does not fix the amount
ofvjcosts to be taxed up by the magis?
trate, but in this county it1, has been
the custom for the magistrate to tax
up and collect as costs ojie dollar for
himself and the same amqunt for the
constable. Until this year the "treas?
urer's cost has been fixed by law at
fifty cents, but at the session of 1905
the legislature passed an act fixing the
treasurer's cost at one dollar.
Magistrate Harby has followed the
custom and on the tax executions
placed in his hands for collection he
has taxed up one dollar for himself
and one dollar for the constable in ad?
dition to the treasurer's fee.
One of the delinquents refuses, to
settle and questions Magistrate Har
by's authority to collect the amount
of costs taxed up against him, al?
though the right of the magistrate to
collect costs is unquestioned and un?
disputed. Another fact that cannot
be disputed is that the magistrates
h?ve the authority. to sentence poll
tax delinquents to the chain gang and
Magistrate Harby will solve the prob?
lem by sending those who refuse to
pay the tax, penalty and costs to join
the good roads brigade.

revere the memory of the heroes of
the Lost Cause and nothing prevents
on each occurring Tenth Day of May
the observance of the simple, yet sol?
emn ceremnoies commemorative of
the heroism and patriotic death of
those who wore the grey and laid
down their lives on the altar of their
country in the defense of State's
rights.
A more unpropitious season for the
observance of Memorial Day than
yesterday afternoon afforded could
not have been selected from the gar?
nered sheaves of Mays from a
cycle of years, but notwithstanding
the lowering clouds, the downpour of
ram, the flash of lightning and th<?
reverberating roar of thunder at in?
tervals throughout the afternoon, t?ie
Daughters of che Confederacy, th?:
Veterans and the Sumter vLight In
fantry, the Gralee* School Cadets, the
Second Regiment Band, the little givU
white clad, and bearing the choic?
est flowers of May in their hands, and
quite a large crowd of others assem?
bled at the cemetery at the appointed
hour and the programme as arrangeJ
was carried out.
Hon. Richard I Manning, of the
Sons of Veterans, presided and intro?
duced the speaker, Major H. F. Wil?
son. The invocation was offered by
Rev. C. C. Brown.
Just as Major Wilson began his ad?
dress a perfect down-pour of rain
came and he was unable to finish. The
introduction ?to his address, however,
which he delivered, was eloquent and
appropriate to time and'occasion.
Sumter Parties Interested.
The secretary of state has chartered
F. X. Wilson Insurance Agency of
Manning. Messrs. Perry Moses and
I. H. Moses, of this city, are among
the incorporators.
There is a lot of talk about the
blue blooded poultry that some of
our chicken fanciers are raising, but
what would interest common folks
would be a farm to raise a few mil?
lion plebian frying size chickens that
are not too blue blooded to grace a
dinner table.

the county would do well to sell the
jail and adjacent lots for a sufficient
sum to build a new jail in a more un?
desirable locality. It is argued that if
the jail were removed the property in
the vicinity of the jail lot would im?
mediately be built up in residences.

REPEAT THE CONCERT
Ladies ot the Baptist Church Asked to
Repeat Their Successful Enter?
tainment.
There is a demand for a repetition
of the concert given at Opera House
recently by the ladies of the First
Baptist church. A large number of
people were deprived of the pleasure
of seeing this splendid performance
owing to the inclemency of the weath?
er. Those present, however, have
spoken sc highly of the performance,
particularly the baby show, that they
have created a demand for another
performance. The manner in which
the sixteen little girls executed the in?
teresting and complicated manouevers
of the drill, and the unique way in
which they were dressed the elocution?
ary ability displayed for children of
age, and their acting in general were
the subjects of very favorable com?
ment. Mrs. B. M. Lebby, of this city,
who trained the children did her work
well. Mrs. Lebby recently took a
course in elocution at St. Joseph's
Academy of Sumter, and was awarded
a diploma, which was accompanied
with a complimentary recommenda?
tion from her teachers. Her recita?
tion, "Music on the Rappahannock,"
at the concert last week was the work
bf an artist, and she held the atten?
tion of the audience throughout her
splendid recital, and displayed unus?
ual ability as an elocutionist. She is
now engaged in teaching elocution in
.this city and she deserves a liberal
support. Those who are fortunate
enough to secure her as a teacher will
not have cause to regret it.
TRUCK FARMING.
Big Profits Can Be Made on Very
Small Investment.
Much has been said recently as to
the reduction of the cotton acreage
and raising peavine hay, etc., and for
the benefit of those who cannot con?
vince themselves that there is money
in anything other than cotton the fol?
lowing is taken from the Elberton.
(Ga.) Star, showing what one gen?
tleman did on less than three-quar?
ters of an acre of land:
"People who are talking hard
times, that there is no money to be
made on the farm, would dc: well to
follow the example of Dr. B. F.
Smith. This gentleman has a lot in
Elberton containing less than three
quarters of an acre-to be accurate
70-100 of an acre-on which he has
2leared already this year $185. This
profit came from the production of
cabbage. Last fall he planted this plat
of ground in this vegatable, which
had been laid by, and Friday he sold
the crop for $250 to J. G. Ginn.
Within the n^ext month this crop will
be harvested and sold, after which
Dr. Smith proposes to put the same
ground in celery, on which he hopes
to again make a big profit. The doc?
tor says that he owns and operates a
three-horse farm, and has pitted his
lot against the profits of his farm for
this year."

the wall, and the officers can use- it as
a locker. It is much more convenient

highly pleased with the new arrange?
ment.

THE COUNCIL MEETING.
Matters of General and Special In
terest Considered.
Council held a regular meeting at
S o'clock p. m., Wednesday.
Present-Mayor Geo. W. Dick, Al?
dermen H. D. Barnett, P. P. Finn, R.
F. Haynsworth, E. W. Hurst, C. G.
Howland, W. G. Stubbs and. R. K.
Wilder.
?Absent-Alderman H. W. Hood.
The minutes cf April ?2 were read
and confirmed. v
Mr. D. D. Moise presented a. petition
on behalf of Mr. T. B. Jenkins asking
that the city have a terra cotta drain
laid in the ditch across his property
and have the ditch filled up in order
that the adjacent lands may be ren?
dered more desirable for residential
purposes. The petition set forth that
the filthy water and sewerage in the
ditch amounts to a nuisance, danger?
ous to public health. The petition
was referred to the committee of pub
Be works to consider and report.
Mr. J. S. Lewis asked to be allowed
one third the cost of digging a ditch
ia the southern part of the city, but
no action was taken.
A letter was read from Messrs. Lee
Moise, attorneys, proposing some
modification of city's contract with
the Sumter Electric Light Company,
-whereby an all night and every night
schedule might be installed; same was
inferred to the committee of public
works as requested by the attorneys
to consider the report.
-Mr. Stubbs brought up the petition
^-of merchants presented in January
.-asking that dealers in second hand
Slothing be required to pay a license
of $500 per annum. Same was refer?
red to the committee on police and
sanitation.
The committee of public works was
t?qtiested to examine condition of the
"bell'tower to be sure of its safety for
t?ie new fire alarm system.
Mr. Barnett for police committee
Sled report of lights not burning. He
recommended purchase of pistols for
the police force.
Chief of Police Bradford stated
that the pistols of the force had been
in use for several years and are not
as reliable as they should be.
The request was referred to the po?
lice committee with power to act.
The finance committee reported
they had disposed of claims referred
to them and had checked up the
derk-and treasurer's report for March
and found it correct. The clerk's an?
nual report for the year ending March
31, is very gratifying to your commit?
tee.
Total habilites of .the city April 21,
1904, were $66,728.86; the same day
1905, $51,975.00, showing a decrease
in the public debt for the year $4,
753.86. This decrease has come about
not as the result of stopping any pub?
lic improvements but from a marked
increase "of income of various kinds,
-for instance,- our license tax shows an
increase, approximately of $1,100;
Teal estate tax, $250; personal prop?
erty tax, $600; street tax, $250; fine.?
and penalties, $500; dispensary prof
its, $1,200.
We disbursed on streets and ditches
In 1905, ?400 more than 1904; scaven?
ger carts, $60 less; electric lights,$300
more; opera house repairs, $350
?more; fire department, $335 less; in?
cidental expenses, $275 more; special
police, $205 more; board of health,
$260 more; interest and discounts,
$250 more; public printing, $100 less;
Officers* salaries, $30 O more. Upon, the
whole the outlook is encouraging.
C. G. Rowland, Chairman
The committee returned bill for re?
pairs of health officer's bicycle, $6.15,
"SOT action of council; it was ordered
paid.
The committee of public works re?
ported the work of claying Washing?
ton street progressing nicely. They
-were requested to prepare, with the
assistance of the clerk, an ordinance
ia regard to trash boxes on the
streets and for the protection of the
tire alarm system.
Reports were received from man?
agers of election this day held on the
question of issuing bonds to purchase
the Sumter water system and for
taree commissioners of public works.
Number of ballots cast 205.
Of these 190 were in favor of the
purchase and bond^ issue and 26 in
opposition thereto.
For commission of public
-works 205 ballots .were cast, of which
H. L. Scarborough and R. L. Ed?
munds received 205 each and W. B.
"Burns received 204.
The clerk was directed to prepare
an ordinance declaring the result of
the election as required by law.
The mayor suggested that an expert
fee employed to examine the1 physical
condition of the water plant, in defer?
ence to the wishes of the citizens of
tao city.
On motion of Mr. Hurst the com?
mittee on waterworks, consisting of
?the mayor, Aldermen Rowland and
Hainsworth, was continued, and they
were authorized to take such steps
as might seem wise and proper.
The following claims were referred
to the finance committee:
Sumter Telephone Co., $40; M.
H. Lazarus Co., $30.
Council then adjourned.

GRAND JURY PRESENTMENTS.
Find all Books of Hie Varions Officers
in Good Order and They Rec?
ommend Sale of Court House
Square for $60,000.
To His Honor, Judge J. C. Kiugh. Eic:
We, your grand jury, beg to make
this, our presentment:
We have examined, passed upon
and returned ail bills given to us. We
through our commitcee, visited the
county offices, including the offices of
treasurer, auditor, judge of probate,
clerk of court, superintendent of edu?
cation and sheriff, which ive have ex?
amined in a cursory manner only ow?
ing to the limited time at our disposal,
but will go into a more minute exam?
ination during the recess of th2 court.
We find the above offices kept in a
j proper manner as far as we could see
in the time allotted. We note, how?
ever, that the clerk of court is. very
j much crowded for room to store his
records, and we recommend that
some suitable place be provided. We
note the improvement to the grounds
around the court house. We recom?
mend that the clerk of court be re?
quested to keep all bill boards, pos?
ters, and such like from any part of
.the court house premises. We also
recommend that the clerk of court be
requested to have the police telephone
station near the court house steps and
the stand under the piazza removed
or-made to present a more sightly ap?
pearance by the erection of a new or
neater stand.
The jail we find in fairly good con?
dition except as to sleeping apart?
ments; they should be improved and
kept neater. The sanitary condition
is good, but we note that the waste
pipe from the second floor down is
insufficient and ift need of repairs. We
find the county alms house kept in
excellent condition, the paupers them?
selves say that they have plenty and
are well provided for. Whe find the
inmates to be sixteen whites and nine
negroes. We notice that there are
some children in the alms house who
should be placed in homes of relia?
ble people if such homes can be se?
cured for them. The chain gang
was visited and we found the prison?
ers at*work and everything was kept
well and conducted in excellent man?
ner.
We recommend that the magistrate
be supplied with necessary stationery
in their official capacity.
We have had the report of the
county supervisor and the board of
county commisioners for the fiscal
year 1904.
Upon information given us as to
the manner of of some of the mag?
istrates in conducting cases, we rec?
ommend that Magistrate Jno F.
Ingram be instructed upon the law
as to the amount and character of
evidence necessary to send a prisoner
up to the higher court and the neces?
sity of .obtaining and keeping all the
evidence in each case.
We understand that there has been
an offer of $60,000 for the purchase
of tue court house property; we there?
fore recommend that such a sale be
made for not less than that amount,
provided that upon the investigation
of the matter by the proper officers
such a sale be advisable.
All of which is respectfully sub?
mitted. M. R. Randie,
Foreman.

Waterworks for Bishopville.
Bishopville, April 26.-An election
was held here today on the question
of bonding the town for the purpose
of establishing waterworks. There was
a solid vote in favor of the proposi?
tion and the town council will take
immediate steps to put in the system.
Messrs. W. K. Crosswell, G. O. Rogers
and M. R. McCutchen were elected
commissioners of the waterworks.

A GRAFTER PINCHED.
A Short Change Artist Captured in
Florence and Made to Settle.
From the Daily Item April 19.
There were quite a lot of grafters of
one sort and another with the Boer
War show and they plied their arts
on the crowds while in this city Mon?
day with considerable sac-cess, and got
away with the goods. One of the
fraternity came to grief, howevar, but
it was necessary to pursue him to
Florence with a warrant to force him
to make restitution.
This short change artits who said
his name was E. S. Crosby of St.
Iiouis operated at the ticket wagon.
The man he undertook to Aim flam was
Mr. L. F. Kennedy. Mr. Kennedy
went to the wagon to purchase a ticket
and handed him a five dollar bill.
Crosby by dextrious slight of hand
substituted a one dollar bill for the
five and handed it to Mr. Kennedy
saying be could not make the change.
Mr. Kennedy protested vigorously
against the fake, hut could get no
.satisfaction, as he had ladies and
children with him and there wera no
police at hand be could not force the
grafter to return his money. fie,
however, took a good look at the man
so be would be able to identify bim
and procured a warrant for his arrest
as soon as could find an officer, hop?
ing to be *\bl? to have bim arresed be?
fore the show crowd left town. In this
he failed, not being able to find his
man. Be did not give up, but follow?
ed the show to Florence yesterday,
where be bad no difficult? in spotting
the short change artist and arresting
him. The grafter was completly non?
plussed and admitted his guilt, but
begged o3 on the ground that it was
a mistake made in the rosh and hurry,
that some one else had given him a
one dollar hill a bon* the same time,
etc He made good the money he had
appropriated and offered to pay any
amount to satisfy Mr. Kennedy rather
than face a trial. Mr. Kennedy made
him pay the expense of the trip- and
let him off. The grafter escaped
easily" all things considered for he
will recoupe his loss on this desi
many times over in a few days by
working his short change game on the
pubic
A CHARMIXG AFFAIR.
The Minor Stores Score Another Suc?
cess With Their Millinery Opening.
From the Daily Item April 19.
Spring and the Easter season were
typefied hythe Minor Stores yesterday.
The show windows were an invitation
from spring herself to enter the portals
and view the garnered beauties of
nature and art provided for the adorn?
ment of Sumter's fair daughters.
These windows had but to be seen to
win the admiration of the lovers of
tt?'e beautiful and artistic. Nature
and art were so skillfully blended that
it was difficult tc. distinguish where
nature ended and art began. The
decorations throughout the stores
carried out the scheme of Spring-like
beauty and in the millinery depart?
ment the display of imparted pattern
hats and trimmings was the climax of
attractiveness and the center of ad?
miration. The stores were thronged
with ladies all day and! in the after?
noon their was a constant stream of
visitors coming and going. There was
a pretty souvenir for each visitor and
Schumachers' Orchestra was present
aud gave a charming concert pro?
gramme of popular and classic music
The opemng was a gratifying success
to the management of the Minor Stores
and a charming entertainment to the
many who attended it.

COUNTY BOARD MEETIN G.
County Commissioners Meet in Regu?
lar Session
The county board of Commissioners
met Thursday morning and remained in
session until 1.45 o'clock when recess
was taken until 3 o'clock.
When the board was called to order
Mr. J. b I. Brogdon presented his
commission as a county commissioner,
which bore the date of April 6th. He
took the prescribed oath of office and
was duly installed as a member of the
board. r _
Today being the time for the elec- I
tion of a clerk for the board, the in?
cumbent, Col. Thomas V. Walsh was
unanimously reelected.
The bond of George P. McKagen, as
dispenser at Mavesville' in the sum of
$3,000, with J. E. Atkins and H. T.
Edens as sureties was filed and approv?
ed.
The County Treasurer's report was
received as information.
A resolution complimentary to the
retiring Commissioner, Mr. J. T. Mc?
Neill was adopted by a unanimous
vote. A number of claims were audi?
ted, approved and ordered paid.
At the afteronon session several im?
portant matters will be brought up for
consideration.
Supervisor Seale stated that he had
bids as follows for the iron fencing
on the court house square: Curbing
$48.95, or at 55 cents per foot; S9 feet
iron fencing at 62 cents per foot,
$55.IS; 4 posts at $2.75, $11.00, and 4
ornaments at end, $3.00, total $69.IS,
in all $118.13. On motion the super?
visor was instructed to proceed with
the work.
Mr. R. B. Belser was before the
board in regard to the proposition to
sell the court house square and the
matter was discussed. It was stated
that the sum of $60,000 was offered
for the square with the buildings and
that the old A. A. Solomons property
on North Main street could be had
for $25,000, leaving $35,000 with
which to build a modern court house.
The board adjourned to meet at the
call of the county supervisor.
W. A. BOWMAN, Prest. C. W. BOSH?MER, Sec. Treas

SUDDEN DEATH.
John Mack Dies of Hcait Disease
Yesterday.
From the Daily Item May 3.
John Mack, a negro v;ho lived on
Mr. Joel E. Brunson's place on the
Mayesville road about five miles from
this city, was found dead in front
of his stable yesterday afternoon.
There was no reason to suspect that
he had been killed, but Coroner Flow?
ers was notified and he went out to
hold an investigation. Dr. S. C. Baker
examined the body and made the fol?
lowing report:
"I have examined the dead body of
John Mack, found dead in the door
of his stable. There are no marks
of violence upon the body. I have
been his physician for a number of
years. He has been suffering from
organic heart disease for five or six
years and from symptoms described
to me as accompanying his death I
feel sure that he died as a result of
heart disease.
"S. C. Eaker, M. D."

Bad, Luck.
Henry Slater, colored, has fallen on
evil fdays and cross-eyed luck has
played him a scurvy trick. Henry is
a negro of uncrossed lineage, and, as
such, deemed himself privileged to di?
vorce the wife of his bosom and take
unto himself another and younger
ebon^-hued damsel without a trip to
Georgt? or consideration of the com
mahdment.He did so and that was
where he fell down. The wife of his
I bosom remembered that there was
such a thing as law and she appealed
to Judge Hurst for satisfaction, the
term being to her synonymous wi tn
revenge, and she put the law m
Henry.
The bare idea of taking a negro in?
to court for deserting one woman for
another has an element of the bizarre
or outre and the actual occurrence
with names and dates specified and
set down in due form and circum?
stance is absolutely stunning.
Fate reserved for Henry the dis?
tinction of being the exception that
proves the rule, and he must go to
the big court for doing no more and
no worse than thousands of: others of
his race have done with impunity.
That.-, is where his luck went wrong.
If all of his race in Sumter county who
are guilty of the same offense he is
charged with were bound over for
trial, the Court of General Sessions
would have to sit continuously for the
next six months to clear the docket.

THE HEALTH OFFICER IS BUSY.
Streets and Sidewalks Being Cleared
of Boxes, Barrels, Signs, Billboards
and Other Obstructions.
From the Daily Item May 4.
Acting under instructions from the
police and sanitary committee of city
council to rigidly enforce the ordi?
nance against obstructing the streets
and sidewalks, and enforcing the scav?
enger ordinance, the police force and
health officer started out yesterday to
carry out orders. The health officer
rode around the city and wherever he
found a box too large to conform to
the scave. ,'er ordinance, or any gar?
bage or rubbish from yards not prop?
erly boxed or barreled, he notified the
people to get the boxes off the streets,
and to properly box or barrel the
same. The city cart drivers began
putting in the boxes and barrels after
emptying them, and the consequence
is that the sidewalks and streets are
being cleared of bose? and barrels
and other obstructions and present a
much neat?r appearance. A regular
system of registration is now kept of
ali parties who are being notified not
to violate the law. The name of the
party violating the law, the nature of
the violation, the number and street
and the date of notice all recorded.
After this week all violations will be
followed by prosecution before the re?
corder. Not a person objected to
complying with the law, in fact most
of them expressed themselves as be?
ing heartily in favor of the rules, say?
ing.that they were only-too glad to do
anything to help improve the general
appearance of the city. The citizens
of Sumter can always be depended
upon to do their share in carrying out
any orders of the city council which
are for the good of Sumter. Our ci?
tizens always back up the officers of
the law. Put out your trash, gar?
bage .and rubbish before 10 o'clock in
the morning and put it out every day, j
except Sunday. Do not let it accumu- j
iate. The oftener you put it out the j
less you have to put out each time. J
Your yards t hen look better, and it is
healthier not to have accumulated
garbage and other matter around the
yard. Be certain to put out all tin
cans, and all useless receptacles which
hold water and breed mosquitos. Look
OUt for water on premises in which
mosquitos can breed. Look out for
the evening papers and read them
carefully so you can look out for the
police in.the "good old summer time.".
Don't wait to borrow your neigh?
bor's paper, for while you are wait?
ing you might miss seeing something
which you should know in time to
get ahead of the police and the health
officer. Most of the sinners before,
the Recorder's court are people who
never subscribe to The Daily Item.

NEW SCAVENGER REGULATIONS.
A More Rigid Enforcement of the
Rules Decided Upon.
Aldermen H. D. Barnett, W. G.
Stubbs, of the police and sanitary
committee, Health Officer Reardon,
Superintendent of Streets White, Chief
of Police Bradford and Recorder
Hurst met yesterday afternoon to ar?
range for a rigid enforcement of the
scavenger ordinance and to stop the
habit of people leaving boxes and bar?
rels on the sidewalks and streets.
The ordinance now m force requires
all trash, garbage and rubbish from
yards to be put out before 10 o'clock
a. m., and to be put in a box or barrel
of such size as can be conveniently
j piccked up and emptied by the cart
drivers. But a great many people put
out large boxes, barrels and other
large receptacles, too large to be
handled by a driver and many pile
out a wagon load at one time. Here?
after the cart drivers will only move
such trash and other matter as is so
boxed that they can pick up the box
and empty it. The drivers must re?
fuse to move any trash or rubbish
from yards not boxed as required by
law, and will report every day at 12
o'clock, noon and at 6 o'ciock in the
evening to Superintendent of Streets
jail trash not properly boxed, and the
I superintendent of streets will report
to the health officer the names, who
will then request City Recorder Hurst
to issue warrants for those persons
who permit violations of the law at j
their homes or places of business. All j
cart drivers will be required after
emptying each box to put the empty j
box off the street just, inside the yard
from whence it came. Any person
placing the box bacii on the street I
empty, or putting oui: a box after 10 I
o'clock a. m., will te arrested. No
person at any house or store or place
of business can place empty boxes or
barrels on the streets or sidewalk. It j
is also unlawful to place on the side?
walk or street any thing of any na?
ture whatsoever, except when loading i
or unloading goods sold or freight,
etc., and that must not be permitted
to remain any longer than is neces?
sary to remove the same.
The Police and Sanitary Committee j
notified the Chief of Police, Superin- j
tendent of Streets ar d Health Officer, j
that the police force and other officers j
vested with police powers will be held
responsible for the rigid enforcement j
of this law. Parties violating must be j
arrested. The streets and sidewalks j
must be kept clean and clear of un- I
sightly boxes and barrels and other
things. This rule will go into effect in
a few days.

HAD TO
CUT DOWN
Owing to business depression dur
ing this quarantine many of our ad
vertisers ordered out their ads this
week and as a result we are compelled
to reduce the size of the paper.
We would have been glad to con
tinue giving our readers an eight-
1 Page paper, but the withdrawal of so
many ads meant a reduction of the
paper's revenue. To meet the expense
and not having the Standard Oil
Trust at o.ir back we are compelled
to make the cut in the paper.
We have told all our advertisers,
contract or no contract, that if they
wanted their ads out during this cri
sis they had the privilege of so doing.
Many, as our readers will see by to
day's Times are still with us, business
or no business. For this we are duly
grateful and believe that in the end
their support of the paper will prove
r each a gain instead of a loss.

tinues good and as yet there have
been no suspicious cases of fever of
any kind to excite the people.
The city is perfectly quarntincd
from the outside world and we can't
see, if it is kept up, any reason for
fear of yelow fever here.
At present no one except from the
Northern States can get a permit to
enter the city at all, and these have to
have health certificates from the city
they hail from, and also proof that
thev have been living there for six
days perceeding their arrival.
Dr. Percy Toombs, the city health,
officer, is working early and late put
ting the city in a first-class sanitary
condition.Two detention cajnps have
been established, one north and the
other east of the city, but as yet only
two persons have been he'd awaiting
developments. Neither of these cases
were sick, but according to the rules
of the quarantine they were required
to remain there six days be.V' be
ing allowed to enter the city.
The committee of five meets three
times a day to hear petitions for per
mits, but the number who apply is
growing less every day.
Business continues at .1 standstill.
I but nothing better is expected until
frost or the fever has been wiped out,
which can hardly now be expected
with the hold it has in New Orleans.
Th action of the "City Council in
refusing to demand the resignation of
Marshal Hebron, at the suggestion
of the Democrat for a mistake he af
terwards acknowledged and apologia
ed for, shows that they have confi
dence in his ability to make the city a
marshall it has long demanded. Bar
ring this one act the refusal to accept
bond from Mr. Riteman when offered,
Marshall Hebron in the few days of
his service, has convinced the people
that he will be fearless in the dis
charge of his duty, and will see to it
that the laws of the city are enforced
and the law breakers brought to jus
tice. It is 6nly on determined offi
cers ike this that the safety of the
home depends. The Times feels
his future acts will be endorsed by our
entire citizenship.
fcNiE SsE Nsi
Governor James K. Vardaman, in
his stand in the Gulf Coast quaran
tine, is backed by the U. S. Govern
ment. Governor Blanchard, of Louis
iana, and hi political heelers will
have to make a different talk to make
"our Jim'' take back water in his po
sition towards them, and his strict en
forcement ofthe quarantine laws be
tween the States.
With every citizen needed to pro
tect the health and lives of our citi
zens this is no time for divisions or
high feeling toward one another. Har
mony and peace, and good will and
respect for the law should prevail
throughout our city, and it is every
man's duty to help bring it about.

amine guards would come nearer liv

NECROLOGICAL
Mrs. Fannie Johl
Mrs. Fannie Johl, aged 73 years,
died in this city last Monday morn
ing, alter a long illness. The deceas
ed was born in Germany, but had
lived in this country for 55 years,
living the greater part of this time in
Memphis. She leaves one son, Mr.
Abe Johl. of this city, and six daugh
ters, to mourn her loss.The remains
were carried to Memphic for burial.
The bereaved ones have th sympa
thy of the entire community
Mrs. Carrie Aiken
Mrs. Carrie Aiken, wife of W. B.
Aiken, of "this city, died on last Sun
day morning after a short- illness.
The funeral service? were conducted
from the residence on South Broad
way Monday morning and the re
mains were interred in the city ceme
tery. She leaves a loving husband
and four small cK dren to mourn her
death.
HOW GREENVILLE
GETS THE NEWS
The city now, each evening, re
ceives over the Postal telegraph lines,
the daily report on the fever situa
tion at New Orleans, as ofiicialy an
nounced at 0 o'clock by the health
officers of that city.
The interest ia these reports is ful
ly shown by the large crowds that
await each evening for the fever tid
ings, andthose who congregate on the
First, National Bank corner to read
these reports after they are posted
every morning.
These reports are paid for by popu
lar subscription and the collection for
this has provento Mr. Goldstein ai
easy. task. The reports are authen
tic as shown the next day by the daily
pres dispatches, and give the true sit
uation of the" fever twelve hours in
advance of the papers we receiy here."
The quarantine guards report wild
chickens in great numbers in the city.
The death of Archbishop Chapelle
is a sad blow to New Orleans and a
loss shared by 'the whole country.

Mrs. Julia Buchanan Crosby
Aged 69 years, died at the residence
of her son, II. T. Crosby, last Sun
day night after a lingering illnes of
eight months. While not unexpect
ed, her death was none the less a sad
blow to her sons and grandchildren
who gave her every attentive care
and attention that loving hearts and
hands could do to relieve her suffer
ing. She leaves three sons, Messrs.
II. T. Crosby, E. Piatt Crosby, of
this city, and Frank G. Crosby, of
St. Lo.iis by her death. The funeral
services were conducted by Rev. W.
A. Borum from the residence of her
son, Monday afternoon, and the re
mains werelaid to rest in the city
cemetery. The editor takes this
means of thanking the many friends
for their kindly offices during her
sickness and demise.
Edith May Waldauer
Edith May, youngest daughter of
Mr. and Mrs. Louis Waldauer. died
Tuesday morning at the home of her
parents after a brief illness of con
gestion. Edith May was just four
years and twenty-days old. and was
one of the brightest and happiest. of
the little children that attended
school, and in her home she was ever
a beam of sunshine. Her unexpected
death has brought sorrow among her
many litle friends, and her parents
have the deepest sympathy of the peo
pie in their great loss.

purpoes of liquidating existing debts
of the Town of Hollandale, in the
State of Mississippi, the Mayor and
Board of Aldermen of said Town will,
at their next regular meeting, to be
held on the first Tuesday in Septem
ber, 1905, make provision for the is
suance' of the bonds of said Town, to
the amount of Seven Thousand Dol
lars ($7,000.00), each bond to be for
the sum of Five Hundred Dollars
($500.00), payable twenty (20) years
from the date of issuance, and to bear
interest, from the date of issuance, at
the rate" of six (6) per cent, per an
num, payable semi-annually; that
said Seven Thousand Dollars ($7,
000.00), of bonds, proposed to be is
sued, will be in addition to the Twen
ty Thousand Dollars (20,000.00), of
bonds of .said Town now outstand
ing. and when added thereto, will ex
ceed seven (7) per cent, of the as
sessed 'value of the taxable property
of said Town, but he. less than ten
(10)- percentum thereof.
The above notice is published by
order of the Mayor and Board of Al
dermen, passed at their regular meet
ing on the first Tuesday in August,
1005. F. IT. RUSSELL,

Recipe*.
Tomato Catsup—Wipe dry one
i bushel and a half of ripe tomatoes,
and put them in a preserving kettle
j with one quart of vinegar, two
jounces of whole cloves, one ounce
of whole allspice, three table
spoonfuls of mustard seed, one tea
spoonfiii of grated nutmeg and five
cloves of garlic. 801 l this all to
gether for two hours, strain and boil
again until quite thick. Dottle and
set aside. Care must be taken that
the color remains red.
Sweet Pickled Peaches —A syrup
of four pounds of brown sugar, one
quart of beet cider vinegar, timl two i
Ounces of stick cinnamon broken in
places. Roil all together twenty
minutes. Have peaches ready, hay- I
ing previously dipped them into!
boiling water and rubbed off fur with
towel. Stick two or three cloves!
into each peach (not* more, ior the!
clove darkens the peach.) Put half
t»p peaches in the syrup at a time;
and cook until clear and tender.
Drain out. and put into jars and pour
the boiling syrup over them, filling i
the jar. Seal.
Quite the simplest way to wash
lace is to make a soap lather by
shredding a quarter of a pound of
soap into a quart of water. Allow
this to simmer over a slow flre until
dissolved. Put a little of this into
a clean, wide-necked bottle. Put in
the lace also after thoroughly shak
ing the dust out of it. Then cork
the bottle and shake it well. As
often as the lather becomes dirty
pour it off and add fresh, shaking as
before. When it is clean, rinse first
in warm water and then in cold.
Have ready a thick piece of flannel
the size of the lace. Lay it on a
clean table, pull the lace gently into
shape, picking out all the little
points and fastening them each with
a pin. Above all, never Iron or blue
fine lace. Ironing makes it shiny,
bluing is criminal, as the creamy tint
of the lace is Its most admired
quality.

AN IMPROMPTU CONCERT.
Sirs. Ledbetter Charms an Audience
at Atlantic Beach HoteL
What was by far the most delightful
iiocial affair of tbe season was tbe
gathering ia tbe parlors of the Atlan?
tic Beach Hotel on Monday evening,
which developed into a concert, vaude?
ville and general jollification. Tbe
affair was entirely impromptu. Tbe
evening was delightfully pleasant as to
weather, and as the hotel is the socal
center, everybody^gafhered^ there as
bj instinct. There were perhaps .150
guests from outside cottages assembled
in addition to the regular guests of the
hotel. No one really anticipated the
treat in store and the program which
followed was more pleasant in conse?
quence.
Mr. C. B.' Garrett took charge of j
the exercises, as he usually takes j
charge of every th icg else. Mr. Garret
STganize a chorus led by himself and
the audience seemed remarkably, ap-
peciative.
The climax of the occasion was yet i
fco come, as the company, reaized, j
whep Mrs. Harry Owsley, of Elberton, j
Ga. Mrs. Scott Ledbetter's mother, j
began singing. Mrs. Owsley is gifted j
with a voice, sweet. ,tiue, powerful,
and which Mrs. Owsiey makes more
effective by ber wonderful powers of j
^expression. Sbe-sang several solos, all
of which were roundly applauded and
encored.
One who was greatly responsible for
the pleasure cf the evening was Mrs.
Robinson, of Atlanta, wi o played the
?accompaniments for the musical nun-- .j
hers Mrs. Robinson is a pianist of i
great ability, and when called upon to!
accompany, a singer she displays that
almost rare sympathy of touch that is
so great an aid to the vocal soloist.
Of a different type of voice, but'just
ac- well appreciated was the singing of
Mrs. Henry Busch, Jr., of Aiken, 3 j
C., who rendered several solos and|
received a full share of applause?
A gifted young virtuoso is Master
Philip Greene, who bails from Atlanta, I
and is stopping at the Atlantic Beach j
Hotel with his mother, Mrs. J. A. j
Greene, and his sister. Miss Laurie
Green. Master Philip is but ll years
of age, but be plays a violin with the
skill and grace of a much older musi?
cian. His rendition of the famous
music of classic au popular typcs was
skillful and indicative of the future
master of bis beloved instrument.
The youthful musician was aided in
his efforts by the accompaniments
played by bis sister, Miss Laurie
JGreene.
The spice of variety was added to
'the evening's program when Mrs. Led?
better, of Sumter, S. C.. recited se ve?
?a! darky character sketches. Mrs.
Xedbetw has well trained elocution?
ary powers, and on this evemog she
was "at her best." Her comic selec^
iions were received and in responding
tc encores the speaker always surpris
cd and delighted ber audience with
^Ettne selection that seemed more
jannsing than the ene preceding.
The company dispersed at 11.30, and
sow all are wishing for the spesdy
return of a like occasion.-Island Life.

CITY COUNCIL MEETING.
Sunday Ordinance Rescinded-Xo
More Cigars or Soda Water on the
J Sabbath.
The city council met in regular ses?
sion Wednesday afternoon at 6 o'clock.
Present-Mayor Geo. W. Dick; Al?
dermen H. D. Barnett, R. F. Hains?
worth, H. W. Hood, C. G. Rowland,
W. G. Stubbs and R. K Wilder.
Absent-P. P. Finn and E. W.
(Hurst.
Rev. Messrs. Kilgo.^Satterwhite, H.
B. Browne, Epting and Inabnit, pas?
tors o? the city and members of the
Ministerial Association of the city, ap?
peared before council and requested
t?at they rescind a resolution of coun?
cil Adopted April 27, 19OG, permitting
"the sale of drugs, cigars, tobacco, ice
and milk and the hiring of vehicles
on Sunday." They called attention
to the fact that this resolution was in
conflict with the law of the state on
the' Sunday question, and should for
that; reason be. rescinded. And for the
further ^reason that the law recog?
nizes the right of every one to rest
from his ordinary labors on the Sab

'bath without taking into' considera?
tion the morai, and religious, phase of
the matter. They realized that drugs,
ice and milk should be-allowed, hut
'thought that no other exceptions
should be made. They further called
attention to the flagrant violation of
fthe Sunday ordinance by the sale of
j fso?t drinks," and asked that the po
jlice be instructed to enforce the law
I rigidly. The matter was taken up in
regular order after and due discussion
the following resolution was unani?
mously adopted:
."Resolved, That the resolution- of
city council adopted April 27th, 1900,
in reference to the Sunday ordinance,
be rescinded, so. as to make the said
ordinance conform to the law of the
state; except so far as it applies to
drugs, milk, ice and 'vehicles.. And
*his law shall not be evaded by giving
away soda water, coca cola, milk
shakes or other soft drinks, ice cream,
tobacco, cigars, etc And dispensing
such articles by any means or in any
manner, or allowing them to be dis?
pensed, shall be deemed a violation of
the ordinance."
The police committee was requested
to instruct the police force to enforce
the ordinance strictly:
Minutes of June 28 and July 6 were
read and approved.
An ordinance to permit N. G. Os
teen, Jr., and associates to erect and
operate *gas works was read for the
first time.
On motion of Mr. Rowland it was
Resolved, That parties applying to
council for franchises be required to
pay the'sum of $25 to cover expenses
of preparation and publication of
ordinances."
The clerk was instructed to prepare
an ordinance carrying the resolution
into effect, should that be necessary.
Mr. Rowland, for the finance com?
mittee, reported approval of bills re?
ferred to them and recommended re?
newal of the lease to Mr. J. W. Mc
Keiver. Action on this recommenda?
tion was postponed, and the commit?
tee granted time to ascertain the date
from which the new lease shall run.
Mr. Haynsworth, for the committee
of public worKs, reported that the
city laborers are now laying drain
from the Walsh pond and trimming
trees. They could not report definitely
in regard to extension of streets in
Ward Xo. 1 and were granted further
tinfe.
Report of the special committee,
submitted June 2Sth, in reference to
lighting contract was taken up and
unanimously adopted in absence of
any further communication from the
company.
An ordinance extending the fire
limits was read and adopted and
ordered published under a resolution
dispensing with the second reading. A
resolution was .passed to the effect that
no permits will be granted to alt??r or
add to any wooden building within the
fire limits. v-je'
The clerk and treasurer's report
for June was referred to the finance
committee, and also the following
claims:

DuRant Hardware Co. 13.98
Burns Hardware Co.85
China's Drug Store. 1.35
Harby Co. 48.00

Kershaw.
The many friends here of Mr. J. B.
Witherspoon will learn with regret
that he has finally decided to leave
Kershaw September 1. Ile has been
a citizen here for nur-? than fourteen
j years and has been very closely iden
I tined with every change that has taken
place since coming herc. He will be
j much missed c- his departure will
j be a decided loss to the community.
Mr. Witherspoon prefers to make his
future home in Sumter, whare his
sons, Messrs. E. L.. M. B. and E. D.
Witherspoon, are all engaged in suc?
cessful business. His family will fol?
low him later.-Kershaw Era.

From the Daily Item July 13.
A gang o? negro boys broke into Mr.
W. S. Reynolds store between 8 and 9 j
o'clock last uigbt tb J on gb one of the j
front windows and -stole four pocket
knives and ten razors.-; When tbe rob?
bery was discovered suspicion feil upon
Prince George and he was arrested this
morning. He gave- away several of
bis confederates and warrants were in
sued for their arrest. A searrch
warrant was also issued and the police
were busy for several hours hunting
the boys and trying to recover the
knives and razors. Weyman Allison,
one cf the the boys implicated by
Prince George, but there is no positive
proof against him. Only two of the

EXTENDING FIRE LOUTS.
Committee o? Public Works Will Rec?
ommend Extension on Main- Street
and Stricter Regulations.
At the next meeting of the city
council Chairman R. F. Haynsworth
of the Committee of Public Works,
?will recommend that the fire district
of the city be extended from their
present limits on Main street to Bart?
lett street south and Calhoun street
on the north. No change will be
made on the eastern and western
boundaries for the present. The re?
port will also contain a recommenda?
tion that the erection of iron clad
frame buildings within the fire district
be forbidden in future and that only
brick or stone buildings be permitted
hereafter within the first district.
This action of Mr. Haynsworth was
influenced by a case that came before
bim yesterday. The police stopped
?work on an iron clad frame building
which is being erected within the fire
district and the matter was referred
to the committee of Public Works.
After an investigation the committee
decided that inasmuch as similar
buildings had been erected within the
fire limits from time to time for a
number of years it would not be fair
to stop work on this building.
If these more rigid regulations to
be recommended are adopted, as they
probably will be, the erection of fire?
traps in the business portion of the
city will be prohibited.
The proposed regulations are both
wise and timely and will result in the
-erection of a better class of buildings
and diminish the danger from fire.
HIS WIFE'S MONEY.
Israel Moses Made the Police Think
He Was a 3Iillionaire.
From the, Daily Item June 2S.
It is a dangerous thing for a poor
man to spend money lavishly, and Is?
rael Moses, colored, discovered yester?
day that it don't pay. Israel is just
an ordinary negro and has never had
enough money to blow the town,
therefore when he started out on a
spending expedition and displayed a
roll he fell under suspcion. He was
gathered in by the- police and asked to
explain, Chief Bradford haying an
idea that Israel had found a purse
containing a considerable sum of
money which Mr. Ed Jenkins lost a
few days ago. Israel explained sever?
al times, but neither of the explan
tions was satisfactory. As a result of
the investigation it was ascertained
that Israel had confiscated and ap?
propriated to his own use about $25
belonging.to his wife without her i
knowledge or consent. When the
fact became known to Recorder Hurst
be took charge of the money found on'
Israel's person and Veturned it to
Israel's wife who rejoiced with an
exceeding great joy that the lost had
been .found. Israel had spent but a
lew dollars, having been pulled before
be had gotten well started on his
spree.
CONSUL AT ODESSA
Reports Distressing State of Affairs.
Five Hundred Rioters Killed.
Washington, June 30.-Ambassador
Meyer cables the state department
under date of June 30 to the effect
that he has received a dispatch from
Consul Heenan at Odessa stating th?t
SOO rioters have been killed and that
the harbor front had been destroyed
by fire.
The fleet of Kruger had not arrived
np to 9 p. m. on the 29th. Heenan
also reports six officers of the Potem?
kin killed and liberated; the remain?
der are prisoners.
P. C. Dowdle, a passenger on a
Southern train, had his skull fractur?
ed by being struck by a rock thrown
through the car window near Rock
Hill last Tuesday.

Crews, a cotton mill man from Gaff?
ney, who was about to drown in the
lake at Chicks Springs Sunday after?
noon. They swam out into'the pond
and rescued him as he was in a death j
struggle with the water.

A DOG INQUEST.
Coroner Flowers anti Deputy Sheriff
Gaillard Make a Long Trip.
From the Daily Item June 29.
Yesterday morning the negroes on
County Supervisor Seale's place creat?
ed a sensation and caused Coroner
Flowers and Deputy Sheriff Gaillard
to take a long, useless and fatigueing
trip to Gaillard's X Roads.
When the negroes were going to
work they passed a house that had
been occupied until a few days ago by
a negro woman. In passing they de?
tected a very offensive odor which
appeared to come from the house.
They peeped through a crack and saw
a body lying on the floor, and with?
out stopping to make a closer inves?
tigation ran to Mr. W. J. Seale and
told him there was a dead baby in
the house. He notified Magistrate L.
R. Williamson, who advised him to
call up Coroner Flowers and Deputy
Sheriff Gaillard and tell them to ar?
rest the woman who had been living
in the house, she having moved to
this city and then to come up and hold
an inquest The woman could not be
found, so without losing time -n
searching for her Coroner Flowers
and Mr. Gaillard procured a buggy
and set out for Gaillard's X Roads to
hold the inquest. When they arrived
they proceeded to the house and
found that the corpse was not a baby,
but a small dog. Xo inquest was held.
The question now is, who will pay the
expenses ?>f J.e trip?

The musical and recital of Profes?
sors Schumacher and Parker was
quite a success and enjoyed by a num?
ber of Sumter's music lovers. The
programme was select but short, in
fact, too short for some and the Miss?
es Tiecher deserve much praise for
they did credit to both Professor
Schumacher afad themselves.
At the close of the programme Mr.
Moise rendered a piano solo which
was greatly enjoyed.
Professor Schumacher again show?
ed his generous heart by giving to his
many friends one of his angelical
sotos.
Another Convict Escapes.
Jim Edwards, who' was doing time
on the chaingang for robbing the
store of Col. W. D. Scarborough at
Dalzell, made his escape -Wednesday
morning before daylight. Edwards,
who was a trusty, had been quite sick, i
or feigned to be, and was not locked
up in the cage with the other prison?
ers. Taking advantage of the oppor?
tunity he slipped away in the darkness
and his flight was not discovered until
daylight.

THE FOURTH 4? DARLINGTON.
A RIG CROWD ENJOYED *j|lNE
PROGRA3S3IE.
3he Streets Were Thronged With Peo?
ple and Everybody Enjoyed the
Day-The Programme.
Staff Correspondent.
Darlington, July 4.-The glorious
fourth was celebrated here today ac?
cording to Darlington style, which is
but another way of saying that it was
.properly, enthusiastically and pa
triotically celebrated.
There was a great crowd present
and the streets were thronged with a
yi: happy holiday crowd that had gath?
ered by companies and reg?menes
f.-om all quarters of the Pee Dee
country, while many representatives
irom other sections of this state and
irom North Carolina mingled with the
-tarong. There was a great crowd, but
Darlington was not overcrowded, and
all the vistors were well taken care of
and all had a good time.
The Sumter contingent was large
and representative and with the Sec?
ond Regiment band to blow the horns
Har them the Sumter crowd was not
lost nor did they pass unnoticed in t! e
throng. The special train thai Vii
Seamier at 8 o'clock carried 213 Sum
*tpr people and enroute pic<t*d tp
many more Sumter and Lee county
folks and when it pulled into Darling-1
non it was full to the doors and over
-fiowing on the platforms.
The Darlington committee cf ai
rangements haa provided on atf.rav;ve
and interesting programme and there
??pas something doing all day to keep
?the crowds moving and entertained.
The Darlington Hotel was the bus
test place in town and Mr. A. F. Dufft,
the popular proprietor, proved him?
self to be a man of resources and the
right man in the right place. He
served 400 dinners and was prepared
to take care of all comers.
The celebration was a great success
and none enjoyed Darlington's hospi?
tality and entertainment. more than
the Sumter crowd.
The programme which follows gives
an idea of the scope of the affair:
First, herds cattle, J. N. Kirven, $20
Second, single cows, L. L. Tread
?way, $5.
Third, double team horses, first, R.
R. Dargan and W. W. Cooley, $10;
second, L. S. Welling, $5.
Fourth, double team mules, Town
.of Darlington, first, $10; W. G. King,
second, $5.
?Fifth, single harness mules, Town
of Darlington, first, $5; T. S. Farmer,
second, $3.
Siixth, single harness horses, R. K.
Dargan, first, $10 W. M. King, driven
by Langston, Jr., second, $5.
Seventh, single harness, pony, driven
by girl under 12 years old, pony to be
nuder 14 hands high, Hilda Hening,
first, $3; Dr. J. S. Garner, M. D., sec?
ond, $1.
Eighth, single harness horse, driven
by lady, Mrs. P. A. Wilcox, Florence/
first, $10; Miss Kate Kirven, second,
*S.
Ninth, pony to be ridden by boy un?
der 12 years old, pony to be under 14
hands high,. Julian Hening, $2.
Tenth, saddle horse, ridden by man,
D. L. Outlaw, horse ridden by A. E.
Dardan, chief cf police of Darlington,
first, $3; Conder Brothers, Bishop
ville, second, $1.
Eleventh, single harness horse, coun?
ty raised, W. M. King, driven by J. T.
langston. Jr., first, $3; L. K. Kirven,
second, $2.
Twelfth, saddle horse, ridden by
lady, Mrs. P. A. Willcox, first, Mrs. S.
C. McCullough, second, $2.
Thirteenth, decorated double turn?
outs, N. L. Harrell, vehicle by Misses
Lou Mciver, I<ena Lawson, Pauline
kelley. Effie Rogers, S10.
Fourteenth, decorated single turn
jonts, Miss Lois Wilson, $10.
The Hon. M. F. Ansell delivered the
address of the day. A large audience
gathered in the opera house and ac?
corded the orator complimentary at?
tention. In view of his candidacy
some people expected a political
stump speech from Mr. Ansell, but
they were disappointed. He made not
the slightest reference to anything
bearing upon the political situation,
but on the other hand talked to his
audience in a manner exactly suitable
to the occasion.
Cheraw and Fort Caswell played a
game of baseball this afternoon to a
park full of people. Cheraw was vic?
torious by a score of 7 to 1.
The citizens of the town of Darling?
ton subscribed nearly $1,000 for the
proper celebration of the day and the
unanimous verdict of all say this
amount was well expended.
Professor Schumacher was in Union
some weeks ago with the famous
Second Regiment Band, of Sumter,
the pride of not only Smuter, but
the entire State, and has been in?
vited by the music loving people of
that city to give a musical recital.
Every trip Professor Schumacher
makes is a big advertisement for Sum?
ter, as he is known as the leader of
one of the best bands in the south,
and the home of the band is Sumter,
Sonth Carolina.
On a Corner in Darlington
BY ONE WHO STOOD THERE
"No wonder those fellows play
well. They are working for the Unii
State government and don't do ai
thing else but play music. But w
do they call it the Second Regim?
Band of Sumter, is there a militi
post established in Sumter? How d(
Sumter manage to keep up such
fine band and orchestra, at such
expense without the aid of the Unit
States government, or state gover
ment. Either the United States
the State of South Carolina must p
those musicians, particularly that pi
fessional band director who leads t
band?" remarked the man who enjo
ed the music.
'Excuse me for butting in, gentl
men,' but I am from Sumter," said
man on the right, "and hearing y<
wonder whether the United Stat
government pays the members of ti
Second Regiment band, or if ti
State of South Carolina contribut
anything financially to its support,
just want to say that this band whi<
has contributed so much to the plea
ure of the Darlington celebratio
judging from the hundreds of peop
whom I have heard expressing then
selves today, is a Sumter organizatic
maintained exclusively by Sumter pei
pie and Sumter money, except sue
funds" as are gathered in by this bar
for playing outside of Sumter on ju
such occasions as this. As far i
the national or state government
concerned the Second Regiment ban
of Sumter has 'been treated very ba*
Take for instance the item of band ir
struments. It is a well known fa<
that the United States governmer
had a complete set of instrumem
made for the Sumter band and anotr.
er military band shortly after thes
two bands enlisted over sixtee
months ago. But the Sumter instru
ments disappeared, while the othe
band received their instrument
over a year ago. No one in authorit
in South Carolina has, to all appear
anees, any authority, and perhaps n
inclination to see about the reaso]
why the Second Regiment band o
Sumter has been deprived ol" their in
struments, or the money appropriatei
by the national government. Bu
things are coming our way, however
I hear it pretty generally rumore*
that Col. Henry T. Thompson, of Dar
lington, the best known, and mos
popular and expert military man ii
South Carolina, will be the next adju
tant and inspector general of thi:
state, and he will get the Sumter bane
what is coming to it, and Sumter wil
be recognized in military circles. Col?
onel Thompson is already working tc
find out what is wrong about those in?
struments. He will get there alright.'
Said a gentleman from Florence:
"Sumter knowes how to advertise.
She never loses an opportunity to ad?
vertise. And the Sumter band is the
very best advertisement Sumter has.
It is remarkable that a town of Sum?
ter's size should keep up a band o?
this class, when Charleston, Columbia,
Greenville, Spartanburg and other
larger cities have no bands to compare
with it, or no band at all. That is thc
secret of the support given the Sumter
band. I have heard hundreds of peo?
ple say he're today that this band was
the best they have ever heard, and
were surprised to learn that it is a
Sumter organization instead of a reg?
ularly paid .government band."
About this time three very attrac?
tive ladies came down the staircase
and passed through the office of the
hotel.
"Who is that beautiful, queenly
looking lady who has just passed
here?" asked the gentleman from
Florence.
Said the gentleman from Sumter:
"She is in truth a queen, she is Sum?
ter's reigning sovereign. She was the
queen of the Sumter Fall Festival, and
a beautiful and worthy queen she
made.
"Oh, well," s ?id the gentleman
from Florence, "I am not surprised
at all. Sumter certainly elected a
queen in all that the word implies.
But seriously speaking I have been at?
tracted by the number of pretty wo?
men and girls over here today from
Sumter, and when you begin to inquire,
who, is that lady and who is this lady,
and un every hand you are told that
a majority of the ladies you ask about
are from Sumter, and when you hear
the Sumter band play, and see the
Sumter automobiles whirl by full of
Sumter beauties, you can't help but
think that Sumter has gotten more
advertising out of Darlington's Fourth
of July celebration than Darlington
really has. Also Emmett Reardon on
the ground with his hot air furnace
in good working order boosting Sum?
ter for all that he's worth."
"Well, said a gentl.-man from the
Emerald Isle. "Sure and that was the
intention, of the Sumter crowd."
"If the Sumter band and orchestra
is ever allowed to disband for lack of
financial assistance, the Chamber of
Commerce, the city council, and the
business men of Sumter will realize
that they have made the biggest mis?
take of their lives," says The Man
Who Was Standing on The Corner.

Darlington Aftermath.
Some of the young ladies who went
to Darlington say that Chief of Police
Bradford was a splendid advertise?
ment for Sumter over there on the
Fourth of July. Some of them even
intimated that he was the handsom?
est man in Darlington, and that those
new shoulder straps attracted the at?
tention of quite a number of people.
Now, which was it, the shoulder
straps or the man? Both, no doubt.
Any how he did some mighty big talk?
ing for Sumter.
Officer W. W. McKagen could
scarcely keep from collecting street
tax in Darlington. He saw so many
Sumter people over there, that he kept
thinking that he was in Sumter most
of the time. Health Officer Reardon
saw about three cart loads of water
melon rinds in the street opposite the
depot and arrested the Daily item re?
porter and two other Georgians be?
fore Chief Bradford and Officer Mc
Kagen could stop him. The chief is
authority for the statement that the
Sumter officers drank nothing strong?
er than red lemonade, but he says
that they drank a sprinkling cart full
of that. Outside of the acid in tilt',
lemonade (.w^ich no doubt was the j
principal ingredient) it was not. strong
enough to hurt a two-day-old baby.
Chairman R. F. Haynsworth, of the j
Public Works Committee fared well. I
and was booming Sumter for all he
was worth, and explaining how we
ran our fall festival, and how we build
macadamized streets, and telling what J
kind of a sewerage system wc are]
getting ready to build, but he explain
ed that he would not allow Superin- j
tendent of Streets White to enter any
of the scavenger cart mules in the
i
"horse show" because blue ribbons
did not match their complexion, nor
match the color of the city carts
which are painted green in Sumter, i
Xow, that should not have been said.
Watch Darlington get back and say
that green is emblematical of the dis- 1
position of Sumter people. Well, any j
how, we may not paint everything j
green, but we do manufacture here in j
Sumter a green colored telephone
which carried, or rather brought back.
the gold medals and first prizes from j
the Paris Exposition, and several oth- j
er similar affairs.

cantile business should work in har?
mony with the manufacturer, each

and there would be no reason for any

SUDDEN DEATH FROM DRINKING
A Young White Man Drinks a Half
Pint of Whiskey and Dies.
A whiteman about 28 years of age
named Charles Christmas died very
suddenly at 1408 Wayne street be?
tween 5 and 6 o'clock yesterday after?
noon. Death is supposed to have
been due to alcohol poisoning, as the
man was a hard drinker and was seen
to drink a half pint of whiskey just
before entering the home where he
lived and where his death occurred.
He walked into the house and in a
few minutes fell in a fit, and before
medical aid could reach him he was
dead.
Coroner Walker was notified and
after viewing the remains had the
body removed to the undertaking es?
tablishment of J. M. VanMetre. The
coroner will investigate the case more
fully today, and if necessary will em?
panel a jury and present the case to
them.-The State.

ADVERTISING RATEB FURNIBHED ON APPLICATION.
FRIDAY, MARCH 3, 1905.
The inauguration of our early Presi
dents used to cost about |5, but they were
not conducted on the twentieth century
style of grandeur. Byway of contrast i
it may be mentioned that the one on i
Saturday will cost more than $500,000. 1
Theodore Roosevelt, who will be inau
gurated as President of the United States
on Saturday, March 4, is the first acci
dental President in the history of our j
country to succeed himself. After serv
ing three years and a-half of President
McKinley’s term, he will tomorrow take 1
the oath of office to which he has been j
elected for the next four years.
According to the statistics of the Weath
er Bureau, of the twenty-seven inaagura-
tions which have occurred since the
bureau was established, ten have been
stormy, while seventeen have been de-
clared as fair,” but not all of these had
good weather. The majority were ex-
tremely cold, and even on the fair days
rain or snow fell during a portion of the
twenty-four hours.
The Commoner thinks the democrats
will be the gainer no matter what the
republican Administration does, and
reasons thusly If the President accom
plishes anything the corporations will
try to secure a corporation man to suc
ceed him, and that will give life to the
next campaign and hope to the demo
cratic party. If the President fails to ac
complish anything the democratic party
will profit by his educational work.
The dollar registration fee in Delaware,
without which no citizen can become
qualified to vote at each general election,
is to be abolished. The Senate passed
last week the proposed amendment to the 1
constitution, removing the requirement.
This means many a dollar saved for
the Addicks corruption fund in Dela
ware, which will be a big help to the
boodlers in the Diamond State at this
time, as ’tis said funds are running low.
The new banking law that is being
quietly pushed through Congress will
greatly decrease the volume of legal ten
der money, made by the government out
of something, and enormously increase
that of hocus pocus made by bankers out
of nothing. Bankers are now collecting
from 1 per cent a year to 1 per cent a day
as interest on over 6,000,000,000 of this
stuff, and its use is the sole cause of com
mercial panics and of most periods of
disastrous business depression.
The Commissioners of Cecil county on
Saturday filed an application with the
Highway Division of the Maryland Geo
logical Survey for State aid in the con
struction of five miles of road in our
county, in accordance with the provisions
of the Shoemaker law. This tardy action
of our Board of Commissioners was only
taken at the last minute after repeated
urgiug by citizens of the county, most
notably a few members of the Sixth Dis
trict Road League, who, through their
persistence, were able to arouse our slow
moving and dilatory Board to action on
the eve of the expiration of the time
limit—March 1.
Just what objection the Board had to
placing the county in a position whereby
it would be able to secure 18,255 69,
Cecil’s apportionment, for road improve
ment by the expenditure of an equal
amount, is bard to determine, as it would
be difficult to make an investment of
county funds whereby the returns will be
as great —a dollar for every dollar ap
propriated for the betterment of our roads.
The old method of squandering money
on the roads in temporary tinkering aud
patching has become such a firmly fixed
principle in road improvement that
it seems difficult to displace it with one
whereby some lasting benefit will be
obtained. With the start already made
in this District by the construction of a
mile of macadamized road that has stood
the test in every respect, the wisdom of
expending money in scientific road build-!
iug has been shown, and, with the ad
dition of the State aid fund, it is hoped j
we are witnessing the dawn of a new era 1
in road construction in Cecil, and that
the work of permanent improvement will
l>e pushed as rapidly as the meagre funds
at the disposal of the Commissioners will
permit, each year adding its quota, until!
eventually our roads are brought to the
standard of those in States which years<
ago relegated such slip-shod methods as
have long been followed in Cecil, to the
past, where they belong, and have put in
practice scientific principles of road build
ing, which, while the first cost is great,
briog returns a hundred fold and are, in
the end. the cheapest.
A bill has been introduced in the Illi
nois senate providing for the appointmeut
by the Governor of a committee to con
stitute an internal improvement commis
sion. One of the duties cf this commis
sion is to investigate the practicability of
a ship canal to connect Lake Michigan
with the Mississippi river, and thereby
establish a great waterway to the Gulf of
Mexico, from Chicago. The move for
the canal is the result of the determina
tion by the Government to complete the
Panama canal, which is sure to divert a
great commerce from lines leading to
Atlantic ports to lines terminating on the
Gulf.
In discussing subsidies the Philadel
phia Record puts the matter forcibly in
the following short paragraph 'Because
the carriers of other nations are willing
to do our carrying for us at rates so low
that our own carriers are unable to com
pete we should therefore pay subsidies to
our own carriers in order that they may
be able to render us a more costly service.
This is the boiled-down argument of the
subsidists. It does not appeal to the hard
horse sense of the nation. We have al
ready carried to the limit the game of
paying favored persons substantial bon
uses in order to enable them to engage in
otherwise unprofitable industries.
In a communication to be found else
where in this issue "Citizen” calls atten
tion to the dog nuisance, which, it was
hoped, the law recently passed by the
General Assembly would do much toward
remedying, and which would prove an
effective meaus of ridding our county of
hundreds of worse than useless curs, if
euforced by the Constables of the various
Districts, in whose hands the responsi
bility of collecting the tax or killing the
dog is placed. Numerous canines of all
sizes, colors, conditions and ages can be
seen ob our streets at almost any hour of
the day, and heard at night, the half of
which we venture have never had a
penny of tax paid for them, and which
are of no earthly use except to fatten
fleas and bark.
Increase Of Lawlessness.
McClure’s Magazine attributes the in
crease of lawlessness in our country to
two leading causes, the increase of wealth
of a part of the people, and the combina
tions of industries for the purpose of
crushing out competition, actuated solely
by selfish greed on the part of those whom
fortunate circumstances have enabled to
gain the ascendency, and who use the
power to further their selfish interests
in the maiutenace of a mean and unfair
advantage over their fellow men. Of
this class McClure’s says:
"We find under various modifications
this criminal oligarchy in control of
many communities iu the United States.
We find representatives of this combina
tion in the United States Senate, among
governors of states, state legislators,
mayors, aldermen, police officials. We
find them among men in busiuess life
captains of industry, bankers, street
railway magnates. In short, wherever
franchises or contracts of any kind are to
be secured from a community, we find
leading citizens iu the ring to rob their
own neighbors, managers of corporations
bribing law makers, lawyers for pay help
ing their clients to bribe safely, jurors
refusing to render just verdicts.
"These men—bribers of voters, voters
who are bribed, bribers of aldermen and
legislators who are bribed, men who
secure control of law making bodies and
have laws passed which enable them to
steal from their neighbors, men who have
laws non-enforced and break laws regu
lating saloons, gambling houses, and, in
short, all men who pervert and befoul
the sources of law—these men we have
called enemies of the Republic. They
are worse —they are enemies of the
human race. They are destroyers of a
people. They constitute a class of crim
iuals very different from ordinary crim
inals who break laws; these men destroy
law.
j "Our governments—city, county state
—are inefficient and sometimes criminal.
1 Under inefficient government the strong
individual oppresses the weak. Take,
I tor example, the extraordinary story of i
the Staudard Oil company, with its years I
of successfully crushing competition, that
practically deprived men of their property
and their business, with its control and
selfish use of railroads that were given
their franchise by the state for the good
and equal use of all. A despotic govern-1

WASHINGTON LETTER.
Seldom has a Congress of the United
States ended its days under more peculiar
or conflicting conditions. The country is
prosperous, peaceful and hopeful in the
midst of political and industrial condi
tions and outlook that is little short of
revolutionary. The indictment of the
beef trust involving prominent railroads
and the summoning of clouds of witnesses,
the investigation of the Standard oil
Monopoly. The railroad rate bill passed
almost unanimously by the House but
held up by Senators Elkins, Kean and
other representatives of the roads in the
Senate portend other far reaching and far
searching investigations of monopolies
until the country is on tip toe of hope and
expectation. What will the Summer’s
incubation bring forth? It is not doubt
ful that the country is behind Theodore
Roosevelt, more powerfully behind him
than it was on the fourth of November,
lor that splendid majority has been re
enforced by Bryan, and a powerful Demo
cratic contingent. Will the Senate con
tinue its obstructive or to be polite, its de
liberative tactics? It is believed in polit
ical circles here that there will be a called
session of the new Congress in September
or October and that the Senate will
not then staud in the way of what the
country so unanimously demands.

a
Congress is finishing up in hot haste.
One day this week a hundred and thirty
bills were passed in less than forty
minutes.


The Canal bill passed the Senate Thurs
day notwithstanding the newspaper
prophecy that it would not be taken up at
this session, but the recommendation
most desired by the President that the
Canal Commission be abolished was
stricken out. Secretary Taft has been
most outspoken in declaring that the
Commission has been an obstruction
rather than an aid in the administration
of canal matters and the House bill made
provision for the abolishment of the Com
mission hut the opposition in the Senate
led by Allison who is a close friend of Rear
Admiral Walker, the president of the
Commission, and Senator Gorman, result
ed in the elimination of the provision
The bill as it passed the Senate states that
the President shall have authority to ap
point a person or persons to govern the
Canal zone and that the President shall
be given further authority to direct such
control but it is not likely that the House
will accept this most important amend-
ment and the bill will go to conference.
Among the reasons that have made the
president so eager to have the Canal com
mission abolished was the lack of actual
work done under the commission. i
V
The House Commttee has threatened
that if this provision of the bill were
eliminated by the Senate it would lay be
fore the President a statement of the
facts developed at the recent bearings re
garding the acceptance of fees on the
part of the Commissioners for acting as
directors of the Panama railroad, and it is
probable that the Committee will proceed 1
to make-the report at once. The accept
ance of such fees was in direct violation,
the committee states, to the order of the
president.

Indications now are that a sea level
canal will be dug. Engineers have been
watching with great interest the work on
the Simplon tunnel, the longest sub- i
terranean road in the world and it is ar
gued that experience in this great excava
tion will greatly aid in tunneling the
Culebra mountain and letting the Cbagres
river out through the tunnel, thus elimi- 1
nating the flood problem which has been
the greatest, or in fact the only obstacle
in the way of a sea level canal. The Sim- I
plon tunnel is twelve miles long, the Cule
bra tunnel will only need be four miles
long and will probably not cost more than
the Bohio dam and locks would cost.

Representative Campbell who has
emerged from youthful obscurity to be
one of the most talked of men of the
day by his introduction of a resolution re
i commending the investigation and crimi
nal prosecution of the Standard Oil Com
pany insists that he 'has the evidence in
r J I
hand to prove all of the charges be has j
made against the company to Commis-
sioner of Corporations Garfield. The de
velopment of the situation in Kansas, 1
taken in conjunction with the popular
1 feeling aroused by the many newspaper j
1 and magazine articles of late on the sub-
ject, gave Mr. Campbell's act a peculiar
timeliness and the House indicated its
feeling by adopting the resolution regard
less of party lines. When the Govern- i
ment starts the investigation it will
measure strength with one of the most
powerful industrial and financial institu
tions iu the world, and the movement
may well be regarded as the most mo
mentous begun in this decade.
